rationales.correct: The patient is experiencing uterine tachysystole (hyperstimulation), depriving the fetus of oxygen (late decels). The absolute first action is to stop the offending medication.
rationales.incorrect: Positioning and oxygen (intrauterine resuscitation) must happen IMMEDIATELY AFTER stopping the oxytocin. If you don't stop the drug, the contractions will continue to suffocate the baby.

scenario: Two hours postpartum, the nurse palpates a boggy uterus displaced to the right side, 2 cm above the umbilicus. The patient has heavy lochia. What is the most appropriate first action?

options.B: Assist the patient to empty her bladder

hint: A mechanical obstruction is pushing the uterus out of place.

rationales.correct: A full bladder physically displaces the uterus (usually up and to the right), preventing it from clamping down and causing hemorrhage. Emptying the bladder fixes the root mechanical cause.
rationales.incorrect: Massage will not keep the uterus firm if the bladder is still full. Meds and calling the MD are secondary to emptying the bladder.

scenario: A patient is admitted in preterm labor at 29 weeks gestation. The provider orders betamethasone IM. What is the primary purpose of this medication?

options.B: To stimulate fetal lung maturation and surfactant production
options.C: To prevent maternal and fetal infection
options.D: To prevent neonatal neural tube defects

hint: Premature babies struggle to keep their alveoli open.

rationales.correct: Betamethasone is a corticosteroid given to the mother to stimulate the production of surfactant in the fetal lungs, drastically reducing the risk of respiratory distress syndrome in the premature infant.
rationales.incorrect: Tocolytics (like nifedipine or terbutaline) stop contractions. Antibiotics prevent infection. Folic acid prevents neural tube defects.

scenario: A mother who is Rh-negative delivers an infant who is Rh-positive. To prevent isoimmunization, the nurse must administer Rho(D) immune globulin (RhoGAM) within what time frame?

options.B: Within 72 hours postpartum
options.D: During the first trimester of her next pregnancy

hint: Stop the mother's immune system before it can finalize antibody production.

rationales.correct: RhoGAM must be administered to an Rh-negative mother within 72 hours of delivering an Rh-positive infant to prevent the formation of antibodies that would destroy future Rh-positive fetuses.
rationales.incorrect: While 24 hours is acceptable, the protocol limit is 72 hours. 6 weeks or the next pregnancy is far too late; irreversible sensitization will have already occurred.

scenario: A patient at 32 weeks gestation presents with painless, bright red vaginal bleeding. Which assessment is strictly contraindicated?

options.B: Digital vaginal examination
options.D: Initiation of a large-bore IV line

hint: The placenta may be covering the cervical opening.

rationales.correct: Painless, bright red bleeding is the hallmark of placenta previa. A digital vaginal exam can puncture the placenta, causing immediate, massive, lethal hemorrhage. Exams are strictly contraindicated.
rationales.incorrect: Ultrasound, fetal monitoring, and IV access are critical, safe interventions.

scenario: A laboring patient receives an epidural anesthetic. Ten minutes later, her blood pressure drops from 120/80 to 85/50. Which action should the nurse take first?

options.A: Turn the patient to the left lateral position and increase IV fluids
options.B: Administer IV ephedrine
options.D: Prepare for an emergency cesarean delivery

hint: Maximize circulating volume and venous return before giving pressors.

rationales.correct: Epidurals cause sympathetic blockade (vasodilation) leading to maternal hypotension. Turning the patient off the vena cava and giving a fluid bolus are the first-line resuscitative actions.
rationales.incorrect: Ephedrine is given if positioning/fluids fail. C-section is premature. Stopping the pump won't instantly reverse the medication already in the epidural space.

scenario: A newborn is delivered through thick meconium. The infant is apneic, floppy, and has a heart rate of 80 bpm. What is the immediate first step in resuscitation?

options.B: Initiate Positive Pressure Ventilation (PPV)

hint: Current NRP guidelines prioritize establishing air exchange over deep suctioning.

rationales.correct: Under current Neonatal Resuscitation Program (NRP) guidelines, if an infant born through meconium is not vigorous (apneic or HR < 100), the priority is immediate PPV to establish oxygenation.
rationales.incorrect: Routine intubation for deep suctioning is no longer the first step. Drying a severely compromised infant wastes time. Epinephrine is only given if HR remains < 60 after effective ventilation and compressions.

scenario: A patient with postpartum hemorrhage is prescribed methylergonovine (Methergine). Which maternal condition is an absolute contraindication for this medication?

options.B: Type 2 Diabetes

hint: This medication acts by causing intense systemic vasoconstriction.

rationales.correct: Methylergonovine is a potent vasoconstrictor. It is strictly contraindicated in patients with hypertension or preeclampsia because it can precipitate a lethal hypertensive crisis or stroke.
rationales.incorrect: Asthma is a contraindication for Carboprost (Hemabate), not Methergine. Diabetes and C-sections are not contraindications.

scenario: A pregnant patient in her third trimester complains of feeling dizzy and clammy when lying flat on her back. What is the physiological cause?

options.A: Compression of the inferior vena cava by the heavy uterus
options.B: Sudden drops in blood sugar due to fetal demands

hint: Supine hypotensive syndrome.

rationales.correct: When a pregnant woman lies supine, the heavy gravid uterus compresses the inferior vena cava, drastically reducing venous return to the heart and causing maternal hypotension. Turning to the left side resolves it.
rationales.incorrect: It is a mechanical positional issue, not related to blood sugar, breathing, or an embolus.

scenario: A patient with gestational diabetes delivers a 9 lb 10 oz baby. At 1 hour of life, the infant exhibits tremors, jitteriness, and a weak cry. Priority action?

options.A: Assess the infant's blood glucose level
options.B: Wrap the infant in warm blankets
options.D: Assess the infant for opioid withdrawal

hint: The maternal glucose supply is gone, but the baby's insulin is still high.

rationales.correct: Infants of diabetic mothers are at high risk for profound rebound hypoglycemia. Jitteriness and tremors are classic signs requiring an immediate heel stick to check blood sugar.
rationales.incorrect: Warming addresses cold stress but ignores the lethal glucose drop. Calcium treats hypocalcemia. Withdrawal is unlikely given the specific GDM history and macrosomia.

scenario: A patient at 12 weeks gestation presents with dark brown vaginal bleeding, severe nausea, and a fundal height measuring 16 weeks. Ultrasound shows a 'snowstorm' pattern. Diagnosis?

options.B: Hydatidiform mole (Molar pregnancy)

hint: Abnormal proliferation of placental tissue creating grape-like clusters.

rationales.correct: A molar pregnancy is a gestational trophoblastic disease causing rapid growth of cystic tissue (snowstorm ultrasound, larger-than-expected uterus) and extreme hCG levels (severe nausea).
rationales.incorrect: Previa isn't diagnosed at 12 weeks. Ectopic pregnancy presents with severe unilateral pain. A missed abortion shows a fetal pole with no heartbeat, not a snowstorm pattern.

scenario: A breastfeeding mother develops mastitis. Her breast is red, swollen, and painful, and she has a fever. What should the nurse teach her about feeding?

options.A: Stop breastfeeding immediately to prevent infecting the infant
options.B: Pump the affected breast and discard the milk
options.C: Continue breastfeeding frequently to completely empty the breast
options.D: Apply cold packs to the breast prior to feeding

hint: Milk stasis is the enemy of healing.

rationales.correct: Mastitis requires antibiotics and frequent, complete emptying of the breast. The milk is safe for the infant. Frequent feeding prevents abscess formation.
rationales.incorrect: Stopping feeding causes severe engorgement. Discarding milk is unnecessary. Warm packs (not cold) are used prior to feeding to dilate ducts and promote let-down.

scenario: A pregnant patient asks why she must avoid unpasteurized soft cheeses and deli meats. The nurse explains these foods carry a high risk of which pathogen?

options.B: Listeria monocytogenes

hint: This bacteria thrives in cold, processed environments.

rationales.correct: Listeriosis can cause miscarriage, stillbirth, or severe neonatal sepsis. Pregnant women must avoid unpasteurized dairy and cold deli meats (unless heated to steaming) to prevent infection.
rationales.incorrect: Toxoplasmosis is from cat feces/undercooked meat. Salmonella is from raw eggs/chicken. CMV is transmitted via bodily fluids.

scenario: During a vaginal examination, the nurse palpates a diamond-shaped fontanel toward the front of the maternal pelvis. What does this indicate?

options.A: The fetus is in an occiput anterior position
options.B: The fetus is in an occiput posterior position ('sunny-side up')
options.C: The fetus is in a breech presentation
options.D: The fetal head is engaged at station 0

hint: Diamond = Front of the baby's head.

rationales.correct: The diamond-shaped fontanel is the anterior fontanel (forehead). If it is felt at the front of the maternal pelvis, the baby's face is pointing forward (occiput posterior), which causes severe back labor.
rationales.incorrect: Occiput anterior presents the triangular posterior fontanel. Breech presents buttocks. Engagement refers to depth (station), not position.

scenario: A patient with severe preeclampsia is hospitalized. Which assessment finding is the most ominous sign of an impending eclamptic seizure?

options.B: 1+ pitting edema in the lower extremities

hint: Pain under the ribs indicates liver swelling/ischemia.

rationales.correct: Severe epigastric or right upper quadrant pain indicates liver capsule distention (often related to HELLP syndrome) and is a classic warning sign of an impending seizure or liver rupture.
rationales.incorrect: BP 150/95, 1+ edema, and trace protein are signs of preeclampsia, but they are relatively mild compared to the severe, acute organ damage indicated by epigastric pain.

scenario: A nurse is assessing a neonate's head and notes a unilateral swelling on the head that does NOT cross the suture lines. What is this finding?

options.B: Cephalohematoma

hint: Blood pooled under the periosteum is trapped by the bone's edges.

rationales.correct: A cephalohematoma is a collection of blood between the skull bone and its periosteum. Because it is confined by the periosteum, it cannot cross the suture lines of the skull.
rationales.incorrect: Caput succedaneum is superficial tissue edema that DOES cross suture lines. Subdural hemorrhage is an internal brain bleed. Molding is the overlapping of cranial bones.

scenario: A nurse receives report on 4 patients. Which patient should be seen FIRST?

options.A: A pt with asthma exacerbation who has a respiratory rate of 28 and audible wheezing
options.B: A pt 2 days post-op from a hip arthroplasty reporting 8/10 pain
options.C: A pt with a deep vein thrombosis receiving a heparin drip who reports leg cramping
options.D: A pt with diabetes whose fasting blood sugar is 175 mg/dL

hint: Airway and Breathing always take precedence over circulation or pain.

rationales.correct: The asthma patient is actively experiencing airway constriction and tachypnea, placing them at risk for respiratory failure. ABCs dictate this is the absolute priority.
rationales.incorrect: Post-op pain is a priority but not life-threatening. Leg cramping with a DVT is expected. A blood sugar of 175 is elevated but stable.

scenario: Which task is most appropriate to delegate to an Unlicensed Assistive Personnel (UAP)?

options.A: Provide the initial feeding to a stroke patient with new dysphagia
options.B: Empty a Foley catheter bag and record the output
options.C: Assess a patient's skin integrity during a bed bath
options.D: Educate a patient on how to use an incentive spirometer

hint: UAPs perform standard, non-invasive procedures requiring no nursing judgment.

rationales.correct: Emptying a catheter and recording volume is a routine, procedural task perfectly suited for a UAP.
rationales.incorrect: Initial dysphagia feeding poses an aspiration risk requiring RN assessment. Assessing skin and teaching are strictly RN scopes of practice.

scenario: A mass casualty incident has occurred. Which patient should the triage nurse tag as RED (Immediate)?

options.A: A conscious pt with a compound femur fracture screaming in pain
options.B: A pt with an open, sucking chest wound and severe dyspnea
options.C: An unresponsive pt with full-thickness burns over 85% of their body
options.D: A pt with a 3-inch scalp laceration wandering the scene in a daze

hint: Red means an immediate threat to life that can be fixed quickly.

rationales.correct: An open pneumothorax (sucking chest wound) is an immediate threat to ABCs but is highly treatable with an occlusive dressing. This patient is tagged Red.
rationales.incorrect: Femur fracture is Yellow (Urgent). 85% burn/unresponsive is Black (Expectant/Deceased) as resources cannot save them. Scalp laceration is Green (Minor).

scenario: A Med-Surg charge nurse is making assignments. An LPN/LVN is on the team. Which patient is the best assignment for the LPN?

options.A: A newly admitted pt with diabetic ketoacidosis on an insulin drip
options.B: A pt 3 days post-appendectomy requiring a sterile dressing change
options.C: A pt with acute pulmonary edema requiring frequent IV push furosemide
options.D: A pt being discharged today who requires extensive heart failure education

hint: LPNs care for stable patients with predictable outcomes and can perform sterile procedures.

rationales.correct: A stable post-op patient requiring a sterile dressing change is highly predictable and well within the LPN scope of practice.
rationales.incorrect: DKA/insulin drips and acute pulmonary edema require critical RN assessment and IV push meds. Initial/complex discharge teaching is an RN responsibility.

scenario: A nurse discovers a fire in the soiled utility room. What is the FIRST step according to the RACE protocol?

options.B: Close the door to the utility room to confine the fire
options.C: Remove any patients or staff in immediate danger
options.D: Attempt to extinguish the fire with a portable extinguisher

hint: Life safety always comes first.

rationales.correct: RACE stands for Rescue, Alarm, Confine, Extinguish. The absolute first step is pulling anyone in immediate danger away from the fire.
rationales.incorrect: Alarm is step 2. Confine is step 3. Extinguish is step 4.

scenario: A doctor writes an order for a medication dose that the nurse knows is dangerously high. The doctor refuses to change it. Next action?

options.A: Administer the medication to avoid insubordination
options.B: Refuse to give it and document the doctor's incompetence in the chart
options.C: Withhold the medication and escalate the issue to the nursing supervisor
options.D: Ask another nurse to administer the medication

hint: Use the chain of command to protect the patient.

rationales.correct: The nurse has an independent legal duty to protect the patient. If the MD refuses to change a lethal order, the nurse must withhold the drug and utilize the nursing chain of command.
rationales.incorrect: Giving it causes harm. Charting 'incompetence' is unprofessional. Passing the buck to another nurse is unethical and unsafe.

scenario: A patient signs a DNR order. Later, the patient stops breathing. The daughter screams at the nurse to 'Do everything and save him!' What is the nurse's ethical duty?

options.A: Begin CPR to prevent a lawsuit from the daughter
options.B: Honor the DNR order and provide comfort care only
options.C: Call the hospital ethics committee immediately while doing compressions
options.D: Ask the attending physician to overturn the DNR verbally

hint: A competent adult's legal directive supersedes family emotion.

rationales.correct: A DNR signed by a competent patient is a legally binding directive of their autonomy. Family members cannot override the patient's documented wishes. The nurse must honor the DNR.
rationales.incorrect: Initiating CPR is battery. The ethics committee isn't needed for a clear DNR. The MD cannot arbitrarily overturn it for family appeasement.

scenario: A nurse smells alcohol on a coworker's breath, and the coworker's speech is slurred. What is the priority action?

options.A: Tell the coworker to go sleep in the breakroom
options.B: Take over the coworker's patient assignment
options.C: Report the observation to the charge nurse or supervisor immediately
options.D: Wait for them to make a clinical error before reporting

hint: Patient safety is paramount and requires immediate removal via authority.

rationales.correct: An impaired nurse is a direct threat to patient safety. The observing nurse must report this immediately to management so the impaired nurse is removed from patient care duties safely.
rationales.incorrect: You cannot authorize them to sleep it off. Just taking their patients leaves them on the floor. Waiting for an error causes patient harm.

scenario: A psych nurse floats to the Med-Surg unit. Which patient is the most appropriate assignment for this float nurse?

options.A: A pt with a new-onset myocardial infarction on a nitroglycerin drip
options.B: A pt experiencing delirium tremens from alcohol withdrawal
options.C: A pt with a temporary transvenous pacemaker
options.D: A pt 4 hours post-op from a total gastrectomy

hint: Match the nurse's primary skill set with the patient's needs.

rationales.correct: A psychiatric nurse has extensive experience managing substance withdrawal, delirium, and behavioral interventions, making the alcohol withdrawal patient the safest assignment.
rationales.incorrect: Nitro drips, pacemakers, and fresh complex surgeries require specialized medical-surgical/cardiac assessment skills.

scenario: A patient falls out of bed but sustains no injuries. The nurse completes an incident report. How should this be documented in the medical record?

options.A: Document 'Patient fell out of bed. Incident report filed.'
options.B: Attach a copy of the incident report to the chart.
options.C: Document the objective clinical facts of the fall and the patient assessment.
options.D: Omit documentation of the fall since there was no injury.

hint: Incident reports are for internal risk management, not clinical records.

rationales.correct: The chart must reflect purely objective clinical facts (found on floor, vitals stable, MD notified).
rationales.incorrect: NEVER document that an incident report was filed, as this makes the internal document subpoenaable in a lawsuit. Failing to document the fall is falsification.

scenario: A patient is declared brain dead. The family is arriving soon. Who should request organ donation from the family?

options.B: The attending physician
options.C: A trained representative from the Organ Procurement Organization (OPO)

hint: Requests require specialized training to avoid coercion and conflict of interest.

rationales.correct: Federal guidelines require that requests for organ donation be made exclusively by trained OPO professionals to ensure ethical, sensitive communication.
rationales.incorrect: Nurses and doctors should not initiate the request to avoid the perception of a conflict of interest (e.g., 'they stopped treating him to harvest organs').

scenario: Four patients press their call lights simultaneously. Who must the nurse answer first?

options.A: A pt with a cast complaining of severe itching underneath
options.B: A pt with heart failure who needs help to the bedside commode
options.C: A pt with a chest tube who states, 'The tube just fell out of my chest'
options.D: A pt requesting their scheduled PRN pain medication

hint: Loss of a chest tube causes an open pneumothorax.

rationales.correct: A dislodged chest tube creates a sucking chest wound, leading to rapid lung collapse. This is an immediate life-threatening airway/breathing emergency requiring a sterile occlusive dressing instantly.
rationales.incorrect: Itching, toileting, and routine pain meds do not pose an immediate threat to life.

scenario: A nurse delegates colostomy care to a UAP. Which specific task is appropriate for the UAP?

options.A: Assess the stoma for color and adequate blood flow
options.B: Empty the ostomy pouch and record the output amount
options.C: Cut and apply a new ostomy wafer to fit the stoma
options.D: Teach the patient how to empty the pouch at home

hint: UAPs perform routine collection and recording.

rationales.correct: Emptying a pouch and recording the volume is a standard, non-sterile, non-invasive procedure safe for a UAP.
rationales.incorrect: Assessing the stoma, fitting a new wafer (requires assessment/sizing), and teaching are strictly RN responsibilities.

scenario: A patient is admitted with suspected bacterial meningitis. What is the very first action the nurse should take?

options.B: Obtain blood and CSF cultures
options.C: Place the patient in droplet isolation precautions

hint: Protect the staff and other patients from a highly contagious pathogen.

rationales.correct: Bacterial meningitis is highly contagious via respiratory droplets. The absolute first action is to place the patient in isolation to prevent transmission.
rationales.incorrect: Cultures must be drawn BEFORE antibiotics, but isolation happens before any direct patient contact. Neuro exams follow isolation.

scenario: A provider orders 'Potassium Chloride 20 mEq IV push STAT'. What is the nurse's action?

options.A: Administer the medication slowly over 5 minutes
options.B: Refuse the order and notify the provider immediately
options.C: Dilute it in 50 mL of normal saline and push it
options.D: Ask the pharmacist to verify the dose

hint: Potassium IV push is a lethal injection.

rationales.correct: Potassium chloride must NEVER be given IV push under any circumstances. It will cause instant, fatal cardiac arrest. The nurse must refuse the order; it must be infused via an IV pump.
rationales.incorrect: Pushing it, even slowly or diluted, is lethal. Pharmacist verification wastes time on an explicitly lethal order.

scenario: The RN asks the LPN to assist with a patient receiving a blood transfusion. Which task can the LPN legally perform?

options.A: Initiate the blood transfusion into the IV site
options.B: Act as the second licensed person to verify the patient ID and blood product at the bedside
options.C: Perform the critical assessment during the first 15 minutes of the infusion
options.D: Evaluate the patient's overall response after the transfusion is complete

hint: LPNs can verify data but cannot initiate or perform critical initial assessments for blood products.

rationales.correct: An LPN/LVN can legally serve as the second licensed person to double-check the blood product and patient ID alongside the RN prior to administration.
rationales.incorrect: Only an RN can physically start the blood. Only an RN can perform the vital first 15-minute assessment. Evaluation is an RN responsibility.

scenario: A patient wants to leave the hospital Against Medical Advice (AMA) while an IV is still in place. What is the nurse's priority action?

options.A: Call security to restrain the patient
options.B: Remove the IV catheter immediately before they can leave
options.C: Explain the medical risks of leaving without completing treatment
options.D: Refuse to provide the AMA form

hint: Ensure the patient is making an informed decision before they walk out.

rationales.correct: Before a patient leaves AMA, the nurse/provider must ensure they fully comprehend the medical risks of leaving. Only after this education (and asking them to sign the form) is the IV removed.
rationales.incorrect: Restraining them is false imprisonment. Removing the IV is done eventually, but education is first. You cannot refuse to let a competent adult leave.

scenario: The nurse receives a critical lab value: Platelets 12,000/mm3. The patient is scheduled for a liver biopsy in 30 minutes. Priority action?

options.B: Notify the provider immediately to cancel or delay the biopsy
options.C: Place a pressure dressing over the liver site in advance
options.D: Document the lab value and proceed with the prep

hint: Normal platelets are 150,000-400,000. Liver biopsies carry a massive bleeding risk.

rationales.correct: Platelets of 12,000 indicate severe thrombocytopenia. The liver is highly vascular; a biopsy at this level carries a massive risk of fatal internal hemorrhage. The provider must be notified to cancel the procedure.
rationales.incorrect: Sedating the patient proceeds with a dangerous procedure. Pressure dressings won't stop severe internal liver bleeding. Proceeding is negligent.

scenario: Which patient requires a negative pressure isolation room?

options.A: A patient with disseminated herpes zoster (shingles)
options.B: A patient with Clostridium difficile
options.C: A patient with a MRSA wound infection
options.D: A patient undergoing an allogeneic stem cell transplant

hint: Which pathogen travels on tiny dust particles that float through the air?

rationales.correct: Disseminated shingles requires airborne precautions (including a negative pressure room) because the virus particles are small enough to travel on air currents.
rationales.incorrect: C. diff and MRSA require contact precautions. Stem cell transplants require POSITIVE pressure rooms to keep hospital pathogens out.

scenario: A nurse delegates the task of obtaining a blood glucose reading to a UAP. The UAP reports a reading of 38 mg/dL. What is the nurse's immediate action?

options.A: Ask the UAP to recheck the blood sugar in 10 minutes
options.B: Go to the patient's room and assess the patient immediately
options.C: Document the reading in the chart
options.D: Ask the UAP to give the patient a glass of apple juice

hint: Never treat a critical value blindly without seeing the patient.

rationales.correct: A glucose of 38 is a critical, life-threatening value. The RN must immediately assess the patient's level of consciousness to determine if they can swallow safely or if they need IV Dextrose.
rationales.incorrect: Waiting 10 minutes risks brain damage. Documenting without acting is malpractice. Delegating a UAP to feed an potentially unconscious patient risks fatal aspiration.

scenario: A patient with a history of Marfan syndrome complains of sudden, severe, tearing chest pain radiating to the upper back. Blood pressure is 80/40 mmHg. What is the priority concern?

options.B: Aortic dissection

hint: Tearing pain radiating to the back in a patient with connective tissue disease.

rationales.correct: Aortic dissection classically presents as sudden, severe, tearing or ripping chest/back pain. Marfan syndrome is a major risk factor due to connective tissue weakness. Hypotension indicates rupture.
rationales.incorrect: MI presents as crushing pressure. PE presents with sudden dyspnea. Pneumothorax presents with asymmetrical breath sounds.

scenario: A patient on a mechanical ventilator suddenly develops severe respiratory distress, absent breath sounds on the right, and tracheal deviation to the left. First action?

options.B: Order a stat chest X-ray

hint: The high pressure has collapsed the lung and is shifting the mediastinum.

rationales.correct: These are signs of a tension pneumothorax, an immediate life-threatening emergency. Needle decompression allows trapped air to escape before a chest tube is placed.
rationales.incorrect: X-ray delays life-saving treatment. Suctioning does not fix pleural air trapping. Fluids do not fix the mechanical compression of the heart.

scenario: A patient with Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA) has a blood glucose of 600 mg/dL. Which intravenous fluid should the nurse initiate first?

options.B: 5% Dextrose in Water

hint: DKA causes profound osmotic diuresis and hypovolemia.

rationales.correct: Isotonic 0.9% Normal Saline is the first-line fluid to rapidly restore intravascular volume and blood pressure in severely dehydrated DKA patients.
rationales.incorrect: 0.45% is hypotonic and used later. D5W worsens hyperglycemia initially. LR is not the standard initial resuscitation fluid for DKA.

scenario: A patient with cirrhosis is lethargic and exhibits asterixis. The ammonia level is 130 mcg/dL. Which medication will the nurse administer?

options.B: Propranolol

hint: This medication lowers pH in the colon to trap toxins.

rationales.correct: Lactulose converts ammonia to ammonium in the gut, trapping it for excretion in stool, effectively treating hepatic encephalopathy.
rationales.incorrect: Spironolactone treats ascites. Propranolol prevents variceal bleeding. Vitamin K corrects clotting factor deficits.

scenario: A patient with a T4 spinal cord injury complains of a pounding headache. Blood pressure is 210/110 mmHg. What is the immediate first action?

options.B: Elevate the head of the bed to 90 degrees

hint: Use gravity to drop the blood pressure instantly.

rationales.correct: The patient is experiencing autonomic dysreflexia. High-Fowler's position immediately induces orthostatic hypotension, dropping the BP to prevent stroke.
rationales.incorrect: Bladder/bowel checks are the second step to find the noxious stimulus. Antihypertensives are given if removing the stimulus fails.

scenario: A patient recovering from a thyroidectomy develops a temperature of 104 F, heart rate of 140 bpm, and extreme agitation. Diagnosis?

options.B: Hypocalcemia

hint: Manipulation of the gland released a massive surge of hormones.

rationales.correct: Thyroid storm (thyrotoxic crisis) is a hypermetabolic emergency caused by excessive thyroid hormone release, characterized by high fever, tachycardia, and agitation.
rationales.incorrect: Hypocalcemia presents with tetany. Infections take days to peak. Malignant hyperthermia occurs during anesthesia induction.

scenario: A patient post-thyroidectomy reports numbness around the mouth. A positive Trousseau's sign is elicited. What should the nurse prepare?

options.B: IV calcium gluconate

hint: The parathyroid glands may have been damaged.

rationales.correct: Perioral numbness and a positive Trousseau's sign indicate acute hypocalcemia from parathyroid damage. IV calcium gluconate is the antidote.
rationales.incorrect: Potassium is for hypokalemia. Tracheostomy is for stridor/hematoma. Paper bags treat respiratory alkalosis.

scenario: A patient 3 days post-op for a hip replacement develops sudden shortness of breath, pleuritic chest pain, and an SpO2 of 88%. What is the most likely cause?

options.B: Pulmonary embolism

hint: Immobility post-orthopedic surgery is a major risk factor for venous clots.

rationales.correct: Sudden dyspnea, pleuritic pain, and hypoxemia post-surgery strongly suggest a pulmonary embolism originating from a DVT.
rationales.incorrect: Fat embolism typically features a petechial rash. Pneumonia has a gradual onset with fever/cough. MI presents with crushing pain, not typically pleuritic.

scenario: A patient with a fractured femur becomes confused, dyspneic, and develops a petechial rash on the upper chest. What does the nurse suspect?

options.B: Deep vein thrombosis

hint: The classic triad: hypoxemia, neurological decline, and petechiae.

rationales.correct: Fat embolism syndrome occurs 24-72 hours after long bone fractures. The classic triad is hypoxemia, neurological changes, and a petechial rash.
rationales.incorrect: Allergies cause hives, not petechiae. DVT causes leg swelling. Compartment syndrome causes severe limb pain.

scenario: A patient with a cast on the right leg reports severe pain unrelieved by narcotics. The toes are pale, cool, and capillary refill is 4 seconds. First action?

options.A: Elevate the leg above the heart
options.B: Apply an ice pack to the cast

hint: Tissue ischemia is occurring due to trapped pressure.

rationales.correct: Unrelieved pain and poor perfusion indicate compartment syndrome, a surgical emergency requiring immediate fasciotomy or cast bivalving to save the limb.
rationales.incorrect: Elevation and ice cause vasoconstriction and decrease arterial flow, worsening ischemia. Narcotics mask symptoms.

scenario: A conscious, alert patient with type 1 diabetes reports shakiness and sweating. Blood glucose is 55 mg/dL. Best action?

options.B: Administer 50% dextrose IV push
options.D: Give a complex carbohydrate and protein snack immediately

hint: The patient can safely swallow.

rationales.correct: For conscious, alert hypoglycemic patients, 15 grams of fast-acting simple carbs (4 oz juice) is the initial treatment (15-15 rule).
rationales.incorrect: Glucagon and D50 are for unconscious/NPO patients. Complex carbs/proteins are given AFTER the glucose stabilizes.

scenario: A patient with Addison's disease presents with a BP of 70/40, profound weakness, and vomiting. Which medication is the absolute priority?

options.B: IV hydrocortisone

hint: The patient is missing stress hormones.

rationales.correct: This is an Addisonian crisis. High-dose IV hydrocortisone is required immediately to replace missing glucocorticoids and restore vascular tone.
rationales.incorrect: Insulin worsens hypoglycemia. PO meds are too slow. Epinephrine is for anaphylaxis.

scenario: A patient is diagnosed with Cushing's syndrome. Which dietary modification should the nurse teach?

options.B: Low sodium, low carbohydrate, high potassium

hint: Excess cortisol causes fluid retention, hyperglycemia, and potassium loss.

rationales.correct: Cushing's causes sodium/water retention, hyperglycemia, and hypokalemia. A low sodium, low carb, high potassium diet is necessary.
rationales.incorrect: High sodium/carb worsens symptoms. Fluid restriction isn't typically as strict as 500mL unless severe HF is present. Protein is needed to prevent muscle wasting.

scenario: A patient has Syndrome of Inappropriate Antidiuretic Hormone (SIADH). Which lab finding is expected?

options.B: Serum sodium 120 mEq/L

hint: The body retains pure water, diluting the blood.

rationales.correct: SIADH causes excess water retention, leading to dilutional hyponatremia (sodium < 135).
rationales.incorrect: High sodium, dilute urine (1.002), and high serum osmolality indicate Diabetes Insipidus (water loss).

scenario: A patient with a head injury begins passing 1,000 mL of pale urine per hour. Blood pressure drops to 90/50. Diagnosis?

options.B: Diabetes Insipidus

hint: Lack of antidiuretic hormone causes massive water loss.

rationales.correct: Diabetes Insipidus (DI) occurs after pituitary trauma, causing a lack of ADH. This results in massive polyuria, dilute urine, and hypovolemic shock.
rationales.incorrect: DM involves high glucose. SIADH causes oliguria. AKI typically causes oliguria, not massive polyuria.

scenario: A patient with acute pancreatitis is made NPO. What is the primary pathophysiological reason for this?

options.B: To stop the stimulation of pancreatic enzyme secretion
options.C: To prepare the bowel for surgery

hint: Food entering the duodenum triggers the organ to digest itself.

rationales.correct: NPO status rests the GI tract, preventing the release of secretin and CCK, which halts the secretion of pancreatic enzymes and stops autodigestion.
rationales.incorrect: Aspiration risk is secondary. Surgery isn't always needed. Ileus is a complication, but NPO primarily rests the pancreas.

scenario: A patient with a history of peptic ulcer disease suddenly develops a rigid, board-like abdomen and severe pain. Priority action?

options.B: Notify the provider immediately

hint: The stomach acid is spilling into the peritoneal cavity.

rationales.correct: A rigid, board-like abdomen indicates a perforated ulcer and chemical peritonitis. This is a surgical emergency requiring immediate provider notification.
rationales.incorrect: Antacids are useless. NG insertion can worsen the tear. Laxatives are contraindicated in acute abdominal emergencies.

scenario: A patient with end-stage renal disease has a potassium of 6.8 mEq/L and peaked T waves. First action?

options.B: IV calcium gluconate

hint: Protect the heart muscle first.

rationales.correct: Calcium gluconate instantly stabilizes the myocardial resting membrane potential, preventing lethal arrhythmias while other methods lower the potassium.
rationales.incorrect: Polystyrene is slow. Insulin shifts K+ but doesn't immediately protect the heart. Dialysis takes time to set up.

scenario: Which assessment finding is a classic sign of right-sided heart failure?

options.B: Pink, frothy sputum

hint: Fluid backs up into the systemic venous circulation.

rationales.correct: Right-sided HF causes blood to back up into the systemic circulation, leading to JVD, peripheral edema, and hepatomegaly.
rationales.incorrect: Orthopnea, frothy sputum, and crackles are signs of left-sided heart failure (fluid backing up into the lungs).

scenario: A patient with left-sided heart failure presents with severe dyspnea and pink, frothy sputum. Which medication should the nurse anticipate giving first?

options.B: Furosemide IV

hint: The lungs are filling with fluid.

rationales.correct: Pink, frothy sputum indicates acute pulmonary edema. IV furosemide rapidly diureses the patient and decreases preload, offloading the failing heart.
rationales.incorrect: Beta-blockers (carvedilol) are contraindicated in acute decompensation. Digoxin is too slow. Amiodarone is an antiarrhythmic.

scenario: What is the correct sequence for donning personal protective equipment (PPE)?

options.B: Gown, mask, goggles, gloves

hint: Work from the bottom up, securing the outer layer first.

rationales.correct: The standard sequence for donning PPE is Gown, Mask/Respirator, Goggles/Face Shield, and finally Gloves (pulled over the gown cuffs).
rationales.incorrect: Putting gloves on first contaminates them while tying the gown. Masks must go under goggles.

scenario: When removing (doffing) PPE, which item should be removed first?

options.B: Goggles

hint: Remove the most heavily contaminated item first.

rationales.correct: Gloves are considered the most contaminated piece of PPE and should be removed first (or simultaneously with the gown if tearing it away).
rationales.incorrect: Removing the mask first exposes the mucous membranes to contaminated hands/gloves.

scenario: Which patient must be placed in a negative pressure isolation room?

options.A: A patient with MRSA in a wound
options.B: A patient with Clostridium difficile

hint: Airborne pathogens require negative pressure.

rationales.correct: Tuberculosis is transmitted via airborne droplet nuclei, requiring a negative pressure room and an N95 respirator to keep pathogens from entering the hallway.
rationales.incorrect: MRSA and C. diff require contact precautions. Influenza requires droplet precautions (standard room).

scenario: A patient is admitted with Neisseria meningitidis. What type of precautions should be implemented?

options.B: Droplet precautions

hint: The bacteria is spread through large respiratory secretions.

rationales.correct: Bacterial meningitis is highly contagious via large respiratory droplets. Droplet precautions (surgical mask) are required for the first 24 hours of effective antibiotic therapy.
rationales.incorrect: Airborne is for TB/Measles. Contact is for skin/stool pathogens. Standard is insufficient.

scenario: A nurse is caring for a patient with Clostridium difficile. Which hand hygiene method is mandatory?

options.B: Chlorhexidine wipes

hint: Spores resist chemical destruction.

rationales.correct: C. difficile forms hardy spores that alcohol cannot kill. Handwashing with soap and water is mandatory to physically scrub and flush the spores down the drain.
rationales.incorrect: Alcohol sanitizers and chlorhexidine wipes are completely ineffective against C. diff spores.

scenario: A confused patient has soft wrist restraints applied to prevent pulling out an NG tube. How often must the nurse assess the patient's skin and circulation?

options.B: Every 2 hours

hint: This is a medical-surgical restraint.

rationales.correct: For non-violent (medical-surgical) restraints, skin integrity, circulation, and range of motion must be assessed and documented at least every 2 hours.
rationales.incorrect: Every 15 minutes is required for violent/behavioral restraints. 4 hours and once per shift are legally inadequate and constitute neglect.

scenario: A fire breaks out in a patient's room. According to the RACE protocol, what is the FIRST action?

options.B: Close the door to the room
options.C: Rescue the patient from the room

hint: Life safety is paramount.

rationales.correct: RACE: Rescue, Alarm, Confine, Extinguish. The absolute first step is pulling any person in immediate danger away from the fire.
rationales.incorrect: Alarming, confining, and extinguishing follow rescuing.

scenario: When operating a fire extinguisher using the PASS acronym, what does the 'A' stand for?

options.B: Aim at the base of the fire

hint: Aim where the fuel is.

rationales.correct: PASS stands for Pull the pin, Aim at the base of the fire, Squeeze the handle, Sweep from side to side.
rationales.incorrect: Activating the alarm is part of RACE. The other options do not fit the established acronym.

scenario: A nurse notes a patient's IV site is pale, cool to the touch, and swollen. The infusion is sluggish. Diagnosis?

options.B: Infiltration

hint: Non-vesicant fluid is leaking into the tissue.

rationales.correct: Infiltration occurs when IV fluid leaks into subcutaneous tissue, causing pallor, coolness, and swelling.
rationales.incorrect: Phlebitis causes redness and warmth. Extravasation involves tissue-damaging (vesicant) drugs. Thrombosis blocks the line but doesn't usually cause external swelling.

scenario: A patient's IV site is red, warm, and painful, with a palpable cord along the vein. What should the nurse do first?

options.A: Stop the infusion and remove the IV
options.B: Slow the IV rate and apply a cool compress
options.C: Flush the IV with normal saline
options.D: Administer an antibiotic through the IV

hint: The vein is inflamed.

rationales.correct: These are signs of phlebitis. The IV must be removed immediately to prevent further damage or clot formation, and a warm compress applied.
rationales.incorrect: Slowing the rate continues the damage. Flushing can dislodge a clot. Using a failing, inflamed line is contraindicated.

scenario: While removing a central venous catheter, the patient suddenly gasps, becomes cyanotic, and clutches their chest. The nurse suspects an air embolism. What position should the patient be placed in?

options.B: Left lateral Trendelenburg

hint: Trap the air in the right atrium before it reaches the lungs.

rationales.correct: The left lateral Trendelenburg (head down) position traps the air bubble in the apex of the right ventricle, preventing it from entering the pulmonary artery and causing a lethal blockage.
rationales.incorrect: High-Fowler's and supine positions allow the air to travel straight to the lungs or brain.

scenario: Before administering the first tube feeding through a newly placed NG tube, what is the ONLY acceptable method to verify placement?

options.A: Aspirate gastric contents and check pH
options.B: Inject air and listen for a whoosh

hint: Visualization is the gold standard.

rationales.correct: An X-ray is the only definitive method to confirm initial NG tube placement before introducing any fluids, preventing fatal pulmonary aspiration.
rationales.incorrect: pH testing is for ongoing verification. Auscultation is unreliable. Speaking does not rule out lung placement.

scenario: In what position should the nurse place a patient to administer a cleansing enema?

options.B: Prone

hint: Follow the anatomy of the descending colon.

rationales.correct: The left lateral Sims position aligns with the natural downward curve of the sigmoid and descending colon, allowing gravity to facilitate fluid flow.
rationales.incorrect: Right lateral forces fluid upward against gravity. Supine and prone are less effective and uncomfortable.

scenario: A patient with left-sided weakness is learning to use a cane. How should the nurse instruct the patient?

options.A: Hold the cane in the left hand and step with the left leg first
options.B: Hold the cane in the right hand and step with the left leg and cane together
options.C: Hold the cane in the right hand and step with the right leg first
options.D: Hold the cane in the left hand and step with the right leg first

hint: Cane Opposite Affected Leg (COAL).

rationales.correct: The cane is held on the strong side (right) to widen the base of support. The patient advances the cane and the weak leg (left) simultaneously, then follows with the strong leg.
rationales.incorrect: Holding the cane on the weak side provides no support. Stepping with the strong leg first leaves the patient balancing entirely on the weak leg.

scenario: A patient is learning to walk up stairs with crutches. Which leg should go up the step first?

options.B: The strong leg
options.D: The crutches go first, then the legs

hint: Up with the good, down with the bad.

rationales.correct: When going UP stairs, the strong (good) leg steps up first to bear the body's weight, followed by the crutches and the weak leg.
rationales.incorrect: Stepping with the weak leg first causes a fall because it cannot lift the body's weight. Putting crutches first throws off the center of gravity.

scenario: A patient is prescribed a 24-hour urine collection starting at 0800. What does the nurse do at exactly 0800?

options.A: Collect the void and save it in the jug
options.B: Ask the patient to void, discard the urine, and start the clock
options.D: Keep the jug at room temperature

hint: The bladder must be completely empty at the start time.

rationales.correct: To accurately measure exactly 24 hours of output, the bladder must be empty at the start. The first void is discarded, and all subsequent urine is saved for 24 hours.
rationales.incorrect: Saving the first void includes urine made prior to the start time. A Foley is invasive. The jug must be kept on ice to prevent bacterial overgrowth.

scenario: A nurse picks up a unit of packed red blood cells from the blood bank. What is the maximum time allowed to complete the transfusion?

options.B: 2 hours

hint: Blood degrades rapidly at room temperature.

rationales.correct: Blood transfusions must be entirely completed within 4 hours of leaving the blood bank to prevent severe bacterial growth and red blood cell hemolysis.
rationales.incorrect: It must be started within 30 minutes, but can run up to 4 hours. 6 hours is too long and risks septicemia.

scenario: What is the primary purpose of the Z-track method for intramuscular injections?

options.B: To speed up absorption
options.C: To prevent medication from tracking back into subcutaneous tissue
options.D: To avoid hitting a blood vessel

hint: It creates a staggered path.

rationales.correct: The Z-track method displaces skin before injection. Upon release, the tissue layers slide back, trapping the medication deep in the muscle to prevent skin staining and irritation.
rationales.incorrect: It does not alter pain, absorption speed, or vessel avoidance.

scenario: A nurse is administering eye drops. Where should the drops be instilled?

options.B: Into the lower conjunctival sac

hint: Avoid the sensitive clear part of the eye.

rationales.correct: Eye drops are instilled into the lower conjunctival sac (pulled down by the nurse) to prevent corneal trauma and systemic absorption via the tear duct.
rationales.incorrect: Dropping directly on the cornea causes pain and reflex blinking. The inner canthus connects to the systemic tear duct.

scenario: A 5-year-old is brought to the ED with severe sore throat, drooling, high fever, and stridor. What is the nurse's priority?

options.A: Inspect the throat with a tongue blade
options.B: Prepare for emergency intubation

hint: The airway is severely inflamed and can close instantly.

rationales.correct: These are classic signs of epiglottitis. The airway can totally occlude at any moment. Emergency intubation or tracheostomy equipment must be immediately available.
rationales.incorrect: Inserting a tongue blade causes reflex laryngospasm, causing instant death. IV insertion causes crying (worsening swelling) and should be delayed. Oral meds pose a choking risk.

scenario: A 2-year-old child with a barking, seal-like cough is diagnosed with viral croup. What is the most effective initial non-pharmacologic intervention?

options.B: Encourage deep breathing exercises

hint: Soothe the swollen vocal cords.

rationales.correct: Cool mist humidification reduces subglottic edema and relieves the stridor and barking cough associated with croup.
rationales.incorrect: Deep breathing and CPT do not relieve upper airway swelling. Warm fluids don't directly reduce mucosal edema like cool mist does.

scenario: A 6-month-old with Tetralogy of Fallot has a hypercyanotic spell (Tet spell). What is the first action the nurse should take?

options.B: Place the infant in the knee-chest position

hint: Increase systemic resistance to push blood back to the lungs.

rationales.correct: The knee-chest position kinks the femoral arteries, increasing systemic vascular resistance, which forces blood through the pulmonary artery, reversing the right-to-left shunt.
rationales.incorrect: Oxygen and morphine are secondary steps. A code is for cardiac arrest, not cyanotic spells.

scenario: A toddler is diagnosed with Cystic Fibrosis. Which instruction must the nurse give the parents regarding pancreatic enzymes?

options.A: Give the enzymes 2 hours after meals
options.B: Give the enzymes immediately before or with all meals and snacks
options.C: Crush the enzymes into a hot bowl of soup
options.D: Administer only when the child has steatorrhea

hint: The enzymes must mix with the food in the stomach.

rationales.correct: Pancreatic enzymes (pancrelipase) must be taken right before or during every meal and snack to properly digest fats and proteins.
rationales.incorrect: Giving them after meals is ineffective. Heat destroys them. They are a daily necessity, not PRN for fatty stools.

scenario: A 5-year-old is diagnosed with Kawasaki disease. Which medication does the nurse expect to administer to prevent coronary artery aneurysms?

options.B: Intravenous Immunoglobulin (IVIG) and high-dose aspirin

hint: Halt the systemic vasculitis and prevent platelets from clotting.

rationales.correct: Kawasaki disease is treated with a single large dose of IVIG and high-dose aspirin to shut down the immune-mediated vasculitis and protect the coronary arteries.
rationales.incorrect: Acetaminophen does not protect the heart. Steroids are second-line. Albuterol treats asthma.

scenario: A child has a history of rheumatic fever. Why must the child take daily prophylactic penicillin?

options.A: To cure the damaged heart valves
options.B: To prevent recurrent Group A Streptococcus infections
options.D: To prevent the spread of the disease to siblings

hint: Prevent the trigger of the autoimmune response.

rationales.correct: Rheumatic fever damages heart valves. Prophylactic antibiotics prevent recurrent Strep A infections, which would trigger further autoimmune attacks on the valves.
rationales.incorrect: It does not cure existing damage or joint pain. Rheumatic fever itself is not contagious.

scenario: An infant with pyloric stenosis has been vomiting for 3 days. Which acid-base imbalance is expected?

options.B: Metabolic alkalosis

hint: Losing stomach acid.

rationales.correct: Projectile vomiting causes massive loss of gastric hydrochloric acid, leading to metabolic alkalosis.
rationales.incorrect: Metabolic acidosis is from diarrhea (loss of base). Respiratory imbalances relate to lung function.

scenario: A 9-month-old infant is admitted with intussusception. What classic assessment finding does the nurse anticipate?

options.B: Ribbon-like stools

hint: Blood and mucus mixed together.

rationales.correct: Intussusception (telescoping bowel) causes ischemia and mucosal sloughing, resulting in stools mixed with blood and mucus ('currant jelly' stools).
rationales.incorrect: Ribbon-like stools = Hirschsprung's. Painless bleeding = Meckel's diverticulum. Clay-colored = Biliary atresia.

scenario: A newborn fails to pass meconium within 48 hours and has abdominal distention. Which condition does the nurse suspect?

options.B: Hirschsprung's disease

hint: Lack of ganglion cells in the colon prevents peristalsis.

rationales.correct: Hirschsprung's disease is an aganglionic megacolon. Without nerve cells to stimulate peristalsis, stool backs up, leading to a failure to pass meconium.
rationales.incorrect: Pyloric stenosis causes vomiting. Celiac causes chronic diarrhea/malabsorption. Intussusception occurs later (6-36 months) with colicky pain.

scenario: An infant is recovering from a cleft lip repair (cheiloplasty). Which position is safest?

options.B: Supine or side-lying

hint: Keep the baby's face off the mattress.

rationales.correct: The infant must be placed supine or side-lying to prevent them from rubbing their face and tearing the delicate surgical suture line against the mattress.
rationales.incorrect: Prone puts direct pressure on the lip. Trendelenburg increases head pressure. High-Fowler's is not a safe sleeping position for an infant.

scenario: A 3-year-old child is scheduled for a lumbar puncture to test for meningitis. How should the nurse position the child?

options.A: Prone with a pillow under the abdomen
options.B: Supine with legs extended
options.C: Side-lying fetal position with knees pulled to the chest

hint: Open the spaces between the vertebrae.

rationales.correct: The side-lying fetal position curves the spine outward, maximizing the space between the lumbar vertebrae for safe needle insertion.
rationales.incorrect: Prone, supine, and dangling do not adequately open the intervertebral spaces.

scenario: A 6-year-old recovering from chickenpox develops severe vomiting, confusion, and lethargy after the parents gave an OTC fever reducer. What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.B: Reye's syndrome

hint: Aspirin given during a viral illness is the trigger.

rationales.correct: Reye's syndrome is a fatal toxic encephalopathy and fatty liver degeneration caused by giving salicylates (aspirin) to children with viral illnesses like varicella or influenza.
rationales.incorrect: Meningitis/encephalitis present with fever and stiff neck. GBS causes ascending paralysis.

scenario: A child with sickle cell disease presents in a vaso-occlusive crisis with pain of 9/10. What is the absolute priority intervention?

options.A: Apply cold compresses to the joints
options.B: Administer aggressive IV hydration

hint: Thick blood is causing the ischemia.

rationales.correct: Aggressive IV hydration is the priority to decrease blood viscosity (hemodilution). Thinner blood flows past the blockages, reversing the tissue ischemia and pain.
rationales.incorrect: Cold compresses cause vasoconstriction, worsening the blockage. Antibiotics are for infection, not the primary fix for the crisis. ROM worsens oxygen demand and pain.

scenario: A 9-month-old infant is prescribed oral liquid iron drops. How should the nurse teach the mother to administer it?

options.A: Mix it in a full bottle of milk
options.B: Administer it with a syringe toward the back of the mouth
options.C: Give it right after a large meal
options.D: Mix it with a crushed calcium tablet

hint: Iron stains teeth permanently.

rationales.correct: Liquid iron stains the teeth. It must be administered via syringe toward the back of the mouth to avoid contact with the front teeth. It absorbs best with Vitamin C (orange juice).
rationales.incorrect: Milk and calcium actively block iron absorption. It is best given on an empty stomach if tolerated.

scenario: A toddler is diagnosed with a Wilms tumor (nephroblastoma). Which sign is strictly placed above the bed?

options.B: Fluid Restriction

hint: The tumor capsule is fragile and can burst.

rationales.correct: Wilms tumor is encapsulated. Palpating the abdomen can rupture the capsule and seed cancer cells throughout the peritoneal cavity.
rationales.incorrect: Fluid restriction, prone positioning, and NPO are not specific interventions for this tumor prior to surgery.

scenario: A 5-year-old child complains of severe perianal itching at night. The nurse suspects pinworms. Which diagnostic test is appropriate?

options.B: Urinalysis
options.C: Transparent tape test applied to the anus in the morning

hint: The female worms crawl out at night to lay eggs.

rationales.correct: Pinworms lay eggs around the anus at night. Placing clear tape over the anus early in the morning before bathing catches the eggs for microscopic diagnosis.
rationales.incorrect: Stool cultures and blood tests do not typically catch this localized parasite.

scenario: A 4-year-old boy uses his hands to 'walk' up his own legs to stand from the floor. The nurse documents this as Gowers' sign. What disease is this a hallmark of?

options.B: Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy (DMD)

hint: Progressive, ascending muscle weakness.

rationales.correct: Gowers' sign indicates severe proximal muscle weakness of the pelvic girdle, a classic hallmark of Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy.
rationales.incorrect: Cerebral palsy features spasticity. Spina bifida features lower limb paralysis. Arthritis features joint stiffness.

scenario: A child with Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) is hospitalized. Which nursing intervention best minimizes anxiety?

options.A: Provide a variety of new toys daily
options.B: Maintain a strict, consistent daily routine
options.C: Place the child in a shared room to promote socialization
options.D: Use frequent physical touch for comfort

hint: Children with ASD rely heavily on predictability.

rationales.correct: Children with ASD experience severe anxiety with environmental changes. Maintaining a predictable, consistent routine mimicking their home schedule prevents outbursts.
rationales.incorrect: New toys and roommates cause sensory overload. Many children with ASD are hypersensitive to touch and find it distressing.

scenario: A 2-year-old child hospitalized for pneumonia cries uncontrollably, kicks the bed, and rejects the nurse when the parents leave for work. Which stage of separation anxiety is this?

options.B: Detachment

hint: Active, aggressive resistance.

rationales.correct: The 'Protest' stage is characterized by crying, screaming, and rejecting strangers. It is a normal, healthy attachment response.
rationales.incorrect: Despair is marked by withdrawal and quiet sadness. Detachment is superficial adjustment where the child ignores the parents. Regression is returning to earlier habits.

scenario: At a well-child check, an 11-month-old infant is unable to sit up without support. How should the nurse interpret this?

options.B: Significant gross motor delay requiring evaluation
options.D: Sign of an impending growth spurt

hint: Gross motor skills develop head-to-toe.

rationales.correct: Infants should be able to sit unsupported by 8 months. Inability to do so at 11 months is a significant gross motor delay requiring neurological evaluation.
rationales.incorrect: It is not normal. It is a gross motor issue, not fine motor. It is not related to a growth spurt.

scenario: A pregnant patient's last menstrual period (LMP) began on April 10. Using Naegele's rule, what is her estimated date of delivery (EDD)?

options.B: February 17 of the next year

hint: Subtract 3 months, add 7 days.

rationales.correct: Naegele's rule: Subtract 3 months from April 10 (January 10), add 7 days (January 17), and add 1 year. The EDD is January 17.
rationales.incorrect: The other options represent math errors using the formula.

scenario: A pregnant patient has a history of one miscarriage at 8 weeks, one full-term infant (living), and preterm twins born at 33 weeks (both living). What is her GTPAL?
hint: Twins count as one pregnancy and one delivery event.

rationales.correct: Gravida = 4 (current + term + preterm + abortion). Term = 1. Preterm = 1 (twins are ONE delivery). Abortion = 1. Living = 3. GTPAL: 4-1-1-1-3.
rationales.incorrect: Counting twins as two parities (P) is a common error.

scenario: A patient at 34 weeks gestation presents with painless, bright red vaginal bleeding. Which procedure is strictly contraindicated?

options.B: Digital vaginal examination

hint: The placenta may be covering the cervix.

rationales.correct: Painless, bright red bleeding indicates placenta previa. A digital vaginal exam can puncture the placenta, causing massive, lethal hemorrhage. Exams are strictly contraindicated.
rationales.incorrect: Ultrasound, fetal monitoring, and IV access are critical, safe interventions.

scenario: A patient at 38 weeks presents with severe, constant abdominal pain, a rigid, board-like abdomen, and dark red vaginal bleeding. Diagnosis?

options.B: Uterine rupture

hint: Pain + Rigid abdomen + Dark bleeding = Separation.

rationales.correct: Placental abruption involves premature detachment of the placenta. This traps blood behind it, causing intense abdominal rigidity, severe pain, and dark red bleeding.
rationales.incorrect: Placenta previa is painless with bright red bleeding. Uterine rupture features a loss of contractions. Vasa previa is painless fetal bleeding.

scenario: A laboring patient's water breaks. The fetal heart rate suddenly drops to 70 bpm, and a pulsating cord is felt in the vagina. What is the immediate first action?

options.B: Use two sterile fingers to lift the fetal head off the cord
options.C: Push the cord gently back into the uterus
options.D: Place the patient in a supine position

hint: Relieve the mechanical compression on the lifeline.

rationales.correct: This is a prolapsed umbilical cord. The absolute first action is to manually lift the baby's head off the cord to restore blood flow while maintaining this position until an emergency C-section is performed.
rationales.incorrect: Oxygen is secondary. NEVER push the cord in (causes vasospasm). Supine position worsens compression (use knee-chest).

scenario: A patient with preeclampsia is receiving IV magnesium sulfate. The nurse notes absent deep tendon reflexes and a respiratory rate of 10. Antidote?

options.B: Calcium gluconate

hint: This electrolyte directly antagonizes magnesium.

rationales.correct: The patient is in magnesium toxicity. The infusion must be stopped, and the specific antidote, calcium gluconate, administered to reverse respiratory and CNS depression.
rationales.incorrect: Naloxone reverses opioids. Flumazenil reverses benzos. Protamine reverses heparin.

scenario: A patient with severe preeclampsia develops HELLP syndrome. Which laboratory profile confirms this?

options.A: High platelets, low liver enzymes, high hemoglobin
options.B: Hemolysis, elevated liver enzymes, low platelets
options.C: High glucose, elevated BUN, low potassium
options.D: Low WBCs, low liver enzymes, high platelets

hint: Read the acronym.

rationales.correct: HELLP stands for Hemolysis (H), Elevated Liver enzymes (EL), and Low Platelets (LP). It is a severe progression of preeclampsia requiring immediate delivery.
rationales.incorrect: The other options contradict the disease pathology, which destroys RBCs and platelets while injuring the liver.

scenario: Two hours postpartum, a patient is bleeding heavily. The fundus is boggy, deviated to the right, and 2 cm above the umbilicus. Most appropriate first action?

options.B: Administer IM methylergonovine
options.C: Assist the patient to empty her bladder

hint: A mechanical obstruction is pushing the uterus out of place.

rationales.correct: A full bladder physically displaces the uterus (usually to the right), preventing it from clamping down. Emptying the bladder fixes the root mechanical cause.
rationales.incorrect: Massage will not keep the uterus firm if the bladder is full. Meds and calling the MD are secondary to emptying the bladder.

scenario: A patient with postpartum hemorrhage is prescribed methylergonovine (Methergine). Which condition is an absolute contraindication?

options.B: Hypertension

hint: This medication causes intense systemic vasoconstriction.

rationales.correct: Methylergonovine is a potent vasoconstrictor. It is strictly contraindicated in patients with hypertension or preeclampsia because it can cause a lethal hypertensive crisis or stroke.
rationales.incorrect: Asthma is a contraindication for Carboprost (Hemabate). Diabetes and C-sections are not contraindications.

scenario: The fetal heart monitor displays late decelerations. What is the physiological cause?

options.B: Umbilical cord compression

hint: VEAL CHOP.

rationales.correct: Late decelerations indicate uteroplacental insufficiency (the fetus is not getting enough oxygen from the placenta). This requires immediate intrauterine resuscitation (LIONS).
rationales.incorrect: Head compression = Early decels. Cord compression = Variable decels. Sleep cycles decrease variability, not decelerations.

scenario: The fetal monitor shows variable decelerations. What is the most appropriate first-line nursing intervention?

options.A: Turn the patient to her left or right side
options.B: Stop the oxytocin infusion

hint: Variable decelerations mean cord compression. Shifting the baby's weight relieves it.

rationales.correct: Variable decelerations are caused by umbilical cord compression. Changing the maternal position often shifts the fetus off the cord, instantly resolving the deceleration.
rationales.incorrect: Stopping oxytocin, giving fluids, and oxygen are secondary steps if positioning fails.

scenario: A mother who is Rh-negative delivers an Rh-positive infant. When must the nurse administer RhoGAM?

options.B: Within 72 hours postpartum
options.D: During the first trimester of her next pregnancy

hint: Stop the mother's immune system before it finalizes antibody production.

rationales.correct: RhoGAM must be administered to an Rh-negative mother within 72 hours of delivering an Rh-positive infant to prevent the formation of antibodies that would destroy future Rh-positive fetuses.
rationales.incorrect: While 24 hours is acceptable, the protocol limit is 72 hours. 6 weeks is far too late; irreversible sensitization will have occurred.

scenario: A patient with an unruptured ectopic pregnancy is prescribed methotrexate. What instruction must the nurse provide?

options.A: Avoid taking folic acid supplements or prenatal vitamins
options.B: Drink at least 4 cups of milk daily
options.C: You will need to have your fallopian tubes tied
options.D: Expect severe shoulder pain over the next few days

hint: Methotrexate works by antagonizing a specific vitamin.

rationales.correct: Methotrexate is a folic acid antagonist used to stop the rapidly dividing cells of the ectopic pregnancy. Prenatal vitamins contain folic acid, which reverses the drug's effectiveness.
rationales.incorrect: Milk is irrelevant. Methotrexate preserves fertility. Severe shoulder pain indicates rupture (a surgical emergency), not an expected outcome.

scenario: A patient undergoes a D&C for a hydatidiform mole (molar pregnancy). What is the most critical post-operative teaching point?

options.A: You must use strict contraception for at least 1 year
options.B: Try to get pregnant again quickly to heal the uterus
options.C: You will need a hysterectomy to prevent cancer
options.D: You must undergo radiation therapy for 6 months

hint: Pregnancy hormones mask the growth of a specific cancer.

rationales.correct: Molar pregnancies carry a high risk of developing into choriocarcinoma. hCG levels must be monitored for 1 year to ensure no cancer grows. Pregnancy naturally raises hCG, masking cancer, making strict contraception mandatory.
rationales.incorrect: Getting pregnant ruins the hCG monitoring. Hysterectomy/radiation are only for confirmed cancer.

scenario: A breastfeeding mother develops mastitis in her right breast. What should the nurse teach her about feeding?

options.B: Pump the right breast and discard the milk
options.C: Continue feeding frequently, offering the unaffected breast first to stimulate let-down
options.D: Wean the baby to formula until antibiotics are finished

hint: Emptying the breast cures the infection; the milk is safe.

rationales.correct: Mastitis requires frequent, complete emptying of the breast. Offering the unaffected breast first triggers the let-down reflex painlessly, allowing the baby to empty the painful breast more easily.
rationales.incorrect: Stopping feeding causes severe engorgement and abscess. The milk is safe for the infant.

scenario: A pregnant patient in her third trimester feels dizzy, clammy, and nauseated when lying flat on her back. What is the physiological cause?

options.A: Compression of the inferior vena cava by the uterus
options.B: Sudden drop in blood sugar

hint: Supine hypotensive syndrome.

rationales.correct: When a pregnant woman lies supine, the heavy gravid uterus compresses the inferior vena cava, drastically reducing venous return to the heart and causing maternal hypotension. Turning to the left side resolves it.
rationales.incorrect: It is a mechanical positional issue, not related to blood sugar, breathing, or an embolus.

scenario: A nurse notes jaundice in a newborn appearing on the 3rd day of life. What does this finding indicate?

options.A: Pathologic jaundice requiring immediate exchange transfusion
options.B: Physiologic jaundice due to an immature liver

hint: Timing is the key differentiator for neonatal jaundice.

rationales.correct: Jaundice appearing AFTER 24 hours (typically days 2-4) is physiologic jaundice, a normal response to the breakdown of fetal RBCs and an immature liver.
rationales.incorrect: Pathologic jaundice (ABO/Rh incompatibility) appears WITHIN the first 24 hours of life and is a medical emergency.

scenario: A newborn born to a mother with an opioid addiction is experiencing Neonatal Abstinence Syndrome (NAS). Which nursing intervention is most appropriate?

options.B: Place the infant in a brightly lit room
options.C: Swaddle the infant tightly and place in a quiet, dark room
options.D: Feed the infant large volumes of formula every 6 hours

hint: The infant's CNS is extremely hypersensitive.

rationales.correct: NAS causes severe CNS hyperirritability. Nursing care focuses on minimizing sensory overload by providing a dark, quiet environment and tight swaddling to prevent self-stimulation.
rationales.incorrect: Stimulation and bright lights trigger distress and seizures. Feedings should be small and frequent.

scenario: A triage nurse assesses victims of a building collapse. A patient has an open head injury, a GCS of 3, agonal breathing, and no radial pulse. Which tag is applied?

options.B: Yellow (Urgent)

hint: Resources cannot be spent on those who cannot survive in a disaster.

rationales.correct: In mass casualty triage, a patient with catastrophic, non-survivable injuries (GCS 3, agonal breathing, shock) is tagged Black (Expectant). Resources must be saved for survivable injuries.
rationales.incorrect: Red is for life-threatening but highly salvageable injuries. Yellow is delayed care. Green is walking wounded.

scenario: Which mass casualty victim should receive a RED tag?

options.A: A patient with a compound femur fracture screaming in pain
options.B: A patient with an open pneumothorax and severe dyspnea
options.C: An unresponsive patient with 90% full-thickness burns
options.D: A patient with minor lacerations wandering in a daze

hint: Life-threatening ABC issue that is easily and quickly fixable.

rationales.correct: A tension or open pneumothorax is immediately life-threatening but highly treatable (needle decompression/occlusive dressing). This earns a Red (Immediate) tag.
rationales.incorrect: Femur fracture is Yellow. 90% burns/unresponsive is Black. Minor lacerations are Green.

scenario: A nurse receives report on 4 patients. Who should be assessed FIRST?

options.A: A patient 12 hours post-op reporting 9/10 incision pain
options.B: A patient with heart failure presenting with pink, frothy sputum
options.C: A patient with pneumonia with a temperature of 101.5 F
options.D: A patient with a DVT whose calf is swollen and warm

hint: Pink frothy sputum means the lungs are filling with fluid.

rationales.correct: Pink frothy sputum is the hallmark of acute pulmonary edema, an immediate life-threatening airway/breathing emergency requiring rapid diuretics and oxygen.
rationales.incorrect: Pain is secondary to ABCs. Fever in pneumonia is expected. Swollen calf in DVT is expected; sudden chest pain would make it a priority (PE).

scenario: Which patient requires the nurse's immediate attention?

options.A: A patient with chronic COPD resting quietly with an SpO2 of 89%
options.B: A patient with stable angina waiting for discharge papers
options.C: A patient with new-onset atrial fibrillation with a heart rate of 145
options.D: A patient 2 days post-appendectomy asking to ambulate

hint: New and unstable beats chronic and stable.

rationales.correct: New-onset atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response (HR 145) is an acute, unstable hemodynamic event that drastically drops cardiac output and risks stroke.
rationales.incorrect: SpO2 of 89% is normal for chronic COPD. Stable angina and post-op ambulation are routine.

scenario: Which task is most appropriate for the RN to delegate to a Licensed Practical Nurse (LPN)?

options.A: Perform the admission assessment on a new patient
options.B: Administer an IV push dose of morphine
options.C: Perform a sterile dressing change on a central venous catheter
options.D: Create the initial nursing care plan for a diabetic patient

hint: LPNs handle complex, sterile procedures on stable patients.

rationales.correct: Sterile dressing changes (including central lines on stable patients) are procedural tasks well within the LPN scope of practice.
rationales.incorrect: Admission assessments, IV push narcotics, and initial care planning are strictly restricted to the RN scope of practice.

scenario: A nurse is working with an Unlicensed Assistive Personnel (UAP). Which task is appropriate to delegate to the UAP?

options.A: Feeding a patient with new-onset dysphagia following a stroke
options.B: Taking vital signs on a patient 15 minutes after a blood transfusion starts
options.C: Emptying a Jackson-Pratt drain and recording the output
options.D: Assessing a patient's heel for pressure ulcer progression

hint: UAPs do routine measuring and recording, not critical assessment.

rationales.correct: Emptying a surgical drain and recording the volume is a standard, non-sterile procedural task perfectly suited for a UAP.
rationales.incorrect: Feeding a NEW dysphagia patient requires RN assessment for choking. The first 15 mins of a blood transfusion require an RN. Assessing skin is an RN duty.

scenario: A nurse floats from the Maternity unit to the Med-Surg unit. Which patient is the safest assignment for this nurse?

options.A: A patient with a continuous IV heparin drip for a pulmonary embolism
options.B: A patient 2 days post-op from a cholecystectomy waiting to be discharged
options.C: A patient admitted 1 hour ago with diabetic ketoacidosis
options.D: A patient with a tracheostomy requiring frequent deep suctioning

hint: Give the float nurse the most stable, predictable patient.

rationales.correct: A stable patient awaiting discharge requires standard basic nursing care (vitals, ambulation) and is the safest, most predictable assignment for a non-specialized float nurse.
rationales.incorrect: Heparin drips, DKA, and complex artificial airways require specialized med-surg or critical care assessment skills.

scenario: A patient is found on the floor. After assessing the patient and calling the provider, the nurse completes an incident report. How should the nurse chart this event?

options.A: Document the objective facts of the fall and note that an incident report was filed
options.B: Attach a copy of the incident report to the patient's medical record
options.C: Document the objective clinical findings, actions taken, and provider notification in the chart
options.D: Do not document the fall in the chart to protect the hospital

hint: The chart is for clinical facts; the incident report is for internal risk management.

rationales.correct: The medical record must reflect only the objective clinical facts of what occurred, the assessment, and the interventions. The incident report remains entirely separate.
rationales.incorrect: NEVER write 'incident report filed' in the chart; doing so makes the internal hospital document subpoenaable in court. Falsifying the record by omitting the fall is illegal.

scenario: A nurse suspects a coworker is impaired by narcotics while on duty. The coworker's speech is slurred. What is the nurse's legal and ethical duty?

options.A: Confront the nurse privately and ask them to go home
options.B: Immediately report the suspicion to the nursing supervisor
options.C: Wait to see if the nurse makes a documented medication error
options.D: Call the police to arrest the nurse for drug diversion

hint: Patient safety is the immediate priority; use the chain of command.

rationales.correct: An impaired nurse is a direct threat to patient safety. The observing nurse has a mandatory duty to report the suspicion immediately to the supervisor to remove the impaired nurse from patient care.
rationales.incorrect: Confronting them allows them to deny it and keep working. Waiting allows patient harm. Calling police bypasses internal hospital protocols.

scenario: A patient signed the informed consent for an elective surgery yesterday. Today, as the transporter arrives, the patient says, 'I changed my mind. I don't want the surgery.' Action?

options.A: Tell the patient they already signed the legally binding contract
options.B: Send the patient to the OR and let the surgeon convince them
options.C: Cancel the transport and notify the surgeon immediately
options.D: Administer a sedative to calm the patient's nerves

hint: Consent is a continuous process, not just a piece of paper.

rationales.correct: A competent patient has the right to withdraw consent at ANY time, even right outside the operating room. The nurse must halt the process and notify the surgeon.
rationales.incorrect: Consent is not a binding contract. Forcing them to the OR is battery. Sedating a refusing patient is chemical restraint and battery.

scenario: A patient with terminal cancer signs a DNR order. The patient stops breathing. The daughter screams at the nurse to 'Do everything and save him!' What is the nurse's ethical duty?

options.A: Begin CPR to prevent a lawsuit from the daughter
options.B: Honor the DNR order and provide comfort care only
options.C: Call the hospital ethics committee immediately while doing compressions
options.D: Ask the attending physician to overturn the DNR verbally
