scenario: A confused patient has bilateral soft wrist restraints applied to prevent pulling out an endotracheal tube. How often must the nurse assess the skin and neurovascular status of the restrained extremities?

options.B: Every 2 hours

hint: Standard medical-surgical restraint monitoring.

rationales.correct: For non-violent (medical-surgical) restraints, the nurse must assess skin integrity, pulses, color, and temperature, and provide range of motion at least every 2 hours.
rationales.incorrect: Every 15 minutes is required for violent/behavioral restraints. 4 hours and once per shift are legally insufficient and constitute neglect.

scenario: A nurse floats from the psychiatric unit to the telemetry unit. Which patient should the charge nurse assign to the float nurse?

options.A: A patient with a new-onset myocardial infarction on a nitroglycerin drip
options.B: A patient admitted for alcohol withdrawal experiencing delirium tremens
options.C: A patient with a temporary transvenous pacemaker
options.D: A patient scheduled for an emergency cardiac catheterization

hint: Match the nurse's primary skill set with the patient's needs.

rationales.correct: A psychiatric nurse has extensive experience managing substance abuse, withdrawal, and behavioral issues. A patient with alcohol withdrawal/delirium tremens is the safest and most appropriate match for their skills.
rationales.incorrect: Nitro drips, pacemakers, and pre-cath patients require highly specialized cardiac assessment skills that a psych nurse may not possess.

scenario: A patient with Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome (ARDS) is mechanically ventilated and remains profoundly hypoxemic despite high FiO2 and PEEP. What is the priority nursing positioning strategy?

options.B: Left lateral decubitus position

hint: Offload the weight of the heart and recruit posterior alveoli.

rationales.correct: Prone positioning improves oxygenation in severe ARDS by recruiting collapsed posterior alveoli, reducing V/Q mismatch, and removing the compressive weight of the heart and abdominal organs from the lungs.
rationales.incorrect: High-Fowler's does not recruit the massive posterior dependent lung regions. Left lateral targets one lung. Trendelenburg pushes abdominal contents against the diaphragm, worsening hypoxemia.

scenario: A patient with liver cirrhosis has bleeding esophageal varices. The provider orders an octreotide continuous IV infusion. What is the primary mechanism of this drug for this patient?

options.A: It promotes rapid clotting at the bleeding site.
options.B: It causes splanchnic vasoconstriction to reduce portal blood flow and pressure.
options.C: It decreases gastric acid production to prevent ulceration.
options.D: It binds to ammonia in the gut to prevent encephalopathy.

hint: This synthetic hormone selectively constricts vessels feeding the liver.

rationales.correct: Octreotide (a synthetic somatostatin analog) causes selective splanchnic vasoconstriction, which significantly decreases blood flow and pressure in the portal circulation, halting variceal bleeding.
rationales.incorrect: It is not a coagulant. PPIs decrease gastric acid. Lactulose binds to ammonia.

scenario: A patient with Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD) on hemodialysis asks for a dietary snack. Which option is safest for this patient?

options.B: A slice of watermelon
options.D: A handful of almonds and a glass of milk

hint: Renal diets must strictly limit potassium, phosphorus, and sodium.

rationales.correct: Apples and grapes are low in potassium and phosphorus, making them safe for a CKD/dialysis diet.
rationales.incorrect: Bananas, oranges, and watermelon are extremely high in potassium. Almonds and milk are very high in phosphorus and potassium.

scenario: A patient with Addison's disease is admitted with pneumonia. What adjustment to the patient's daily hydrocortisone dose does the nurse anticipate?

options.A: The dose will be decreased to prevent masking the infection.
options.B: The dose will be temporarily discontinued to boost the immune system.
options.C: The dose will be increased to mimic the body's natural stress response.
options.D: The dose will remain exactly the same.

hint: Addison's patients cannot endogenously produce stress hormones.

rationales.correct: During times of physiological stress (infection, trauma, surgery), the adrenal glands normally secrete extra cortisol. Addison's patients cannot do this, so their exogenous hydrocortisone dose must be increased ('stress dosing') to prevent a lethal adrenal crisis.
rationales.incorrect: Decreasing, stopping, or maintaining the baseline dose during stress will rapidly lead to an Addisonian crisis (hypotension, shock, death).

scenario: A patient with Cushing's syndrome is admitted. Which nursing diagnosis is the highest priority?

options.B: Disturbed Body Image

hint: Excessive cortisol profoundly suppresses the immune system.

rationales.correct: Cushing's syndrome involves hypercortisolism, which suppresses the inflammatory and immune responses. Patients are at an extremely high risk for overwhelming, 'silent' infections (where fever/redness may be masked).
rationales.incorrect: Body image (moon face/buffalo hump) is psychosocial. They risk HYPERglycemia, not hypoglycemia. Mobility may be affected by osteoporosis, but infection is the immediate life threat.

scenario: A patient is admitted in myxedema coma. The patient has a core temperature of 94.2 F (34.5 C). What is the safest nursing intervention to address the hypothermia?

options.A: Apply external warming blankets on the highest setting
options.B: Immerse the patient in a warm water bath
options.C: Apply warm blankets slowly and maintain a warm room temperature

hint: Rapid warming causes massive vasodilation, leading to cardiovascular collapse.

rationales.correct: In myxedema coma, the body is profoundly hypometabolic and vasoconstricted. Gentle, passive rewarming with blankets and a warm room is required to prevent sudden vasodilation and profound, lethal shock.
rationales.incorrect: Active, rapid external warming (heating blankets, hot baths, rapid hot IVs) causes rapid peripheral vasodilation, abruptly dropping blood pressure and causing cardiovascular collapse.

scenario: A patient in thyroid storm is prescribed propylthiouracil (PTU). What is the primary therapeutic action of this drug in a crisis?

options.A: It permanently destroys the thyroid gland.
options.B: It blocks the synthesis of new thyroid hormones and prevents peripheral conversion of T4 to T3.
options.C: It rapidly lowers the patient's heart rate.
options.D: It immediately neutralizes circulating T3 in the bloodstream.

hint: It stops the factory and stops the product from being activated.

rationales.correct: PTU is preferred in thyroid storm over methimazole because it not only blocks the synthesis of new thyroid hormones in the gland but also blocks the peripheral conversion of T4 into the more active T3.
rationales.incorrect: Radioactive iodine destroys the gland. Beta-blockers (propranolol) lower the heart rate. No drug neutralizes already circulating T3; they just stop further production/conversion.

scenario: A patient with DKA is receiving an IV insulin infusion. The blood glucose is 350 mg/dL, and the serum potassium is 3.1 mEq/L. What is the priority nursing action?

options.B: Hold the insulin and notify the provider for potassium replacement
options.C: Administer an IV push of potassium chloride
options.D: Add 5% Dextrose to the IV fluids

hint: Insulin pushes this electrolyte out of the blood and into the cells.

rationales.correct: Insulin drives potassium back into the cells. If the serum K+ is already low (3.1), giving more insulin will cause profound, lethal hypokalemia. The insulin must be held until the potassium is replaced (usually >3.3 mEq/L).
rationales.incorrect: Increasing insulin is lethal here. Potassium is NEVER given IV push. Dextrose is added when glucose drops below 250 mg/dL, not 350.

scenario: A patient with Hyperosmolar Hyperglycemic State (HHS) has a blood glucose of 900 mg/dL. What is the absolute first step in management?

options.A: Administer an IV bolus of regular insulin
options.B: Infuse 0.9% normal saline aggressively

hint: HHS causes extreme, life-threatening dehydration that precedes the need for glucose control.

rationales.correct: Patients with HHS suffer from profound osmotic diuresis and massive dehydration (often losing up to 10 liters of fluid). The absolute first priority is aggressive IV fluid resuscitation (0.9% NS) to restore perfusion before insulin is given.
rationales.incorrect: Insulin is given AFTER volume is expanding; giving it first drives water into cells, worsening vascular collapse. Bicarbonate is for severe acidosis (DKA), which HHS patients typically lack. SQ insulin is too slow.

scenario: A patient with a new right-sided ischemic stroke is ordered oral medications. What must the nurse do before administering the first pill?

options.B: Crush the pills and mix them in water
options.C: Administer the pills with a straw
options.D: Place the pills on the left side of the patient's mouth

hint: Ensure the airway is safe before giving anything PO.

rationales.correct: All stroke patients must be kept strictly NPO until a formal bedside swallow screening (usually with water) is performed and passed, due to the extraordinarily high risk of dysphagia and silent aspiration.
rationales.incorrect: Crushing pills without assessing swallow risk still causes aspiration. Straws shoot liquid to the back of the throat, increasing aspiration risk. Pill placement doesn't override the need for a swallow screen.

scenario: A patient is having a tonic-clonic seizure that has lasted for 6 minutes (Status Epilepticus). Which medication should the nurse anticipate administering first?

options.B: IV Lorazepam

hint: A fast-acting benzodiazepine is needed to break the seizure.

rationales.correct: Status epilepticus is a medical emergency. The first-line drug of choice to rapidly break the seizure activity is a fast-acting IV benzodiazepine, such as lorazepam or diazepam.
rationales.incorrect: IV Phenytoin is given as a longer-acting maintenance/loading drug after the benzo breaks the seizure. PO meds are impossible during a seizure. Propofol is for induced comas if benzos fail.

scenario: A patient with a spinal cord injury at T5 complains of severe constipation. To establish a neurogenic bowel program, when is the best time for the nurse to administer the daily suppository?

options.B: First thing in the morning before breakfast
options.D: Only when the patient reports the urge to defecate

hint: Take advantage of the body's natural reflex.

rationales.correct: To train a neurogenic bowel, the suppository and digital stimulation should be performed 30 to 60 minutes after a meal (usually breakfast) to utilize the body's natural gastrocolic reflex to stimulate evacuation.
rationales.incorrect: Bedtime or before breakfast ignores the gastrocolic reflex. A patient with a T5 injury has lost the sensory urge to defecate, so waiting for them to report it will result in severe impaction.

scenario: A patient with heart failure is receiving IV furosemide. The patient's BNP level drops from 900 pg/mL to 200 pg/mL over 3 days. What does this indicate?

options.A: The patient is developing acute kidney injury.
options.B: The heart failure exacerbation is worsening.
options.D: The patient is experiencing severe hypokalemia.

hint: BNP is released when the heart ventricles are stretched by fluid overload.

rationales.correct: B-type Natriuretic Peptide (BNP) is a biomarker for ventricular stretch and volume overload in heart failure. A significant decrease in BNP indicates that the fluid volume (preload) has decreased and the therapy is effective.
rationales.incorrect: It indicates improvement, not worsening HF or AKI. While furosemide causes hypokalemia, the BNP lab specifically measures cardiac stretch/fluid status, not potassium.

scenario: A patient presents to the ED with severe chest pain. The ECG shows no ST elevation, but the serum troponin I level is critically elevated. Diagnosis?

options.B: Non-ST-Elevation Myocardial Infarction (NSTEMI)

hint: There is cardiac muscle damage without full-thickness wall ECG changes.

rationales.correct: An NSTEMI involves partial-thickness myocardial damage. It is characterized by elevated cardiac biomarkers (Troponin) without classic ST-segment elevation on the ECG.
rationales.incorrect: STEMI features ST elevation. Unstable angina features chest pain at rest but negative (normal) cardiac biomarkers (no cell death yet). Stable angina is pain with exertion.

scenario: A patient is 4 hours post-insertion of a permanent transvenous pacemaker. The patient suddenly complains of severe, uncontrollable hiccups. What complication does the nurse suspect?

options.B: Lead wire perforation stimulating the diaphragm
options.C: Normal localized muscle twitching from the generator

hint: The phrenic nerve or diaphragm is receiving an electrical shock.

rationales.correct: Uncontrollable hiccups (diaphragmatic pacing) after pacemaker insertion indicate that the pacing lead has perforated the right ventricle and is directly shocking the diaphragm or phrenic nerve. This requires immediate provider notification.
rationales.incorrect: Pacemaker syndrome is loss of AV synchrony. It is not normal. Infection presents with redness/fever, not hiccups.

scenario: A patient with asthma uses a peak flow meter at home. The reading falls into the 'Yellow Zone' (50-80% of personal best). What should the nurse's teaching plan instruct the patient to do?

options.A: Go to the emergency department immediately.
options.B: Continue the daily maintenance medications only.
options.C: Use the short-acting rescue inhaler and reassess in 15-30 minutes.
options.D: Stop all inhalers and rest until breathing improves.

hint: Yellow means 'Caution', requiring intervention but not immediately the hospital.

rationales.correct: The Yellow Zone indicates acute airway narrowing. The patient must use their short-acting beta-agonist (albuterol) rescue inhaler and monitor to see if they return to the Green Zone.
rationales.incorrect: The ED is for the Red Zone (<50%). Continuing only maintenance meds ignores the acute narrowing. Stopping inhalers is dangerous.

scenario: The nurse teaches pursed-lip breathing to a patient with emphysema. What is the primary physiological purpose of this technique?

options.A: To increase the oxygen saturation in the blood
options.B: To strengthen the diaphragm muscle
options.C: To create positive expiratory pressure and prevent airway collapse
options.D: To clear thick mucus from the upper airway

hint: Emphysema destroys the elastic recoil of the alveoli.

rationales.correct: In emphysema, the alveoli lose elastic recoil and collapse during exhalation, trapping CO2. Pursed-lip breathing creates gentle back-pressure (auto-PEEP) that props the airways open, allowing trapped CO2 to escape.
rationales.incorrect: It does not directly pull in more O2; it lets CO2 out. It doesn't strengthen the diaphragm (diaphragmatic breathing does). Huff coughing clears mucus.

scenario: A patient arrives in the ED with sudden pleuritic chest pain and dyspnea. The D-dimer test is significantly elevated. What is the next expected diagnostic test to confirm the diagnosis?

options.B: Transthoracic echocardiogram
options.C: Spiral CT angiogram of the chest

hint: Look for the blood clot in the lungs.

rationales.correct: An elevated D-dimer indicates clot degradation in the body but is not specific. To definitively diagnose a Pulmonary Embolism (PE), a spiral CT angiogram (CTPA) is the gold standard diagnostic test.
rationales.incorrect: An ECG rules out an MI but doesn't diagnose a PE. An echo looks at heart valves/function. An ABG shows hypoxemia but cannot physically locate or confirm the clot.

scenario: A patient with a chest tube is being transported to radiology. The transporter asks if the chest tube should be clamped to prevent leaks during transit. Nurse's best response?

options.A: Yes, clamp it close to the patient's chest.
options.B: Yes, clamp it near the collection chamber.
options.C: No, never clamp a chest tube during transport as it can cause a tension pneumothorax.
options.D: No, but disconnect the tube from the drainage unit.

hint: If air continues to leak from the lung and the exit is blocked, pressure builds up.

rationales.correct: Chest tubes should almost NEVER be clamped, especially during transport. Clamping blocks the exit for air leaking from the lung, quickly causing a fatal tension pneumothorax.
rationales.incorrect: Clamping at any location is strictly prohibited unless briefly changing the system or assessing for a leak. Disconnecting the tube breaks the water seal, causing lung collapse.

scenario: A patient with suspected meningitis presents with a stiff neck and photophobia. The nurse gently flexes the patient's neck to the chest, and the patient involuntarily flexes their hips and knees. What is this sign?

options.B: Positive Brudzinski's sign

hint: B = Brain/Neck.

rationales.correct: Brudzinski's sign is positive when severe neck stiffness (meningeal irritation) causes the patient's hips and knees to flex when the neck is flexed.
rationales.incorrect: Kernig's is inability to straighten the leg when the hip is flexed 90 degrees. Babinski is a foot reflex. Romberg tests balance.

scenario: While removing a patient's central line, the patient suddenly gasps, becomes cyanotic, and grasps their chest. The nurse suspects an air embolism. What is the immediate positioning intervention?

options.B: Supine with legs elevated

hint: Keep the air bubble trapped in the right atrium.

rationales.correct: Placing the patient in the left lateral Trendelenburg position (head down, left side) traps the air embolus in the apex of the right ventricle, preventing it from entering the pulmonary artery and causing a lethal blockage.
rationales.incorrect: High-Fowler's or Right lateral positions allow the air to travel straight to the pulmonary circulation or brain.

scenario: A patient receiving a blood transfusion develops severe shortness of breath, a bounding pulse, distended neck veins, and crackles in the lung bases. Blood pressure is 160/90. Diagnosis?

options.B: Transfusion-Associated Circulatory Overload (TACO)

hint: The heart cannot pump the volume fast enough, causing backup.

rationales.correct: These are classic signs of fluid overload (TACO) due to the blood infusing too rapidly for the patient's cardiovascular system to handle. The rate should be slowed and diuretics administered per order.
rationales.incorrect: Hemolytic causes back pain/shock. TRALI causes severe dyspnea but typically hypotension/fever. Anaphylaxis features wheezing and hives.

scenario: When donning Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) for a patient on Airborne Precautions, what is the correct sequence?

options.A: Gown, N95 mask, Goggles/Face Shield, Gloves
options.B: N95 mask, Goggles, Gown, Gloves

hint: Work from the bottom up, finishing with the hands.

rationales.correct: The correct sequence for donning PPE is Gown, Mask/Respirator, Goggles/Face shield, and finally Gloves.
rationales.incorrect: Gloves are always put on last so they remain completely clean before touching the patient. Masks must go under goggles.

scenario: A nurse is preparing to administer enoxaparin (Lovenox) subcutaneously. The prefilled syringe contains a small air bubble. What should the nurse do?

options.A: Expel the air bubble before injecting the medication
options.B: Leave the air bubble in the syringe and inject it last
options.C: Draw back on the plunger to add more air
options.D: Discard the syringe and obtain a new one

hint: The bubble serves a specific mechanical purpose in the tissue.

rationales.correct: The air bubble in prefilled enoxaparin syringes is designed to be injected last to create an 'air lock' in the subcutaneous tissue, preventing the medication from leaking back out and causing severe bruising.
rationales.incorrect: Expelling the air bubble ensures the drug will leak back up the needle track, causing hematomas.

scenario: A patient has a newly inserted nasogastric (NG) tube. Before administering the first feeding, how MUST the nurse verify placement?

options.A: Aspirate gastric contents and test the pH
options.B: Inject 30 mL of air and auscultate the stomach
options.D: Ask the patient if they can feel the tube in their throat

hint: The gold standard for initial placement is visual confirmation.

rationales.correct: An X-ray is the only definitive, legally and clinically acceptable method to confirm initial NG tube placement before putting any fluids down the tube, preventing fatal pulmonary aspiration.
rationales.incorrect: pH testing is for ongoing daily checks AFTER the initial X-ray. Air auscultation is outdated and highly unreliable.

scenario: A patient is confused and continuously pulling at their IV line. The nurse wishes to apply physical restraints. What must the nurse do FIRST?

options.A: Obtain an order from the physician
options.B: Implement and document alternative, less restrictive measures
options.C: Inform the family that restraints are necessary
options.D: Apply the restraints and get the order within 1 hour

hint: Restraints are legally the absolute last resort.

rationales.correct: Legally and ethically, physical restraints are the last resort. The nurse MUST first attempt and document the failure of less restrictive alternatives (e.g., bed alarms, hiding the IV, a sitter, reorientation).
rationales.incorrect: Getting an order or applying them emergently is only acceptable after alternatives have failed. Family notification happens, but exploring alternatives is the required first step.

scenario: To effectively prevent falls in a geriatric unit, what is the most successful evidence-based nursing intervention?

options.A: Raising all four side rails on the beds
options.B: Conducting purposeful hourly rounding
options.C: Keeping patients in their beds as much as possible

hint: Anticipate the patient's needs before they try to fulfill them independently.

rationales.correct: Purposeful hourly rounding (checking on the 4 P's: Pain, Potty, Positioning, Possessions) proactively meets the patient's needs, significantly reducing the likelihood they will attempt to get out of bed unsafely.
rationales.incorrect: 4 side rails are a restraint and cause worse falls. Bed rest causes deconditioning. Restraints are a last resort.

scenario: When pouring a sterile liquid onto a sterile field, what is the correct technique regarding the bottle's lip?

options.A: Wipe the lip with a sterile alcohol swab before pouring
options.B: Pour a small amount of liquid into a waste receptacle before pouring onto the field
options.C: Rest the lip of the bottle on the sterile basin to ensure accuracy
options.D: Hold the bottle 12 inches above the field to prevent contamination

hint: Wash away the unsterile edge.

rationales.correct: Pouring a small amount of liquid into a waste receptacle ('lipping' the bottle) washes away any airborne contaminants from the rim of the bottle before pouring the liquid onto the sterile field.
rationales.incorrect: Wiping it introduces fibers. Resting the unsterile bottle on the basin contaminates the field. 12 inches is too high and causes splashing (hold it 4-6 inches above).

scenario: The nurse assesses a pressure ulcer that is completely covered in thick, black, leathery eschar. What is the correct staging for this ulcer?

options.B: Stage 4

hint: You cannot stage what you cannot see.

rationales.correct: An ulcer is Unstageable when the true depth of the wound cannot be visualized because it is completely obscured by slough or eschar. It must be debrided before it can be staged.
rationales.incorrect: Stage 3/4 require visualizing the wound bed. A DTI is a purple/maroon intact area of skin, not an open crater with eschar.

scenario: A patient receiving continuous enteral tube feedings develops Dumping Syndrome. Which intervention best prevents this complication?

options.A: Administer the feeding as a rapid bolus
options.B: Administer the feeding at room temperature
options.C: Keep the patient flat during the feeding
options.D: Switch to a hypertonic feeding formula

hint: Avoid extremes in temperature and speed.

rationales.correct: Administering tube feedings at room temperature helps prevent gastric cramping and the rapid emptying associated with Dumping Syndrome.
rationales.incorrect: Rapid boluses CAUSE dumping syndrome. Patients must be upright (30-45 degrees) to prevent aspiration. Hypertonic formulas pull water into the gut, severely worsening dumping syndrome.

scenario: A patient with a Foley catheter is being transferred from the bed to a wheelchair. Where should the nurse attach the urinary drainage bag?

options.A: To the movable armrest of the wheelchair
options.B: To the patient's lap for comfort
options.C: To the IV pole at chest level
options.D: To the immovable frame of the wheelchair below the level of the bladder

hint: Urine must flow downhill and the bag cannot be dropped or yanked.

rationales.correct: To prevent Catheter-Associated Urinary Tract Infections (CAUTI), the bag must ALWAYS remain below the level of the bladder to prevent retrograde flow, and must be attached to an immovable part of the chair/bed to prevent yanking.
rationales.incorrect: Armrests move (yanking the tube). The lap and IV pole place the bag above the bladder, forcing dirty urine back into the patient.

scenario: When performing routine tracheostomy care, what solution is traditionally used to clean the inner cannula?

options.B: Half-strength hydrogen peroxide mixed with sterile normal saline

hint: It bubbles to break up thick crusty mucus without being too caustic.

rationales.correct: Traditionally, the inner cannula is soaked and cleaned in half-strength hydrogen peroxide (to loosen crusts) and then rinsed thoroughly with sterile normal saline to prevent airway irritation.
rationales.incorrect: Alcohol and Chlorhexidine are highly toxic to the respiratory mucosa. Tap water is not sterile and risks pneumonia.

scenario: A patient is receiving 100% oxygen via a non-rebreather mask. The nurse notes that the reservoir bag completely deflates each time the patient inhales. What is the appropriate nursing action?

options.B: Document the finding as normal
options.C: Tighten the mask straps around the patient's head
options.D: Remove the mask and notify the respiratory therapist

hint: The bag must remain partially inflated to provide the high oxygen concentration.

rationales.correct: The reservoir bag on a non-rebreather mask must remain at least 1/3 to 1/2 full during inspiration. If it deflates completely, the flow rate is too low (usually needs to be 10-15 L/min) and must be increased immediately so the patient doesn't suffocate.
rationales.incorrect: It is not normal. Tightening the mask does not fix the flow deficit. Removing the mask deprives the patient of needed oxygen.

scenario: A nurse administers pain medication to a suffering patient despite knowing the medication might cause the patient's blood pressure to drop slightly. The nurse stays to monitor the patient. Which ethical principle is primarily driving this action?

options.B: Beneficence

hint: The duty to do good.

rationales.correct: Beneficence is the active duty to do good and promote the well-being of the patient (relieving suffering). The nurse balances this with nonmaleficence (preventing harm) by monitoring the BP.
rationales.incorrect: Autonomy is patient choice. Justice is fairness. Veracity is truth-telling.

scenario: A patient refuses to let the nurse start an IV. The nurse says, 'If you don't let me do this, I will tie you down and do it anyway.' What legal tort has the nurse committed?

options.B: Assault

hint: Creating the fear of unwanted contact.

rationales.correct: Assault is an intentional threat that creates a reasonable fear of imminent, harmful, or offensive contact. Because the nurse only threatened the patient and didn't touch them yet, it is assault.
rationales.incorrect: Battery is the actual physical touching without consent. False imprisonment is actually tying them down. Malpractice is professional negligence.

scenario: A patient is prescribed a trough level for an IV antibiotic scheduled for 1000. When should the nurse draw the blood sample?
hint: Right before the next dose.

rationales.correct: Trough levels measure the lowest concentration of the drug and must be drawn exactly 15 to 30 minutes immediately before the next scheduled dose (between 0930 and 0945).
rationales.incorrect: 1030 and 1100 measure peak levels. 0900 is too early.

scenario: A patient is on a PCA (Patient-Controlled Analgesia) pump. The patient complains they are still in pain because the machine 'locks them out.' What is the nurse's best action?

options.A: Teach the family member to press the button for the patient when they are asleep
options.B: Perform a comprehensive pain assessment and notify the provider
options.C: Explain that they just have to tolerate the pain until the lockout is over
options.D: Manually override the lockout to give an extra dose

hint: The current regimen is not working.

rationales.correct: If the patient is hitting the lockout and still in severe pain, the current PCA settings (basal rate or bolus dose) are inadequate. The nurse must assess the pain and call the provider for a dose adjustment.
rationales.incorrect: PCA by proxy (family pressing) is strictly forbidden and lethal. Ignoring the pain is unethical. Nurses cannot manually override lockouts without a medical order.

scenario: A patient who is a Jehovah's Witness experiences severe hemorrhagic shock. They refuse packed RBCs. Which alternative therapy should the nurse anticipate administering?

options.B: Platelets

hint: Stimulate the body to make its own blood.

rationales.correct: Jehovah's Witnesses refuse primary blood components (whole blood, RBCs, plasma, platelets). Synthetic erythropoietin (Epoetin alfa), iron, and volume expanders (normal saline/Dextran) are acceptable alternatives to stimulate internal RBC production.
rationales.incorrect: FFP, platelets, and whole blood are all strictly forbidden in the Jehovah's Witness faith.

scenario: A dying patient is exhibiting periods of deep, rapid breathing followed by periods of apnea lasting 20 seconds. How should the nurse document this breathing pattern?

options.B: Cheyne-Stokes respirations

hint: A waxing and waning pattern leading to apnea.

rationales.correct: Cheyne-Stokes respirations are characterized by a rhythmic crescendo and decrescendo of breathing depth/rate followed by periods of apnea. It is a classic sign of impending death or severe brain injury.
rationales.incorrect: Kussmaul is deep/rapid (DKA). Biot's is completely irregular/chaotic. Agonal is gasping, labored, isolated breaths during cardiac arrest.

scenario: Four patients in the ED need immediate attention. Based on the ABCs, who does the nurse see FIRST?

options.A: A patient with asthma reporting dyspnea and SpO2 92%
options.B: A patient with anaphylaxis exhibiting high-pitched inspiratory stridor
options.C: A patient with a laceration and active arterial bleeding
options.D: A patient with a head injury who is entirely unresponsive

hint: Airway obstruction beats breathing and circulation.

rationales.correct: Stridor indicates a near-complete physical obstruction of the upper Airway. According to the ABCs (Airway, Breathing, Circulation), resolving the obstructed airway is the absolute highest priority.
rationales.incorrect: Asthma (Breathing) is secondary to a blocked Airway. Arterial bleeding (Circulation) is secondary to Airway. Unresponsiveness (Neurological) is assessed after ABCs are secured.

scenario: A 5-year-old is diagnosed with Kawasaki disease. The provider orders IVIG and high-dose aspirin. The parents question the aspirin due to the risk of Reye's syndrome. What is the nurse's best response?

options.A: Aspirin is safe for children if given with IV fluids.
options.B: Aspirin is necessary here to prevent severe inflammation and damage to the coronary arteries.
options.C: We only use it for 24 hours to break the fever.
options.D: Reye's syndrome only occurs in infants under 1 year old.

hint: Protect the heart.

rationales.correct: Kawasaki disease causes lethal systemic vasculitis. The benefit of high-dose aspirin (antiplatelet/anti-inflammatory cardioprotection to prevent aneurysms) vastly outweighs the rare risk of Reye's syndrome.
rationales.incorrect: Aspirin is generally NOT safe for children. It is used for weeks, not 24 hours. Reye's syndrome affects all children, not just infants.

scenario: A 10-month-old with Tetralogy of Fallot becomes deeply cyanotic and irritable during a blood draw. First action?

options.B: Place the infant in the knee-chest position

hint: Increase systemic resistance.

rationales.correct: Placint the infant in a knee-chest position kinks the femoral arteries, instantly increasing systemic vascular resistance and forcing blood back through the pulmonary artery for oxygenation (resolving the 'Tet spell').
rationales.incorrect: Oxygen and morphine are steps 2 and 3. A Code Blue is for pulseless arrest.

scenario: A 2-year-old child presents with a barking, seal-like cough and stridor at night. What is the immediate treatment of choice?

options.B: IV antibiotics

hint: Shrink the subglottic edema rapidly.

rationales.correct: The child has viral croup. Nebulized racemic epinephrine acts quickly to cause mucosal vasoconstriction, reducing subglottic edema and relieving the stridor.
rationales.incorrect: Antibiotics don't treat viruses. Intubation is for epiglottitis (unless croup is refractory). Albuterol treats lower airway spasms (asthma), not upper airway swelling.

scenario: A child presents to the ED with sudden high fever, drooling, severe sore throat, and a tripod posture. What must the nurse strictly AVOID?

options.B: Administering blow-by oxygen
options.C: Inserting a tongue depressor to inspect the throat
options.D: Keeping the child on the parent's lap

hint: Do not agitate the inflamed airway.

rationales.correct: The child has acute epiglottitis. Inserting anything into the mouth (tongue depressor, swab) can trigger reflex laryngospasm, causing instant, total airway obstruction and death.
rationales.incorrect: Intubation prep, oxygen, and keeping the child calm are all necessary and correct interventions.

scenario: A toddler is diagnosed with Cystic Fibrosis. When should the nurse instruct the parents to administer pancreatic enzymes (pancrelipase)?

options.B: Crushed and dissolved in a warm drink
options.C: Immediately before or during every meal and snack
options.D: Only when the child has fatty, foul-smelling stools

hint: The enzymes must mix with the food in the gut.

rationales.correct: CF patients require pancreatic enzymes to digest food. The enzymes must be taken immediately before or during every meal and snack to be effective.
rationales.incorrect: After meals renders them useless. Heat and crushing destroy the enteric-coated enzymes. They are required daily, not PRN.

scenario: A 9-month-old is admitted with severe colicky abdominal pain, drawing knees to the chest, and vomiting. The nurse notes 'currant jelly' stool. Diagnosis?

options.B: Hirschsprung's disease

hint: Telescoping bowel shears the mucosa.

rationales.correct: Intussusception (telescoping of the bowel) causes colicky pain and mucosal sloughing, resulting in stools mixed with blood and mucus (currant jelly).
rationales.incorrect: Pyloric stenosis features projectile vomiting. Hirschsprung's features chronic constipation. Appendicitis features constant RLQ pain.

scenario: A 4-week-old infant is constantly hungry and experiences forceful, non-bilious projectile vomiting after every feeding. Assessment reveals an olive-shaped mass in the RUQ. Diagnosis?

options.B: Pyloric stenosis

hint: The exit from the stomach is blocked by a hypertrophied muscle.

rationales.correct: Pyloric stenosis is hypertrophy of the pyloric sphincter. It traps food in the stomach, leading to forceful projectile vomiting. The hypertrophied muscle can be felt as an olive-shaped mass.
rationales.incorrect: Intussusception has currant jelly stools. GERD is passive spit-up. Biliary atresia features jaundice and clay-colored stools.

scenario: A newborn has not passed meconium in 48 hours and has abdominal distention. A rectal biopsy shows an absence of ganglion cells in the colon. Diagnosis?

options.B: Celiac disease

hint: Aganglionic megacolon.

rationales.correct: Hirschsprung's disease involves a lack of nerve cells (ganglion cells) in the lower colon. This halts peristalsis, causing stool to back up and a classic failure to pass meconium.
rationales.incorrect: Meconium ileus is related to CF. Celiac is gluten intolerance. NEC is bowel necrosis in premature infants.

scenario: A child is diagnosed with Celiac disease. The nurse teaches the parents to adhere strictly to the BROW acronym. What does BROW stand for?

options.B: Barley, Rye, Oats, Wheat

hint: These grains contain gluten or are highly cross-contaminated.

rationales.correct: Celiac disease requires a lifelong gluten-free diet. The BROW acronym specifies the grains to avoid: Barley, Rye, Oats (due to heavy cross-contamination), and Wheat.
rationales.incorrect: Rice, beans, corn, and fruits are naturally gluten-free and safe to consume.

scenario: A child with sickle cell anemia is admitted with a vaso-occlusive crisis. What is the absolute priority nursing intervention?

options.A: Apply cold compresses to the joints
options.B: Administer aggressive IV hydration

hint: Decrease the blood viscosity to stop the clumping.

rationales.correct: Aggressive IV hydration (hemodilution) thins the viscous blood, allowing sickled cells to flow past blockages, reversing the ischemia and severe pain.
rationales.incorrect: Cold causes vasoconstriction (worsening blockages). ROM increases O2 demand (worsening pain). Antibiotics are secondary to hydration and pain control.

scenario: A 9-year-old with Hemophilia A sustains a knee injury. The joint is swelling rapidly. What is the first medical intervention required?

options.B: Administer IV Factor VIII replacement

hint: Replace what the blood is missing.

rationales.correct: Hemophilia A is a Factor VIII deficiency. The absolute first step for active bleeding (hemarthrosis) is replacing the missing clotting factor via IV to stop the internal hemorrhage.
rationales.incorrect: Aspiration without clotting factor will cause fatal bleeding. Heat worsens bleeding (use ice). Ibuprofen blocks platelets and is contraindicated.

scenario: A nurse is assessing a child with suspected meningitis. The nurse gently flexes the child's neck toward the chest, and the child involuntarily flexes the hips and knees. This is documented as:

options.B: Positive Brudzinski's sign

hint: B = Brain/Neck.

rationales.correct: Brudzinski's sign is a classic indicator of meningeal irritation. Flexing the neck causes pain, leading to an involuntary reflexive flexion of the hips and knees.
rationales.incorrect: Kernig's is inability to straighten the knee when the hip is flexed 90 degrees. Babinski is a foot reflex. Ortolani checks for hip dysplasia.

scenario: A 4-month-old infant is admitted with hydrocephalus. Which physical assessment finding is most indicative of increased intracranial pressure in an infant?

options.B: Bulging, tense anterior fontanel

hint: The infant skull has open spaces to accommodate pressure.

rationales.correct: Because an infant's cranial sutures are not fused, increased ICP causes the fontanels (especially the anterior) to bulge outward and feel tense and firm.
rationales.incorrect: A sunken fontanel indicates dehydration. Kernig's is unreliable in infants. A 4-month-old cannot complain of a headache.

scenario: A 5-year-old child attempts to stand by pushing his hands against his knees and 'walking' his hands up his thighs. The nurse documents Gowers' sign. What disease is this a hallmark of?

options.B: Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy (DMD)

hint: Progressive, ascending muscle weakness.

rationales.correct: Gowers' sign indicates severe proximal muscle weakness of the pelvic girdle, a classic hallmark of Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy.
rationales.incorrect: CP involves spasticity. Spina bifida involves paralysis below a spinal lesion. Rheumatic fever involves joint pain and cardiac issues.

scenario: An infant is diagnosed with Developmental Dysplasia of the Hip (DDH) and fitted with a Pavlik harness. What is a critical teaching point for the parents?

options.A: Remove the harness for 2 hours daily to stretch the hips
options.B: Place the harness directly against the baby's bare skin
options.C: Place the baby's diaper and a light shirt under the harness straps
options.D: Adjust the straps daily as the baby grows

hint: Protect the skin from friction while maintaining continuous joint alignment.

rationales.correct: The Pavlik harness must be worn continuously. To prevent severe skin breakdown, parents should place a light shirt and the diaper UNDER the harness straps.
rationales.incorrect: Removing it defeats the treatment. Direct skin contact causes friction burns. Only the provider should adjust the straps to prevent nerve damage.

scenario: A 12-month-old child is scheduled for routine immunizations. Which vaccine is an example of a LIVE attenuated virus and is typically given at this age?

options.B: MMR (Measles, Mumps, Rubella)

hint: Live vaccines are delayed until the maternal antibodies fade at 1 year.

rationales.correct: MMR and Varicella are live attenuated vaccines. They are typically administered for the first time at 12-15 months of age because maternal antibodies protect the child before then and would kill the live vaccine virus.
rationales.incorrect: DTaP, IPV, and Hep B are inactivated (killed or recombinant) vaccines given earlier in infancy (2, 4, 6 months).

scenario: To drastically reduce the risk of Sudden Infant Death Syndrome (SIDS), what is the most important instruction the nurse must give new parents?

options.A: Always place the infant on their back (supine) to sleep
options.B: Keep the room very warm and use heavy blankets
options.C: Place the infant on their stomach (prone) to prevent choking on spit-up
options.D: Use bumper pads in the crib to prevent head injury

hint: Back to sleep.

rationales.correct: The 'Back to Sleep' campaign established that the supine position is the single most effective intervention to prevent SIDS.
rationales.incorrect: Overheating is a major SIDS risk factor. Prone positioning vastly increases SIDS risk. Bumper pads, pillows, and soft bedding are suffocation hazards and are strictly prohibited.

scenario: A 2-year-old is brought to the ED with a fractured humerus. The X-ray reveals a spiral fracture. The parents' explanation of the injury is inconsistent. What must the nurse suspect and do?

options.A: Suspect osteogenesis imperfecta and draw calcium levels
options.B: Suspect child abuse and report it to child protective services immediately
options.C: Suspect a simple fall and cast the arm
options.D: Confront the parents directly about lying

hint: This type of fracture requires a violent twisting motion.

rationales.correct: Spiral fractures (caused by twisting force) in toddlers, especially with inconsistent stories, are a massive red flag for physical abuse. Nurses are mandatory reporters and must report suspicions immediately to CPS.
rationales.incorrect: Osteogenesis imperfecta features multiple fractures at different healing stages, but spiral is classic for abuse. Confronting parents is dangerous and can cause them to flee with the child.

scenario: A 3-year-old child sustains burns. When calculating the Total Body Surface Area (TBSA) using the pediatric Rule of Nines, what major anatomical difference must the nurse account for compared to an adult?

options.A: The child's arms account for a larger percentage
options.B: The child's head accounts for a much larger percentage (18%) than an adult's (9%)
options.C: The child's torso accounts for less percentage
options.D: The child's legs account for a larger percentage

hint: Babies have proportionally massive heads.

rationales.correct: In children, the head makes up a much larger proportion of the body surface area (18% in infants/toddlers compared to 9% in adults). The legs account for proportionally less (14% each vs 18% in adults).
rationales.incorrect: Arms and torso percentages remain largely the same between adults and children. Legs account for LESS, not more.

scenario: A child with Type 1 Diabetes is sick with a stomach virus and is not eating. What 'Sick Day Rule' is crucial for the parents to follow?

options.A: Stop administering insulin until the child starts eating again
options.B: Continue taking insulin and monitor blood glucose and ketones frequently
options.C: Switch from insulin injections to oral diabetic pills temporarily
options.D: Restrict all fluids to prevent further vomiting

hint: Infection causes cortisol release, which spikes blood sugar.

rationales.correct: Infection and stress trigger counter-regulatory hormones (cortisol) that spike blood sugar, risking DKA. Insulin MUST be continued even if the child isn't eating, and BG/ketones must be checked every 3-4 hours.
rationales.incorrect: Stopping insulin is the #1 cause of DKA in sick kids. Type 1 diabetics cannot use oral pills. Fluids must be encouraged to clear ketones.

scenario: A patient with severe preeclampsia is receiving an IV magnesium sulfate infusion. Assessment reveals a respiratory rate of 10 breaths/min, oxygen saturation 90%, and absent deep tendon reflexes. First action?

options.B: Stop the magnesium sulfate infusion immediately

hint: Remove the toxic agent before giving the antidote.

rationales.correct: The patient has magnesium toxicity (respiratory depression, absent reflexes). The absolute first action is to stop the offending medication infusion.
rationales.incorrect: Calcium gluconate is the antidote and is given IMMEDIATELY AFTER stopping the drip. Calling the MD and checking FHR delay the immediate life-saving action.

scenario: A pregnant patient with preeclampsia begins to have a tonic-clonic seizure (Eclampsia). What is the nurse's first priority?

options.B: Insert a padded tongue blade into her mouth
options.C: Turn the patient to her side and protect the airway
options.D: Prepare for an emergency cesarean section

hint: Airway and safety always come first during a seizure.

rationales.correct: During an eclamptic seizure, the absolute priority is maternal safety and airway patency. Turning the patient on her side prevents aspiration of secretions/emesis.
rationales.incorrect: Magnesium is given after the airway is secure. Never put anything in a seizing patient's mouth. C-section prep happens after the mother is stabilized.

scenario: A patient with preeclampsia complains of severe right upper quadrant (RUQ) pain. Laboratory results show elevated AST/ALT, hemolysis, and a platelet count of 45,000. Diagnosis?

options.B: HELLP syndrome

hint: Hemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets.

rationales.correct: HELLP syndrome is a severe, life-threatening variant of preeclampsia. It is characterized by Hemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, and Low Platelets. RUQ pain indicates a swelling, ischemic liver.
rationales.incorrect: Gestational diabetes involves glucose. While cholecystitis causes RUQ pain, it doesn't cause this specific triad of lab abnormalities. This is a lethal emergency, not normal.

scenario: A patient at 33 weeks gestation presents with painless, bright red vaginal bleeding. Which procedure is strictly contraindicated?

options.B: Digital vaginal examination

hint: The placenta may be covering the cervical opening.

rationales.correct: Painless bright red bleeding is the hallmark of placenta previa. A digital vaginal exam can puncture the placenta, causing massive, lethal hemorrhage. Exams are strictly contraindicated.
rationales.incorrect: Ultrasound, IV access, and fetal monitoring are critical, safe interventions.

scenario: A patient at 38 weeks gestation presents with sudden, severe, constant abdominal pain, a rigid board-like abdomen, and dark red bleeding. Diagnosis?

options.B: Placental abruption (Abruptio placentae)

hint: Pain + Rigid abdomen + Dark bleeding = Separation.

rationales.correct: Placental abruption occurs when the placenta detaches prematurely, trapping blood behind it. This causes intense abdominal rigidity, severe pain, and dark red bleeding.
rationales.incorrect: Previa is painless with bright red blood. Vasa previa is painless fetal bleeding. Uterine rupture features a loss of contractions.

scenario: A laboring patient experiences a gush of fluid, and the fetal heart rate suddenly drops to 70 bpm. The nurse feels a pulsating cord in the vagina. What is the immediate position for the patient?

options.B: High-Fowler's

hint: Use gravity to pull the baby off the cord.

rationales.correct: This is a prolapsed umbilical cord. The knee-chest position (or extreme Trendelenburg) uses gravity to shift the fetus back into the uterus, relieving compression on the umbilical cord.
rationales.incorrect: Supine, High-Fowler's, and Lithotomy all push the fetus downward, worsening the compression and suffocating the baby.

scenario: The fetal monitor shows late decelerations during contractions. What is the physiological cause of this pattern?

options.B: Umbilical cord compression

hint: VEAL CHOP.

rationales.correct: Late decelerations (drops in FHR that start after the peak of a contraction) indicate uteroplacental insufficiency—the fetus is not getting enough oxygen from the placenta. This is an ominous sign.
rationales.incorrect: Head compression = Early decels. Cord compression = Variable decels. Sleep cycles = decreased variability.

scenario: The fetal monitor shows variable decelerations. What is the most appropriate first-line nursing intervention?

options.A: Turn the patient to her left or right side
options.B: Administer IV ephedrine

hint: Variable decelerations mean cord compression.

rationales.correct: Variable decelerations are caused by umbilical cord compression. Simply changing the maternal position often shifts the fetus off the cord, instantly resolving the deceleration.
rationales.incorrect: Ephedrine is for hypotension. C-section is premature. Oxygen via face mask (not NC) is a secondary step if positioning fails.

scenario: Thirty minutes after a vaginal delivery, a patient is bleeding heavily. The nurse palpates a soft, boggy fundus at the umbilicus. What is the immediate first action?

options.B: Empty the patient's bladder

hint: The uterus is midline but lacks tone.

rationales.correct: The uterus is boggy but midline. The immediate first action for uterine atony is vigorous fundal massage to physically stimulate the muscle to contract and clamp down on the bleeding vessels.
rationales.incorrect: Meds are given if massage fails. The bladder isn't the primary issue if the fundus is perfectly midline. Calling the provider delays immediate physical intervention.

scenario: A patient with postpartum hemorrhage is prescribed methylergonovine (Methergine). Which condition in the patient's history is an absolute contraindication?

options.B: Hypertension

hint: This medication acts by causing intense systemic vasoconstriction.

rationales.correct: Methylergonovine is a potent vasoconstrictor. It is strictly contraindicated in patients with hypertension or preeclampsia because it can precipitate a lethal hypertensive crisis or stroke.
rationales.incorrect: Asthma is a contraindication for Carboprost (Hemabate). Diabetes and C-sections are not contraindications.

scenario: A mother who is Rh-negative delivers an Rh-positive infant. To prevent isoimmunization, the nurse must administer RhoGAM within what timeframe?

options.B: Within 72 hours of birth

hint: Stop the mother's immune system before it can finalize antibody production.

rationales.correct: RhoGAM must be administered to an Rh-negative mother within 72 hours of delivering an Rh-positive infant to prevent the formation of antibodies that would destroy future Rh-positive fetuses.
rationales.incorrect: While 24 hours or before discharge is acceptable, the strict protocol limit is 72 hours. 6 weeks is far too late.

scenario: A patient with a suspected unruptured ectopic pregnancy complains of sudden, sharp, severe pain in her right shoulder. What does this signify?

options.B: Anxiety and hyperventilation
options.C: The fallopian tube has ruptured, causing internal bleeding

hint: Kehr's sign.

rationales.correct: Sudden referred shoulder pain (Kehr's sign) indicates the ectopic pregnancy has ruptured. Blood is pooling under the diaphragm, irritating the phrenic nerve. This is a catastrophic surgical emergency.
rationales.incorrect: It is a physical, anatomical referral of pain from hemorrhage, not normal strain, anxiety, or a heart attack.

scenario: A patient undergoes a D&C for a hydatidiform mole (molar pregnancy). The nurse provides discharge teaching. Which patient statement indicates understanding?

options.A: I should try to get pregnant again quickly to heal my uterus.
options.B: I must use reliable birth control to prevent pregnancy for at least 1 year.
options.C: I will need a hysterectomy to prevent cancer.
options.D: This means I can never have children in the future.

hint: Pregnancy hormones will mask the growth of a specific cancer.

rationales.correct: Molar pregnancies carry a high risk of developing into choriocarcinoma. hCG levels must be monitored for 1 year. Pregnancy raises hCG naturally, masking cancer, making strict contraception mandatory.
rationales.incorrect: Getting pregnant ruins the hCG monitoring. Hysterectomy is only for confirmed cancer. They can have children after the 1-year monitoring period.

scenario: A patient with gestational diabetes delivers a 10 lb macrosomic infant. At 1 hour of life, the infant is jittery, twitching, and has a weak cry. Priority action?

options.A: Assess the infant's blood glucose level
options.B: Wrap the infant in warm blankets

hint: The maternal glucose supply is gone, but the baby's insulin is still high.

rationales.correct: Infants of diabetic mothers are at high risk for profound rebound hypoglycemia. Jitteriness and tremors are classic signs requiring an immediate heel stick to check blood sugar.
rationales.incorrect: Warming addresses cold stress but ignores the lethal glucose drop. Calcium treats hypocalcemia. Phenobarbital treats seizures, but checking glucose is the diagnostic first step.

scenario: A patient at 10 weeks gestation presents with severe, intractable vomiting (Hyperemesis Gravidarum), a 5 lb weight loss, and extreme weakness. Which lab finding is expected?

options.B: Hypokalemia and ketonuria

hint: Vomiting loses potassium, and starvation burns fat.

rationales.correct: Severe vomiting causes massive loss of potassium (hypokalemia) and metabolic alkalosis. The inability to eat causes starvation, leading the body to break down fat, producing ketones in the urine.
rationales.incorrect: Vomiting does not cause hyperkalemia. Calcium is generally unaffected. Proteinuria is a sign of preeclampsia (which occurs >20 weeks).

scenario: A pregnant patient at 36 weeks tests positive for Group B Streptococcus (GBS). What is the mandatory intervention?

options.B: Administer IV antibiotics to the mother during labor
options.D: Administer IV antibiotics to the infant after birth

hint: Protect the baby as they pass through the birth canal.

rationales.correct: GBS is harmless to the mother but lethal to the infant. IV antibiotics (penicillin) are given during labor to ensure the baby is exposed to the drug while passing through the birth canal.
rationales.incorrect: Oral antibiotics prior to labor fail because the bacteria quickly repopulate. C-sections are not required. Treating the baby after birth is too late to prevent exposure.

scenario: A breastfeeding mother is diagnosed with mastitis in her right breast. What should the nurse teach her about feeding her infant?

options.A: Stop breastfeeding immediately to prevent infecting the infant
options.B: Pump the right breast and throw the milk away
options.C: Continue feeding frequently, starting on the unaffected breast to trigger let-down
options.D: Wean the baby to formula until the antibiotics are finished

hint: Emptying the breast cures the infection; the milk is safe.

rationales.correct: Mastitis requires frequent, complete emptying of the breast. The milk is not infected and is safe for the infant. Starting on the unaffected breast triggers the let-down reflex painlessly, making it easier to empty the painful breast.
rationales.incorrect: Stopping feeding causes severe engorgement and abscess formation. Discarding milk is unnecessary.

scenario: A patient's last menstrual period (LMP) began on March 15. Using Naegele's rule, what is her estimated date of delivery (EDD)?

options.B: December 8 of the same year

hint: Subtract 3 months, add 7 days.

rationales.correct: Naegele's rule: Subtract 3 months from March 15 (December 15), add 7 days (December 22). The EDD is December 22.
rationales.incorrect: The other options represent math errors using the formula.

scenario: A pregnant patient has a history of one miscarriage at 12 weeks, twins born at 35 weeks (both living), and one full-term singleton birth (living). What is her GTPAL?
hint: Twins count as one pregnancy and one delivery event, but two living children.

rationales.correct: Gravida = 4 (current + term + preterm + abortion). Term = 1. Preterm = 1 (twins are ONE delivery). Abortion = 1. Living = 3. GTPAL: 4-1-1-1-3.
rationales.incorrect: Counting twins as two parities (P) is a common error. They are one birth event.

scenario: At 1 minute of life, a newborn has a heart rate of 120, a lusty cry, active flexion, pulls away from suction, and has acrocyanosis (blue hands/feet). APGAR score?
hint: Acrocyanosis loses one point.

rationales.correct: HR > 100 (2). Cry strong (2). Active flexion (2). Reflex irritability (2). Color acrocyanosis (1). Total = 9.
rationales.incorrect: A perfect 10 requires a completely pink body. The baby is not compromised enough for an 8 or 7.

scenario: A mass casualty incident occurs. A triage nurse assesses a patient with a piece of shrapnel in the neck causing an active arterial bleed and respiratory distress. Which tag is applied?

options.B: Yellow (Urgent)

hint: Airway and severe hemorrhage are immediate threats to life.

rationales.correct: Active arterial bleeding and airway compromise are immediately life-threatening but highly treatable with pressure and airway control. This earns a Red (Immediate) tag.
rationales.incorrect: Yellow is delayed care. Green is walking wounded. Black is dead or unsalvageable (e.g., massive head trauma with GCS 3).

scenario: A triage nurse assesses a disaster victim who has massive open head trauma, exposed brain matter, agonal respirations, and absent radial pulses. Which tag is assigned?

options.B: Yellow

hint: Resources cannot be spent on those who cannot survive in a disaster.

rationales.correct: In mass casualty triage, a patient with catastrophic, non-survivable injuries is tagged Black (Expectant). Resources must be saved for those with survivable injuries.
rationales.incorrect: Red is for life-threatening but salvageable injuries. Yellow is stable. Green is minor.

scenario: When performing START triage at a disaster, what is the very first instruction the nurse shouts to the crowd?

options.A: Who needs a doctor right now?
options.B: Everyone who can walk, move to the green tarp over there!
options.C: Everyone lie still and wait for assessment!

hint: Clear the easiest patients out of the way immediately.

rationales.correct: The first step of START triage is to clear the 'walking wounded'. By having them walk to a designated area, they are automatically tagged Green, clearing the scene to focus on the critically injured on the ground.
rationales.incorrect: Asking who needs a doctor causes chaos. Having people lie still traps healthy people in the hazard zone.

scenario: Which task can the Registered Nurse (RN) safely delegate to a Licensed Practical Nurse (LPN)?

options.A: Perform the admission assessment on a new patient
options.B: Perform a sterile dressing change on a central venous catheter
options.C: Administer an IV push dose of morphine
options.D: Create the initial nursing care plan for a diabetic patient

hint: LPNs handle complex, sterile, but predictable procedures on stable patients.

rationales.correct: Sterile dressing changes (including central lines on stable patients) are procedural tasks well within the LPN scope of practice.
rationales.incorrect: Admission assessments, IV push narcotics, and initial care planning are strictly restricted to the RN scope of practice.

scenario: A nurse is delegating tasks to an Unlicensed Assistive Personnel (UAP). Which task is appropriate?

options.A: Providing the initial feeding to a stroke patient with new dysphagia
options.B: Assessing a patient's pain level after giving Tylenol
options.C: Assisting a stable patient with routine bathing and toileting
options.D: Educating a patient on how to use an incentive spirometer

hint: UAPs perform standard Activities of Daily Living (ADLs).

rationales.correct: Assisting with ADLs (bathing, toileting, ambulating stable patients) is the primary role of the UAP.
rationales.incorrect: Initial feeding for dysphagia requires RN assessment. Assessing pain and educating are strictly RN scopes of practice.

scenario: A registered nurse cannot delegate tasks that involve the 'EAT' acronym. What does 'EAT' stand for in nursing delegation?

options.B: Emergencies, Administration of drugs, Transfers

hint: These require the critical thinking of the nursing process.

rationales.correct: EAT stands for Evaluate, Assess, and Teach. These are the core components of the nursing process requiring independent clinical judgment and can NEVER be delegated to an LPN or UAP.
rationales.incorrect: UAPs handle Eating, Ambulation, and Toileting. Administration of certain drugs is allowed for LPNs.

scenario: A nurse receives report on 4 patients. Who should be assessed FIRST?

options.A: A pt 24 hours post-thyroidectomy reporting stridor and a tight neck
options.B: A pt 2 days post-appendectomy reporting 8/10 incision pain
options.C: A pt with COPD who has an SpO2 of 89% on room air
options.D: A pt with diabetes whose fasting blood sugar is 175 mg/dL

hint: The airway is physically closing.

rationales.correct: Stridor and neck tightness post-thyroidectomy indicate a rapidly expanding hematoma crushing the trachea. This is an immediate, lethal airway obstruction.
rationales.incorrect: Post-op pain is important but not life-threatening. 89% is normal for COPD. A blood sugar of 175 is elevated but stable.

scenario: A patient is admitted with suspected sepsis. The provider orders a serum lactate level, blood cultures, IV broad-spectrum antibiotics, and a normal saline bolus. First action?

options.B: Draw blood cultures

hint: Identify the pathogen before killing it.

rationales.correct: Blood cultures MUST be drawn before administering broad-spectrum antibiotics, so the antibiotics do not sterilize the blood sample and obscure the specific pathogen identification.
rationales.incorrect: Antibiotics are given immediately AFTER cultures. Fluids run concurrently. Oxygen is given if hypoxic.

scenario: A diabetic patient reports feeling shaky and sweaty. The UAP checks the blood glucose and reports it is 45 mg/dL. The patient is awake and talking. What is the nurse's priority action?

options.B: Provide 4 oz of orange juice immediately
options.D: Ask the UAP to recheck the blood sugar in 15 minutes

hint: The patient is symptomatic but able to safely swallow.

rationales.correct: For a conscious, alert hypoglycemic patient, 15 grams of fast-acting simple carbs (4 oz juice) is the initial, safest treatment.
rationales.incorrect: Glucagon and D50 are for unconscious/NPO patients. Waiting 15 minutes allows brain death.

scenario: A float nurse from the pediatric unit is assigned to the adult Med-Surg unit. Which patient is the safest assignment?

options.A: A pt with a continuous IV heparin drip for a pulmonary embolism
options.B: A pt admitted 1 hour ago with diabetic ketoacidosis
options.C: A pt 2 days post-op from an appendectomy waiting to be discharged
options.D: A pt with a tracheostomy requiring frequent deep suctioning

hint: Give the float nurse the most stable, predictable patient.

rationales.correct: A stable post-op patient awaiting discharge requires standard basic nursing care (vitals, ambulation, basic teaching) and is the safest, most predictable assignment.
rationales.incorrect: Heparin drips, DKA, and complex artificial airways require specialized adult critical care/med-surg assessment skills.

scenario: Two nurses have a conflict over scheduling. They sit down and create a schedule where both nurses compromise to find a creative solution that fully satisfies both of their needs. What conflict resolution style is this?

options.B: Competing

hint: A true win-win solution.

rationales.correct: Collaborating is a 'win-win' conflict resolution style where parties work together to find a creative solution that fully satisfies the concerns of both sides.
rationales.incorrect: Avoiding is ignoring it. Competing is a win-lose (one forces their way). Accommodating is lose-win (one entirely gives up their needs).

scenario: A hospital unit has seen a 25% increase in medication errors. The manager initiates a process to identify the systemic reasons for the failures, rather than blaming individuals. What is this process called?

options.B: Peer review

hint: Finding the fundamental breakdown in the system.

rationales.correct: Root Cause Analysis (RCA) is a retrospective quality improvement process used to identify systemic, underlying system failures that lead to adverse events, rather than assigning individual blame.
rationales.incorrect: Peer review evaluates individual performance. Risk avoidance is not a QI process. Disciplinary panels focus on punitive action.

scenario: A competent, alert patient demands to leave the hospital Against Medical Advice (AMA) while an IV is still in place. What is the nurse's legal requirement?

options.A: Call security to restrain the patient
options.B: Remove the IV catheter immediately before they can walk out
options.C: Explain the medical risks of leaving without completing treatment
options.D: Refuse to provide the AMA form until the doctor arrives

hint: Ensure the patient is making an informed decision before they walk out.

rationales.correct: Before a patient leaves AMA, the nurse/provider must ensure they fully comprehend the medical risks of leaving. Only after this education (and asking them to sign the form) is the IV removed.
rationales.incorrect: Restraining them is false imprisonment. Removing the IV is done eventually, but education is first. You cannot hold them hostage waiting for a doctor.

scenario: A physician asks the nurse to obtain informed consent for a surgical procedure. What is the nurse's explicit legal role in this process?

options.A: Explaining the risks and benefits of the surgery to the patient
options.B: Ensuring the patient understands the surgical alternatives
options.C: Witnessing that the patient signed voluntarily and is competent
options.D: Determining if the patient truly needs the surgery

hint: The nurse acts as an advocate and a legal observer.

rationales.correct: The nurse's legal role in informed consent is purely to act as a witness that the patient signed voluntarily, was competent to sign, and that the signature is authentic.
rationales.incorrect: Explaining risks, benefits, and alternatives is the legal responsibility of the provider performing the procedure.

scenario: A patient asks the nurse, 'Am I dying? The doctor won't tell me.' The nurse knows the prognosis is terminal. The nurse replies, 'Yes, the treatments are no longer working.' Which ethical principle is the nurse upholding?

options.B: Veracity

hint: The obligation to tell the truth.

rationales.correct: Veracity is the ethical principle of truth-telling. The nurse is obligated to provide honest, accurate information to the patient.
rationales.incorrect: Beneficence is doing good. Justice is fairness in care delivery. Nonmaleficence is doing no harm.

scenario: A nurse smells alcohol on a coworker's breath, and the coworker is slurring their words. What is the nurse's ethical and legal duty?

options.A: Confront the nurse privately and ask them to go home
options.B: Immediately report the suspicion to the nursing supervisor
options.C: Wait to see if the nurse makes a documented medication error
options.D: Take over their patients and let them sleep in the breakroom

hint: Patient safety is the immediate priority; use the chain of command.

rationales.correct: An impaired nurse is a direct threat to patient safety. The observing nurse has a mandatory duty to report the suspicion immediately to the supervisor to remove the impaired nurse from patient care.
rationales.incorrect: Confronting them yourself allows them to deny it and keep working. Waiting allows patient harm. Hiding them is complicit and dangerous.

scenario: A nurse discovers a fire in a patient's wastebasket. According to the RACE protocol, what must the nurse do FIRST?

options.A: Pull the fire alarm located in the hallway
options.B: Confine the fire by closing the patient's door
options.C: Rescue the patient by removing them from the room
options.D: Extinguish the fire using the nearest fire extinguisher

hint: Life safety always takes priority over property or system activation.

rationales.correct: RACE stands for Rescue, Alarm, Confine, Extinguish. The absolute first action is to Rescue anyone in immediate danger by pulling the patient out of the room.
rationales.incorrect: Activating the alarm, confining the smoke, and extinguishing the fire are vital but only happen AFTER the patient is removed from the hazard.

scenario: During shift handover, the nurse uses the SBAR format. Under which section would the nurse state, 'I suggest we order a stat chest X-ray and a fluid bolus'?

options.B: Background

hint: What do you want the provider to do?

rationales.correct: In SBAR, 'Recommendation' is where the nurse explicitly states what they need from the provider (e.g., orders for tests, medications, or for the provider to come evaluate).
rationales.incorrect: Situation is the immediate problem. Background is history. Assessment is vital signs/clinical findings.

scenario: To prevent Central Line-Associated Bloodstream Infections (CLABSI), which bundle of practices is globally mandated?

options.A: Hand hygiene, maximal sterile barrier precautions, chlorhexidine skin antisepsis, optimal site selection, daily review of line necessity
options.B: Routine changing of the central line catheter every 72 hours over a guidewire
options.C: Prophylactic administration of IV antibiotics before insertion
options.D: Applying antibiotic ointment to the insertion site daily

hint: The famous 5-step bundle.

rationales.correct: The evidence-based central line bundle includes: strict hand hygiene, maximal sterile barriers (cap, mask, sterile gown, full body drape), chlorhexidine scrub, avoiding the femoral vein, and daily review for prompt removal.
rationales.incorrect: Central lines are NOT routinely changed every 72 hours (only when infected). Prophylactic antibiotics and antibiotic ointments are not recommended due to resistance and fungal overgrowth.

scenario: A patient is highly confused, combative, and pulling at their life-saving endotracheal tube. The nurse wishes to use restraints. What is the guiding legal principle?

options.A: Always apply the most secure restraint (e.g., 4-point) immediately to prevent injury.
options.B: Restraints must be the least restrictive option and used only after alternative measures have failed.
options.C: Restraints can be applied indefinitely as long as the provider signs the order once.
options.D: Family consent must be obtained before applying restraints in an emergency.

hint: Restraints are the absolute last resort.

rationales.correct: Legally and ethically, physical restraints are the last resort. The nurse MUST first attempt and document the failure of less restrictive alternatives, and then apply the least restrictive restraint necessary to maintain safety.
rationales.incorrect: Immediate 4-point restraints are excessive. Orders expire rapidly (4-24 hrs) and require face-to-face renewal. Family consent is not legally required to stop an imminent life-threatening action (pulling a breathing tube).

scenario: A patient is admitted with infective endocarditis. The nurse observes small, painful, red nodules on the pads of the patient's fingers and toes. How should the nurse document this finding?

options.B: Osler's nodes

hint: O = Ouch. These nodules are painful.

rationales.correct: Osler's nodes are tender, painful, red or purple nodules found on the fingertips or toes, classically associated with infective endocarditis.
rationales.incorrect: Janeway lesions are painless, flat, red spots on the palms/soles. Roth spots are retinal hemorrhages. Splinter hemorrhages are dark lines under the fingernails.

scenario: A patient with chronic kidney disease undergoes a barium swallow study for dysphagia. What is the most critical post-procedure nursing intervention?

options.A: Keep the patient NPO for 24 hours
options.B: Administer aggressive IV fluid hydration and laxatives
options.C: Monitor the patient for severe diarrhea

hint: Barium turns into a concrete-like substance if not flushed out.

rationales.correct: Barium contrast can harden in the gastrointestinal tract, causing severe bowel impaction and obstruction. The nurse must push fluids and administer laxatives to expel the barium (chalky white stools).
rationales.incorrect: NPO status worsens the impaction. Barium causes severe constipation, not diarrhea. Enemas are not the first-line preventive measure compared to oral/IV hydration and laxatives.

scenario: A patient is experiencing an acute exacerbation of Meniere's disease. Which dietary modification should the nurse reinforce?

options.B: Strict sodium restriction
options.C: Fluid restriction of 1000 mL per day

hint: The disease involves fluid volume excess in the inner ear.

rationales.correct: Meniere's disease causes an overaccumulation of endolymphatic fluid in the inner ear, leading to vertigo and tinnitus. A strict low-sodium diet is essential to reduce systemic fluid retention and inner ear pressure.
rationales.incorrect: Protein/carb ratios do not affect inner ear fluid. Fluid restriction is not generally recommended; sodium restriction regulates the fluid balance. Tyramine triggers migraines, not Meniere's.

scenario: A patient with hypothyroidism is prescribed daily levothyroxine. Which statement by the patient indicates a need for further teaching?

options.A: I will take my pill every morning at 0600.
options.B: I will take it with my morning coffee and bran muffin.
options.C: I know this medication will likely be a lifelong requirement.
options.D: I will report any sudden weight loss or racing heart to my doctor.

hint: Thyroid hormones require an acidic, perfectly empty stomach for absorption.

rationales.correct: Taking levothyroxine with food, coffee, or high-fiber foods (like a bran muffin) severely decreases its absorption. It must be taken on a completely empty stomach with just water, 30-60 minutes before breakfast.
rationales.incorrect: Taking it early in the morning, recognizing it as a lifelong therapy, and monitoring for signs of hyperthyroidism (toxicity) are all correct understandings.

scenario: A patient with liver cirrhosis is scheduled for a therapeutic paracentesis. Before the procedure, what is the nurse's priority action to ensure patient safety?

options.A: Instruct the patient to empty their bladder
options.B: Administer an IV bolus of normal saline
options.C: Place the patient in a supine position
options.D: Ensure the patient has been NPO for 8 hours

hint: The trocar needle will be inserted into the lower abdominal cavity.

rationales.correct: An empty bladder drastically reduces its size, preventing accidental puncture and rupture by the trocar needle during a paracentesis.
rationales.incorrect: Saline boluses are not routinely given before (albumin is given during/after). The patient should be seated upright (high-Fowler's), not supine. NPO is not required for paracentesis.

scenario: A patient with severe Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease (GERD) asks for advice on preventing nighttime heartburn. What should the nurse recommend?

options.A: Drink a glass of warm whole milk before bed to coat the stomach.
options.B: Eat a small, high-fat snack before sleeping.
options.C: Wait at least 3 hours after eating before lying down.
options.D: Lie flat on the right side during sleep.

hint: Gravity is the best defense against acid reflux.

rationales.correct: Remaining upright for 2-3 hours after meals allows the stomach to empty and prevents gravity from pushing acid up the incompetent lower esophageal sphincter.
rationales.incorrect: Whole milk and high-fat snacks delay gastric emptying and trigger massive acid production, worsening GERD. The patient should sleep with the HOB elevated, ideally on the left side.

scenario: A patient with acute pancreatitis complains of excruciating abdominal pain. Which positioning technique provides the most relief?

options.A: Lying supine with legs fully extended
options.B: Sitting upright and leaning forward slightly
options.C: Lying completely flat on the right side

hint: The pancreas is located in the retroperitoneal space.

rationales.correct: The pancreas is a retroperitoneal organ. Sitting upright, leaning forward, or pulling the knees to the chest (fetal position) pulls the inflamed organ away from the posterior abdominal wall and stretches the peritoneum less, relieving pain.
rationales.incorrect: Lying supine, flat, or prone pulls the abdominal muscles tightly over the inflamed pancreas, significantly exacerbating the pain.

scenario: A patient has experienced a right-hemispheric stroke and is displaying left-sided homonymous hemianopsia. What nursing intervention is most appropriate?

options.A: Place the bedside table and call light on the patient's left side
options.B: Approach the patient from their left side to encourage them to look that way
options.C: Place the patient's food entirely on the right side of their meal tray
options.D: Apply an eye patch to the left eye

hint: The patient cannot see out of the left half of their visual field.

rationales.correct: Homonymous hemianopsia means the patient has lost the left visual field in both eyes. Food and important items must be placed on their right (unaffected) side so they can see them.
rationales.incorrect: Placing items on the left side means they are invisible to the patient. Approaching from the blind side causes severe startling. An eye patch treats diplopia, not visual field cuts.
