scenario: A patient with a deep vein thrombosis is receiving a heparin drip. The patient's aPTT is 115 seconds, and the nurse notes hematuria. What is the nurse's first action?

options.B: Stop the heparin infusion
options.D: Notify the provider for an order to decrease the rate

hint: Remove the source of the toxicity before giving the antidote.

rationales.correct: An aPTT of 115 is critically high, and hematuria indicates active bleeding. The absolute first action is to stop the heparin infusion to prevent further anticoagulation.
rationales.incorrect: Vitamin K is the antidote for warfarin. Protamine sulfate is the antidote for heparin, but it is given after the drip is stopped and by provider order. Decreasing the rate is unsafe during active bleeding.

scenario: A patient with left-sided weakness is being taught how to use a cane. Which instruction by the nurse is correct?

options.A: Hold the cane in the left hand and step with the left leg first.
options.B: Hold the cane in the right hand and advance the cane simultaneously with the left leg.
options.C: Hold the cane in the right hand and advance the right leg first.
options.D: Hold the cane in the left hand and advance the cane simultaneously with the right leg.

hint: Cane Opposite Affected Leg (COAL).

rationales.correct: The cane must be held on the strong side (right hand). To provide a wide base of support, the patient should advance the cane and the weak leg (left leg) forward at the same time, then follow with the strong leg.
rationales.incorrect: Holding the cane on the weak side provides no leverage. Stepping with the strong leg first leaves the patient balancing entirely on the weak, unsupported leg.

scenario: A patient with a fractured right ankle is learning to walk up stairs with crutches. What is the correct sequence of movement?

options.A: Step up with the right leg first, then bring the crutches and left leg up.
options.B: Move the crutches up first, then hop up with both legs.
options.C: Step up with the left leg first, then bring the crutches and right leg up.
options.D: Step up with the crutches and right leg simultaneously, then bring the left leg up.

hint: Up with the good, down with the bad.

rationales.correct: When going UP stairs, the patient must place the strong/unaffected leg (left leg) on the step first to bear the body's weight and push up, followed by bringing the crutches and the weak leg up.
rationales.incorrect: Stepping with the weak leg first leaves the patient unable to lift their body weight against gravity, causing a fall. Putting crutches first throws off the center of gravity.

scenario: A nurse is caring for a patient on Droplet Precautions for Influenza. What specific Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) rule applies when providing care?

options.A: An N95 respirator must be worn before entering the room.
options.B: A standard surgical mask must be worn when within 3 feet of the patient.
options.C: A sterile gown and gloves are required for all patient contact.
options.D: The door to the patient's room must remain closed at all times to maintain negative pressure.

hint: Droplets are heavy and fall to the ground quickly.

rationales.correct: Droplet precautions require the use of a standard surgical mask when coming within 3 feet of the patient (or upon entering the room, per many hospital policies), as droplets are heavy and do not travel far in the air.
rationales.incorrect: N95 respirators and negative pressure rooms are for Airborne precautions (TB/Measles). Sterile gowns are for surgical environments; clean gowns are used for Contact precautions.

scenario: What is the correct sequence for donning personal protective equipment (PPE) before entering an isolation room?

options.B: Gown, Mask, Goggles, Gloves

hint: Work from the bottom up, securing the outer layer first, and covering your hands last.

rationales.correct: The standard sequence for donning PPE is Gown first, followed by the Mask/Respirator, then Goggles/Face Shield, and finally Gloves (pulled over the gown cuffs).
rationales.incorrect: Putting gloves on first contaminates them while tying the gown. Masks must go under goggles.

scenario: The nurse is preparing to administer enoxaparin (Lovenox) via subcutaneous injection. The prefilled syringe contains an air bubble. What is the correct action?

options.A: Expel the air bubble completely before injecting.
options.B: Leave the air bubble in the syringe and inject it after the medication.
options.C: Draw back on the plunger to add more air to the syringe.
options.D: Discard the syringe and obtain a new one without an air bubble.

hint: The bubble creates a seal in the tissue.

rationales.correct: The air bubble in prefilled low-molecular-weight heparin syringes is designed to be injected last to create an 'air lock' in the subcutaneous tissue. This prevents the medication from leaking back up the needle track and causing severe bruising.
rationales.incorrect: Expelling the air bubble guarantees the drug will leak back out, causing hematomas. Adding more air is unnecessary.

scenario: A patient is prescribed an intramuscular injection of iron dextran. The nurse uses the Z-track method. What is the primary clinical purpose of this technique?

options.A: To decrease the pain associated with a large volume injection.
options.B: To ensure the needle reaches the deepest part of the muscle.
options.C: To prevent the dark medication from leaking back and permanently staining the skin.
options.D: To increase the rate of absorption into the bloodstream.

hint: The displaced tissue slides back to close the needle track.

rationales.correct: The Z-track method displaces the skin before injection. Upon release, the tissue layers slide back, trapping the medication deep in the muscle. This is crucial for iron dextran to prevent permanent brown staining of the subcutaneous tissue and skin.
rationales.incorrect: It does not alter the depth, pain, or speed of absorption significantly; its primary purpose is preventing tissue tracking and irritation/staining.

scenario: A patient is receiving a continuous enteral feeding via a nasogastric tube. The nurse checks the gastric residual volume (GRV) and gets 600 mL. The patient is asymptomatic. What is the nurse's priority action?

options.A: Discard the residual, stop the feeding, and notify the provider.
options.B: Return the residual, hold the feeding, and notify the provider.
options.C: Return the residual and continue the feeding at the ordered rate.
options.D: Flush the tube with 50 mL of water and increase the feeding rate.

hint: High volume indicates delayed emptying, but the fluid contains essential electrolytes.

rationales.correct: A GRV >500 mL indicates delayed gastric emptying and high aspiration risk. The feeding must be stopped and the MD notified. However, the residual MUST be returned to the stomach to prevent severe metabolic alkalosis and hypokalemia.
rationales.incorrect: Discarding residuals causes severe electrolyte derangements. Continuing the feeding or increasing the rate risks fatal pulmonary aspiration.

scenario: To prevent Catheter-Associated Urinary Tract Infections (CAUTI) in a patient with an indwelling Foley catheter, which practice is correct?

options.A: Cleanse the urethral meatus vigorously with chlorhexidine daily.
options.B: Routinely irrigate the catheter with sterile saline every shift.
options.C: Maintain the drainage bag below the level of the bladder at all times.
options.D: Change the catheter completely every 7 days.

hint: Prevent stagnant urine from flowing backward.

rationales.correct: Maintaining the drainage bag below the level of the bladder is a critical, evidence-based intervention to prevent retrograde flow of contaminated urine back into the sterile bladder.
rationales.incorrect: Routine irrigation breaks the sterile seal and introduces bacteria. Catheters are not routinely changed based on time (only when infected/obstructed). Daily perineal care with soap and water (not harsh chlorhexidine) is recommended.

scenario: A patient receiving a blood transfusion complains of severe lower back pain, chills, and hematuria 15 minutes into the infusion. What type of reaction is occurring?

options.B: Anaphylactic reaction

hint: The donor cells are being destroyed, and the debris is clogging the kidneys.

rationales.correct: Flank/lower back pain and hematuria are the classic hallmarks of an acute hemolytic reaction caused by ABO incompatibility. The destroyed RBCs clog the renal tubules. The transfusion must be stopped immediately.
rationales.incorrect: Febrile causes fever/chills but no back pain/hematuria. Anaphylaxis causes wheezing/hives. TRALI causes severe respiratory distress.

scenario: The nurse is administering an IV vesicant chemotherapy drug. The patient complains of burning, and the nurse notes swelling and pallor at the site. What is the FIRST action the nurse must take?

options.B: Stop the infusion pump.
options.C: Aspirate the drug from the catheter.
options.D: Inject the specific antidote into the site.

hint: Halt the delivery of the damaging agent before taking any other steps.

rationales.correct: The patient is experiencing extravasation. The absolute first action is to stop the infusion pump immediately to prevent any more of the tissue-destroying vesicant from entering the subcutaneous tissue.
rationales.incorrect: Removing the catheter immediately eliminates the best route to deliver the antidote into the exact tissue space. Aspiration and antidote administration are steps 2 and 3.

scenario: A fire breaks out in a trash can in a patient's room. According to the RACE protocol, what is the very first action the nurse must take?

options.A: Pull the fire alarm in the hallway
options.B: Close the door to the patient's room to confine the smoke
options.C: Rescue the patient by moving them out of the room
options.D: Extinguish the fire using the nearest extinguisher

hint: Life safety always takes priority over property or system activation.

rationales.correct: RACE stands for Rescue, Alarm, Confine, Extinguish. The absolute first action is to Rescue anyone in immediate danger by pulling the patient out of the room.
rationales.incorrect: Activating the alarm, confining the smoke, and extinguishing the fire are vital but must wait until the patient is safely removed from the hazard.

scenario: A patient who had abdominal surgery is reporting pain. The patient's heart rate is 88 bpm, and they are watching TV comfortably, but they rate their pain as an 8 on a 0-10 scale. What is the nurse's best action?

options.A: Document the pain as 3/10 based on the patient's relaxed behavior.
options.B: Administer the pain medication as ordered based on the patient's self-report.
options.C: Wait 30 minutes to see if the patient's heart rate increases before giving medication.
options.D: Ask the patient if they are sure it is an 8, since they are watching TV.

hint: Pain is whatever the experiencing person says it is.

rationales.correct: The patient's self-report is the single most reliable indicator of pain. Behavioral or physiological signs (vital signs, watching TV) do not negate a high self-reported pain score, especially in chronic or steady-state pain.
rationales.incorrect: Documenting a lower score is falsification. Waiting for vital sign changes or doubting the patient's report is unethical and ignores the gold standard of pain assessment.

scenario: A confused patient requires bilateral soft wrist restraints. How should the nurse secure the restraint ties to the bed?

options.A: Tie them to the movable side rails using a square knot.
options.B: Tie them to the immovable bed frame using a quick-release knot.
options.C: Tie them to the headboard using a double knot.
options.D: Tie them to the mattress springs using a slip knot.

hint: The knot must come undone instantly, and the attachment point must not move independently of the patient.

rationales.correct: Restraints must always be secured using a quick-release knot (slip knot) so they can be removed instantly in an emergency. They must be tied to the immovable portion of the bed frame.
rationales.incorrect: Tying to a side rail will violently yank the patient's arm if the rail is lowered. Square or double knots cannot be untied quickly in a fire. Mattress springs are not secure.

scenario: A patient with severe heart failure is experiencing extreme dyspnea and orthopnea. In which position should the nurse place the patient?

options.B: Prone

hint: Use gravity to drop the diaphragm and keep fluid in the bases of the lungs.

rationales.correct: High-Fowler's position (sitting upright at 90 degrees) drops the diaphragm, allowing for maximum chest expansion, and uses gravity to pool pulmonary edema fluid at the lung bases, improving gas exchange.
rationales.incorrect: Supine positioning causes fluid to spread across the entire lung field, drastically worsening dyspnea (orthopnea). Prone and side-lying do not maximize chest expansion.

scenario: The nurse is collecting a culture from a suspected infected surgical wound. What is the correct technique?

options.A: Swab the purulent drainage pooled on the old dressing.
options.B: Clean the wound with normal saline first, then swab the red granulation tissue.
options.C: Swab the intact, inflamed skin surrounding the wound edges.
options.D: Apply an antibiotic ointment to the wound, then swab the center.

hint: You want to culture the actively growing bacteria inside the tissue, not the dead surface exudate.

rationales.correct: To obtain an accurate culture of the infecting organism, the nurse must first cleanse the wound with normal saline to remove surface debris and old exudate, then firmly swab the actively growing (red granulation) tissue.
rationales.incorrect: Swabbing old drainage or dressings only cultures dead surface contaminants. Swabbing intact skin cultures normal flora. Applying antibiotics ruins the culture.

scenario: A nurse accidentally administers 100 mg of metoprolol instead of the ordered 50 mg. What is the very first action the nurse must take?

options.B: Complete an incident report
options.C: Assess the patient's vital signs and clinical status
options.D: Inform the charge nurse of the error

hint: Patient safety comes before reporting.

rationales.correct: The absolute first action after any medication error is to assess the patient for any adverse effects (in this case, bradycardia and hypotension) to ensure their immediate safety.
rationales.incorrect: Notifying the provider is the second step, as the provider will need the patient's current vital signs to give further orders. Incident reports and notifying management come after clinical stabilization.

scenario: An elderly patient with terminal cancer signs a Living Will explicitly refusing intubation and CPR. The patient goes into cardiac arrest. The patient's designated Healthcare Proxy demands the nurse begin CPR. What is the nurse's action?

options.A: Begin CPR because the Healthcare Proxy has the final legal authority.
options.B: Honor the Living Will and do not initiate CPR.
options.C: Call the hospital ethics committee for an emergency consult.
options.D: Provide rescue breathing but withhold chest compressions.

hint: The patient's own voice overrides the proxy.

rationales.correct: A Living Will contains the patient's direct, explicit medical choices. A Healthcare Proxy is only supposed to make decisions based on what the patient would want. The written Living Will legally supersedes the proxy if they conflict.
rationales.incorrect: The proxy cannot override the patient's written directive. Waiting for ethics violates the legal document in an emergency. Partial codes are unethical.

scenario: A physician is obtaining informed consent for a surgical procedure. The patient asks the nurse, 'What are the alternatives to this surgery?' What is the nurse's legal responsibility?

options.A: Explain the medical alternatives to the patient.
options.B: Tell the patient there are no other options.
options.C: Refuse to let the patient sign and notify the physician to return and answer the question.
options.D: Have the patient sign the form, then leave a note for the physician.

hint: The nurse only acts as a witness to an informed signature.

rationales.correct: If a patient asks medical questions about risks, benefits, or alternatives, they are not truly 'informed'. The nurse cannot legally provide this medical information and must halt the consent process until the physician returns to answer the questions.
rationales.incorrect: Explaining alternatives is outside the RN scope for a surgical procedure. Having them sign while confused invalidates the consent.

scenario: A nurse logs out of the electronic health record (EHR) immediately after charting and before leaving the computer terminal unattended. Which ethical and legal principle is the nurse upholding?

options.B: Beneficence

hint: Protecting patient information from unauthorized eyes.

rationales.correct: Logging off the EHR prevents unauthorized individuals from viewing or altering a patient's protected health information, directly upholding HIPAA and the principle of confidentiality.
rationales.incorrect: Autonomy is patient choice. Beneficence is doing good. Veracity is telling the truth.

scenario: The RN is delegating tasks for the shift. Which task is appropriate to delegate to an Unlicensed Assistive Personnel (UAP)?

options.A: Taking the first set of vital signs on a newly admitted patient
options.B: Emptying a Foley catheter bag and recording the output
options.C: Assessing a patient's pain level after ambulation
options.D: Showing a patient how to use an incentive spirometer

hint: UAPs handle routine measuring and recording, not initial assessments or teaching.

rationales.correct: Emptying a Foley and recording the output is a standard, non-invasive procedural task that requires no independent nursing judgment, making it perfect for a UAP.
rationales.incorrect: The FIRST set of vitals on an admission must be done by the RN to establish the baseline assessment. Assessing pain and teaching are strictly RN scopes of practice.

scenario: A 6-month-old infant is admitted with Respiratory Syncytial Virus (RSV) bronchiolitis. The infant is tachypneic and refuses to feed. What is the priority nursing intervention before offering a bottle?

options.B: Suction the infant's nares with a bulb syringe
options.D: Place the infant in a prone position

hint: Infants are obligate nose breathers.

rationales.correct: Infants with RSV produce copious nasal secretions and are obligate nose breathers. Suctioning the nares before feeding clears the airway, allowing them to breathe while they suck and swallow, preventing aspiration and exhaustion.
rationales.incorrect: Albuterol is generally ineffective for RSV bronchiolitis. Antibiotics don't treat viruses. Prone positioning increases SIDS risk.

scenario: A 4-year-old child presents to the ED with sudden high fever, drooling, severe sore throat, and leaning forward in a tripod position. Which nursing action is strictly contraindicated?

options.B: Examining the throat with a tongue depressor
options.C: Keeping the child calm on the parent's lap

hint: The airway is severely inflamed and highly irritable.

rationales.correct: The child has acute epiglottitis. Inserting anything into the mouth (tongue depressor, swab) can trigger reflex laryngospasm, causing instant, total airway obstruction and death.
rationales.incorrect: Intubation prep, keeping the child calm, and giving oxygen are all necessary, life-saving interventions.

scenario: A 2-year-old child has a seal-like, barking cough and inspiratory stridor. The physician orders nebulized racemic epinephrine. What is the expected outcome of this medication?

options.A: It kills the virus causing the infection.
options.B: It causes mucosal vasoconstriction, reducing subglottic edema.
options.C: It acts as a strong sedative to keep the child calm.
options.D: It paralyzes the vocal cords to prevent spasm.

hint: The drug shrinks swollen tissues in the upper airway.

rationales.correct: Racemic epinephrine is an alpha-adrenergic agonist. It causes rapid vasoconstriction of the mucosal blood vessels, reducing the subglottic swelling and relieving the stridor of viral croup.
rationales.incorrect: It does not kill viruses. It is a stimulant (causes tachycardia/jitteriness), not a sedative. It does not paralyze vocal cords.

scenario: A 10-year-old with Cystic Fibrosis is receiving chest physiotherapy (CPT) to mobilize thick secretions. When should the nurse schedule this treatment?

options.B: One hour before meals or 2 hours after meals

hint: Tapping on the chest right after eating will cause a specific GI reaction.

rationales.correct: CPT involves percussion and postural drainage, which can easily induce nausea and vomiting. It must be scheduled on an empty stomach (1 hour before or 2 hours after meals) to prevent vomiting and aspiration.
rationales.incorrect: Immediately after meals causes vomiting. It should be done 2-4 times daily, not just at bedtime. It is not done during acute bronchospasm as it can worsen airway reactivity.

scenario: An infant with Tetralogy of Fallot becomes intensely cyanotic, tachypneic, and agitated (a 'Tet spell') while crying. What is the nurse's immediate first action?

options.B: Place the infant in the knee-chest position

hint: Increase systemic resistance to push blood back to the lungs.

rationales.correct: Placing the infant in a knee-chest position kinks the femoral arteries, instantly increasing systemic vascular resistance and forcing blood back through the pulmonary artery for oxygenation.
rationales.incorrect: Oxygen and morphine are given AFTER the mechanical positioning. CPR is only for pulseless cardiac arrest.

scenario: A nurse is assessing a newborn and auscultates a continuous, loud, 'machinery-like' murmur over the left upper sternal border. The nurse anticipates an order for which medication to close this defect?

options.B: Indomethacin

hint: This NSAID blocks the hormones keeping the fetal duct open.

rationales.correct: A machinery-like murmur indicates a Patent Ductus Arteriosus (PDA). Indomethacin, an NSAID, inhibits prostaglandin synthesis, which is required to permanently close the ductus arteriosus in premature infants.
rationales.incorrect: Prostaglandin E1 keeps the PDA OPEN (used in ductal-dependent lesions like TGA). Digoxin and furosemide treat the resulting heart failure but do not physically close the hole.

scenario: During a routine assessment of an infant, the nurse notes bounding pulses in the arms and weak, thready pulses in the legs. Blood pressure is significantly higher in the arms than the legs. Diagnosis?

options.B: Coarctation of the Aorta

hint: A stricture in the descending aorta acts like a kinked hose.

rationales.correct: Coarctation of the aorta is a narrowing of the descending aorta. This causes high pressure and bounding pulses before the stricture (upper extremities) and low pressure/weak pulses after the stricture (lower extremities).
rationales.incorrect: ASD, TGA, and VSD do not typically present with this classic upper-to-lower extremity pressure gradient.

scenario: A 5-year-old is diagnosed with Kawasaki disease. To prevent the development of coronary artery aneurysms, the nurse prepares to administer which two therapies?

options.B: Intravenous Immunoglobulin (IVIG) and high-dose aspirin

hint: Halt the systemic immune response and prevent platelets from sticking to the inflamed arteries.

rationales.correct: Kawasaki disease is treated with a single large dose of IVIG to shut down the immune-mediated vasculitis, and high-dose aspirin to provide anti-inflammatory and antiplatelet cardioprotection.
rationales.incorrect: Antibiotics and acyclovir don't treat this autoimmune condition. Acetaminophen lacks the antiplatelet effect. Warfarin is for chronic clotting disorders.

scenario: A 10-year-old child recently recovered from an untreated sore throat. The child now presents with involuntary, purposeless, jerking movements of the limbs and face, and a new heart murmur. What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.B: Acute Rheumatic Fever (Sydenham chorea)

hint: An autoimmune reaction to Group A Strep attacking the basal ganglia and heart valves.

rationales.correct: Acute Rheumatic Fever follows untreated Group A Strep. It damages heart valves (new murmur) and the basal ganglia, causing Sydenham chorea (rapid, aimless, involuntary movements).
rationales.incorrect: Tourette's involves chronic tics. Kawasaki features fever and strawberry tongue. Meningitis features stiff neck and altered consciousness.

scenario: A child with sickle cell disease presents to the ED with severe joint pain and a fever. What is the absolute highest priority nursing intervention?

options.A: Apply cold compresses to the affected joints
options.B: Administer aggressive intravenous hydration
options.C: Perform passive range of motion to prevent stiffness

hint: Thick blood is causing the ischemia.

rationales.correct: In a vaso-occlusive crisis, aggressive IV hydration is the absolute priority to decrease blood viscosity (hemodilution). Thinner blood flows past the sickled blockages, reversing tissue ischemia and pain.
rationales.incorrect: Cold compresses cause vasoconstriction, worsening the blockage. ROM increases oxygen demand, worsening pain. Penicillin is for daily prevention, not acute crisis management.

scenario: A 9-year-old with Hemophilia A falls and injures his knee, which rapidly swells. What is the first medical intervention required?

options.B: Administer IV Factor VIII replacement
options.C: Apply a warm heating pad to the knee

hint: Replace what the blood is missing.

rationales.correct: Hemophilia A is a Factor VIII deficiency. The absolute first step for active bleeding (hemarthrosis) is replacing the missing clotting factor via IV to stop the internal hemorrhage.
rationales.incorrect: Aspiration without clotting factor will cause fatal bleeding. Heat worsens bleeding (use ice). Ibuprofen blocks platelets and is strictly contraindicated.

scenario: A 5-year-old boy uses his hands to push against his knees to stand up from the floor. This is documented as Gowers' sign. What disease does this indicate, and how is it inherited?

options.B: Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy; X-linked recessive

hint: It primarily affects boys and causes progressive, ascending muscle weakness.

rationales.correct: Gowers' sign is a classic hallmark of Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy (DMD). DMD is an X-linked recessive disorder, meaning it almost exclusively affects males, passed down from carrier mothers.
rationales.incorrect: Cerebral palsy is brain injury, not genetic. Spina bifida is a neural tube defect. Cystic fibrosis does not cause primary muscle wasting.

scenario: A 9-month-old infant is admitted with episodic, severe abdominal pain, pulling knees to the chest, and vomiting. The nurse notes 'currant jelly' stool. What is the primary treatment/diagnostic procedure for this condition?

options.B: Air or barium enema

hint: The pressure of the gas/fluid pops the telescoped bowel back into place.

rationales.correct: The child has intussusception (currant jelly stools). An air or barium enema is both diagnostic and highly successful at therapeutically 'un-telescoping' the bowel due to the hydrostatic pressure.
rationales.incorrect: Pyloromyotomy is for pyloric stenosis. Appendectomy is for appendicitis. Pull-through surgery is for Hirschsprung's disease.

scenario: A 4-week-old infant is brought to the clinic for forceful, non-bilious projectile vomiting after every feeding. Assessment reveals an olive-shaped mass in the RUQ. Which acid-base imbalance is expected?

options.B: Metabolic alkalosis

hint: The infant is continuously losing gastric acid.

rationales.correct: The infant has pyloric stenosis (projectile vomiting, olive mass). The continuous loss of gastric hydrochloric acid leads to metabolic alkalosis.
rationales.incorrect: Metabolic acidosis results from losing base (e.g., lower GI diarrhea). Respiratory imbalances result from lung/ventilation issues.

scenario: A newborn has not passed meconium in 48 hours and has abdominal distention. A rectal biopsy shows an absence of ganglion cells in the colon. What is this condition?

options.B: Celiac disease

hint: Aganglionic megacolon.

rationales.correct: Hirschsprung's disease involves a lack of nerve cells (ganglion cells) in the lower colon. This halts peristalsis, causing stool to back up and a classic failure to pass meconium.
rationales.incorrect: Meconium ileus is related to CF. Celiac is gluten intolerance. NEC is bowel necrosis in premature infants.

scenario: A child is diagnosed with Celiac disease. The nurse is teaching the parents about dietary restrictions using the BROW acronym. What foods must be eliminated?

options.B: Barley, Rye, Oats, Wheat

hint: These grains contain gluten or are highly cross-contaminated.

rationales.correct: Celiac disease requires a strict lifelong gluten-free diet. The BROW acronym specifies the grains to avoid: Barley, Rye, Oats (due to heavy cross-contamination), and Wheat.
rationales.incorrect: Rice, beans, corn, and fruits are naturally gluten-free and are safe staple carbohydrates for celiac patients.

scenario: A 4-year-old is admitted with nephrotic syndrome. Which laboratory finding is the primary defining characteristic of this disease?

options.B: Massive proteinuria

hint: The glomeruli become highly permeable to large molecules, causing fluid to shift into tissues.

rationales.correct: Nephrotic syndrome is characterized by massive proteinuria (loss of protein in the urine). This leads to severe hypoalbuminemia (low blood protein), causing severe generalized edema.
rationales.incorrect: Gross hematuria is a sign of acute glomerulonephritis. WBCs indicate infection. The serum albumin is extremely LOW, not high.

scenario: A child is admitted with acute post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis. What is the most critical assessment for the nurse to perform?

options.A: Monitoring blood pressure for severe hypertension
options.B: Assessing the throat for a return of the strep infection
options.D: Checking the urine for massive glycosuria

hint: The inflamed kidneys cannot filter fluid, leading to massive intravascular volume overload.

rationales.correct: Acute glomerulonephritis causes massive fluid retention, which can lead to life-threatening hypertension and hypertensive encephalopathy. Frequent BP monitoring is critical.
rationales.incorrect: The strep infection is usually gone by the time the autoimmune kidney damage occurs. They experience weight GAIN (edema), not loss. Glycosuria is diabetes, not glomerulonephritis.

scenario: At a well-child clinic, a nurse assesses a 6-month-old infant. Which gross motor milestone should the infant be demonstrating?

options.B: Sitting up perfectly straight without any support

hint: Gross motor skills progress from head to toe.

rationales.correct: By 6 months of age, an infant should be able to roll from their back to their abdomen (and vice versa) and sit with support.
rationales.incorrect: Walking is 12-15 months. Sitting without support is 8 months. Crawling usually begins around 9 months.

scenario: To drastically reduce the risk of Sudden Infant Death Syndrome (SIDS), what is the most important instruction the nurse must give new parents?

options.A: Always place the infant on their back (supine) to sleep
options.B: Keep the room very warm and use heavy blankets
options.C: Place the infant on their stomach (prone) to prevent choking on spit-up
options.D: Use bumper pads in the crib to prevent head injury

hint: Back to sleep.

rationales.correct: The 'Back to Sleep' campaign established that the supine position on a firm mattress is the single most effective intervention to prevent SIDS.
rationales.incorrect: Overheating is a major SIDS risk factor. Prone positioning vastly increases SIDS risk. Bumper pads, pillows, and soft bedding are suffocation hazards and are strictly prohibited.

scenario: A pregnant patient reports her last menstrual period (LMP) began on October 5. Using Naegele's rule, what is her estimated date of delivery (EDD)?

options.B: August 12 of the next year

hint: Subtract 3 months, add 7 days.

rationales.correct: Naegele's rule: Subtract 3 months from October (July), add 7 days to the 5th (12th). The EDD is July 12.
rationales.incorrect: The other dates are math errors based on incorrect application of the formula.

scenario: A pregnant patient has a history of one miscarriage at 10 weeks, one full-term infant (living), and preterm twins born at 34 weeks (both living). What is her GTPAL?
hint: Twins count as one pregnancy and one delivery event.

rationales.correct: Gravida = 4 (current + term + preterm + abortion). Term = 1. Preterm = 1 (twins are ONE delivery). Abortion = 1. Living = 3. GTPAL: 4-1-1-1-3.
rationales.incorrect: Counting twins as two parities (P) is a common error. They are one birth event.

scenario: A patient arrives at the clinic suspecting pregnancy. Which finding is considered a 'Positive' (diagnostic) sign of pregnancy?

options.A: A positive urine hCG pregnancy test
options.B: Amenorrhea and breast tenderness
options.C: Fetal heart tones heard via Doppler

hint: Positive signs offer indisputable proof of a fetus, distinct from the mother's body.

rationales.correct: Positive signs of pregnancy attribute directly to the fetus: Fetal heart tones, ultrasound visualization, and fetal movement felt by the provider.
rationales.incorrect: A pregnancy test and Chadwick's sign are 'Probable' signs (can be caused by tumors/moles). Amenorrhea is a 'Presumptive' (subjective) sign.

scenario: A pregnant patient in her third trimester complains of feeling dizzy and clammy when lying flat on her back. What is the nurse's best advice?

options.A: Eat a high-carbohydrate snack before lying down
options.B: Turn onto your left side to sleep or rest
options.C: Take deep, slow breaths to prevent hyperventilation
options.D: This is a sign of premature labor; go to the hospital

hint: The weight of the uterus is crushing a major blood vessel.

rationales.correct: This is supine hypotensive syndrome. The gravid uterus compresses the inferior vena cava when the patient lies flat, dropping venous return. Turning to the left lateral position relieves this pressure instantly.
rationales.incorrect: It is not related to blood sugar (snacks) or breathing. It is a positional mechanical issue, not a sign of labor.

scenario: A patient at 33 weeks gestation presents with painless, bright red vaginal bleeding. Which procedure is strictly contraindicated?

options.B: Continuous electronic fetal monitoring
options.D: Initiation of a large-bore IV line

hint: The placenta may be covering the cervical opening.

rationales.correct: Painless, bright red bleeding is the hallmark of placenta previa. A digital vaginal exam can puncture the placenta, causing massive, lethal hemorrhage for both mother and baby.
rationales.incorrect: Fetal monitoring, drawing blood for potential transfusion, and starting an IV are all critical and safe interventions for placenta previa.

scenario: A patient at 37 weeks gestation presents with sudden, severe, constant abdominal pain, a rigid, board-like abdomen, and dark red vaginal bleeding. Diagnosis?

options.B: Placental abruption (Abruptio placentae)

hint: Pain + Rigid abdomen + Dark bleeding = Separation.

rationales.correct: Placental abruption occurs when the placenta detaches prematurely. It causes severe pain, a rigid abdomen (due to blood trapped in the uterine muscle), and dark red bleeding.
rationales.incorrect: Placenta previa is painless with bright red blood. Uterine rupture presents with loss of fetal station and cessation of contractions. Vasa previa is painless fetal bleeding.

scenario: A patient with severe preeclampsia is receiving a magnesium sulfate infusion. Assessment reveals a respiratory rate of 10 breaths/min, oxygen saturation 90%, and absent deep tendon reflexes. First action?

options.B: Stop the magnesium sulfate infusion immediately

hint: Remove the toxic agent before giving the antidote.

rationales.correct: The patient has magnesium toxicity (respiratory depression, absent reflexes). The absolute first action is to stop the offending medication infusion.
rationales.incorrect: Calcium gluconate is the antidote and is given IMMEDIATELY AFTER stopping the drip. Calling the MD and checking FHR delay the immediate life-saving action.

scenario: A pregnant patient with preeclampsia develops HELLP syndrome. Which laboratory findings confirm this diagnosis?

options.A: High platelets, low liver enzymes, high hemoglobin
options.B: High blood glucose, elevated BUN, low potassium
options.C: Hemolysis, elevated liver enzymes, low platelets
options.D: Low WBCs, low liver enzymes, high platelets

hint: Read the acronym.

rationales.correct: HELLP stands for Hemolysis (H), Elevated Liver enzymes (EL), and Low Platelets (LP). It is a severe, life-threatening progression of preeclampsia requiring immediate delivery.
rationales.incorrect: The other options are incorrect interpretations of the acronym and completely opposite to the disease pathology (which destroys RBCs and platelets while injuring the liver).

scenario: A patient is diagnosed with an ectopic pregnancy and the tube has ruptured. She reports severe shoulder pain. What causes this?

options.B: Musculoskeletal strain from bearing down
options.D: Referred pain from phrenic nerve irritation by intra-abdominal blood

hint: Blood pooling under the diaphragm.

rationales.correct: Rupture causes massive internal bleeding. The blood pools under the diaphragm, irritating the phrenic nerve, which refers pain to the tip of the shoulder (Kehr's sign).
rationales.incorrect: It is a physical, anatomical referral of pain from hemorrhage, not psychological, musculoskeletal, or cardiac in origin.

scenario: A patient undergoes a D&C for a hydatidiform mole (molar pregnancy). What is the most critical post-operative teaching point?

options.A: You must use strict contraception for at least 1 year.
options.B: You should try to get pregnant again quickly to heal the uterus.
options.C: You will need a hysterectomy to prevent cancer.
options.D: You must undergo radiation therapy for 6 months.

hint: Pregnancy hormones will mask the growth of a specific cancer.

rationales.correct: Molar pregnancies carry a high risk of developing into choriocarcinoma. hCG levels must be monitored for 1 year to ensure no cancer grows. Pregnancy raises hCG naturally, masking cancer, so strict contraception is mandatory.
rationales.incorrect: Getting pregnant ruins the hCG monitoring. Hysterectomy and radiation are only for confirmed, advanced cancer, not standard post-molar care.

scenario: A patient is admitted in preterm labor at 29 weeks gestation. The provider orders betamethasone IM. What is the primary purpose of this medication?

options.B: To prevent maternal infection
options.D: To stimulate fetal lung maturation and surfactant production

hint: Premature babies lack surfactant to keep their alveoli open.

rationales.correct: Betamethasone is a corticosteroid given to mothers in premature labor. It crosses the placenta and stimulates the fetal lungs to produce surfactant, preventing respiratory distress syndrome if the baby is born early.
rationales.incorrect: Tocolytics (like terbutaline or nifedipine) stop contractions. Antibiotics prevent infection. Antihypertensives lower BP.

scenario: A laboring patient at 39 weeks experiences a sudden gush of fluid, and the fetal heart rate drops to 70 bpm. The nurse performs a vaginal exam and feels a pulsing loop of cord. First action?

options.B: Use two sterile fingers to elevate the fetal presenting part off the cord
options.C: Push the cord back into the uterus
options.D: Prepare the patient for an immediate vaginal delivery

hint: Take the pressure of the baby's head off the cord.

rationales.correct: This is a prolapsed umbilical cord. Gravity must be used to relieve pressure on the cord by placing the mother in knee-chest or Trendelenburg position, and the nurse must lift the baby's head off the cord to restore blood flow.
rationales.incorrect: Oxygen is secondary to the mechanical fix. NEVER push the cord back in (causes severe spasm/occlusion). A C-section is required, not a vaginal delivery.

scenario: A laboring patient is experiencing late decelerations on the fetal monitor. What does this pattern indicate?

options.B: Umbilical cord compression

hint: VEAL CHOP: Late = Placental.

rationales.correct: Late decelerations occur after the peak of the contraction and indicate uteroplacental insufficiency (the fetus is not getting enough oxygen from the placenta). This requires immediate intervention (LIONS: Left side, IV fluid, Oxygen, Notify MD, Stop Pitocin).
rationales.incorrect: Early decels = Head compression. Variable decels = Cord compression. Sleep cycles don't cause decels.

scenario: The fetal heart monitor displays variable decelerations. What is the most appropriate first-line nursing intervention?

options.B: Administer IV fluids
options.C: Turn the patient to her left or right side

hint: Variable decelerations mean cord compression. Shifting the baby's weight often relieves it.

rationales.correct: Variable decelerations are caused by umbilical cord compression. Simply changing the maternal position often shifts the fetus off the cord, instantly resolving the deceleration.
rationales.incorrect: Stopping oxytocin, giving fluids, and oxygen are secondary steps if positioning fails or for late decelerations (placental insufficiency).

scenario: A patient is receiving an oxytocin infusion for labor induction. Contractions are lasting 100 seconds and occurring every 1.5 minutes. The fetal heart rate is 110 bpm with late decelerations. First action?

options.A: Turn the patient to the left lateral position
options.B: Stop the oxytocin infusion immediately

hint: Remove the cause of the uterine hyperstimulation.

rationales.correct: The patient is experiencing uterine tachysystole (hyperstimulation), which is cutting off blood flow to the fetus (late decels). The absolute first action is to stop the offending drug (oxytocin).
rationales.incorrect: Turning and oxygen are required immediately AFTER stopping the oxytocin. If you don't stop the drug, the contractions will continue to suffocate the baby.

scenario: Thirty minutes after a vaginal delivery, a patient is bleeding heavily. The nurse palpates a soft, boggy fundus at the umbilicus. What is the immediate first action?

options.B: Empty the patient's bladder

hint: The uterus is in the right place but lacks tone.

rationales.correct: The uterus is boggy but midline (not displaced by the bladder). The immediate first action for uterine atony is vigorous fundal massage to mechanically stimulate the uterine muscle to contract and stop the bleeding.
rationales.incorrect: Meds are given if massage fails. The bladder isn't the primary issue if the fundus is perfectly midline at the umbilicus. Calling the provider delays immediate life-saving physical intervention.

scenario: Two hours postpartum, the nurse palpates a boggy uterus displaced to the right, slightly above the umbilicus. The patient is bleeding heavily. What is the most appropriate first action?

options.B: Assist the patient to the bathroom to void
options.D: Notify the physician of impending hemorrhage

hint: What anatomical structure sits right below the uterus and pushes it out of place when full?

rationales.correct: A full bladder displaces the uterus (usually to the right) and prevents it from contracting properly (uterine atony), leading to bleeding. Emptying the bladder fixes the root mechanical problem.
rationales.incorrect: Massage alone won't work if the bladder remains full. Oxytocin won't overcome the mechanical obstruction. Emptying the bladder is the first nursing intervention before calling the MD.

scenario: A mother who is Rh-negative delivers an Rh-positive infant. When must the nurse administer Rho(D) immune globulin (RhoGAM)?

options.B: Within 72 hours of birth
options.D: During the first trimester of her next pregnancy

hint: It prevents the mother from forming antibodies before the immune system finalizes the reaction.

rationales.correct: RhoGAM must be given to an Rh-negative mother within 72 hours of delivering an Rh-positive baby to prevent her immune system from forming antibodies that would attack future Rh-positive pregnancies.
rationales.incorrect: 24 hours is acceptable but the strict protocol limit is 72 hours. 6 weeks is too late. Waiting until the next pregnancy is useless because sensitization will have already occurred.

scenario: A breastfeeding mother develops mastitis. Her breast is red, swollen, and painful, and she has a fever. What should the nurse teach her about feeding?

options.A: Stop breastfeeding completely to prevent infecting the infant
options.B: Pump the affected breast and discard the milk
options.C: Continue feeding frequently to completely empty the breast
options.D: Apply cold packs to the breast prior to feeding

hint: Milk stasis is the enemy of healing.

rationales.correct: Mastitis requires antibiotics and frequent, complete emptying of the breast. The milk is safe for the infant. Frequent feeding prevents abscess formation.
rationales.incorrect: Stopping feeding causes severe engorgement. Discarding milk is unnecessary. Warm packs (not cold) are used prior to feeding to dilate ducts and promote let-down.

scenario: A newborn born to a mother with a heroin addiction is experiencing Neonatal Abstinence Syndrome (NAS). Which nursing intervention is most appropriate?

options.A: Provide vigorous tactile stimulation to keep the infant awake
options.B: Place the infant in a brightly lit room near the nurses' station
options.C: Swaddle the infant tightly and place in a quiet, dark room
options.D: Feed the infant large volumes of formula every 6 hours

hint: The infant's central nervous system is extremely hypersensitive and irritable.

rationales.correct: NAS causes severe CNS hyperirritability (tremors, high-pitched crying). Nursing care focuses on minimizing sensory overload by providing a dark, quiet environment and tight swaddling to prevent self-stimulation.
rationales.incorrect: Stimulation and bright lights will trigger severe distress and potential seizures. Feedings should be small and frequent due to high metabolic demands and poor suck/swallow coordination.

scenario: A mass casualty incident has occurred. Using the START triage method, a victim has a respiratory rate of 36 breaths per minute. Which tag should the nurse assign?

options.B: Yellow (Delayed)

hint: RPM: Respirations > 30 = Red.

rationales.correct: In START triage, any patient with a respiratory rate greater than 30 breaths per minute is immediately tagged Red (Immediate) because they are in respiratory distress and shock.
rationales.incorrect: Yellow is < 30 but cannot walk. Green is walking. Black is apneic even after airway repositioning.

scenario: A triage nurse assesses a disaster victim who is not breathing. The nurse repositions the airway, but the patient remains apneic. Which tag is assigned?

options.B: Yellow

hint: Resources cannot be spent on CPR during a mass casualty.

rationales.correct: If a patient remains apneic after a simple airway repositioning in a mass casualty event, they are tagged Black (Expectant/Deceased). Resources must be saved for survivable victims.
rationales.incorrect: If they started breathing after repositioning, they would be tagged Red. Yellow and Green patients are breathing spontaneously.

scenario: A nurse receives report on 4 patients. Which patient must the nurse assess FIRST?

options.A: A patient 1 day post-op from a bowel resection reporting sudden, severe shortness of breath
options.B: A patient with COPD who has an SpO2 of 88% on room air
options.C: A patient admitted with a kidney stone rating pain 10/10 and vomiting
options.D: A patient with a UTI who has a temperature of 102.1 F

hint: Sudden dyspnea after surgery is a classic sign of a lethal clot.

rationales.correct: Sudden shortness of breath post-op is the hallmark of a Pulmonary Embolism. This is an acute, life-threatening airway/breathing emergency requiring immediate intervention.
rationales.incorrect: 88% is normal for COPD. Pain and vomiting are distressing but not immediately lethal. A fever is expected with a UTI.

scenario: Which patient requires the most immediate assessment by the charge nurse?

options.A: A patient with asthma requesting their PRN albuterol inhaler
options.B: A patient with heart failure with 2+ pitting edema in both legs
options.C: A patient with a severe asthma exacerbation whose wheezing has suddenly stopped completely but they are lethargic
options.D: A patient with diabetes who is sweating and has a blood sugar of 68 mg/dL

hint: Silence in an asthmatic lung is an ominous sign.

rationales.correct: A 'silent chest' with lethargy indicates complete airway obstruction and impending respiratory arrest. The patient is no longer moving enough air to create a wheeze and needs immediate intubation.
rationales.incorrect: PRN albuterol is urgent but the patient is still moving air. Edema is chronic HF. Blood sugar of 68 is low, but easily fixed with juice by a UAP or LPN while the RN saves the airway.

scenario: A patient with sickle cell anemia is admitted with a vaso-occlusive crisis. Suddenly, the patient complains of severe chest pain, dyspnea, and has a new fever. Priority action?

options.A: Administer PRN morphine for the crisis pain
options.B: Notify the provider immediately and prepare for aggressive oxygenation/transfusion
options.C: Apply cold compresses to the chest
options.D: Encourage the patient to use an incentive spirometer

hint: This is acute chest syndrome, a leading cause of death in sickle cell patients.

rationales.correct: Acute chest syndrome is characterized by vaso-occlusion in the pulmonary microcirculation leading to chest pain, fever, hypoxemia, and new lung infiltrates. It requires immediate, aggressive medical intervention (exchange transfusions, oxygen, fluids).
rationales.incorrect: Pain meds are needed, but diagnosing and treating the lethal pulmonary occlusion is the priority. Cold compresses worsen sickling. Spirometry is preventative, not a treatment for an active infarction.

scenario: A patient is 6 hours post-thyroidectomy. The patient tells the nurse, 'My voice sounds squeaky and my neck feels tight.' What is the nurse's priority action?

options.A: Assess the surgical dressing and call the rapid response team
options.B: Document the finding as expected hoarseness
options.D: Provide the patient with ice chips

hint: The airway is physically closing.

rationales.correct: A squeaky voice (stridor) and neck tightness post-thyroidectomy indicate a rapidly expanding hematoma or severe edema crushing the trachea. This is an immediate, lethal airway obstruction requiring emergency airway management.
rationales.incorrect: It is not expected. Calcium treats hypocalcemia (tetany), not physical airway compression. Ice chips pose a choking hazard in a closing airway.

scenario: A patient with a closed head injury has been stable. The nurse notes the Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) score drops from 14 to 9, the heart rate drops to 45, and the blood pressure rises to 190/60. Priority action?

options.A: Administer IV labetalol to lower the blood pressure
options.B: Notify the neurosurgeon immediately and prepare for emergency intervention
options.C: Perform a sternal rub to wake the patient
options.D: Document the findings and recheck in 15 minutes

hint: Cushing's triad is occurring.

rationales.correct: A dropping GCS combined with Cushing's triad (widened pulse pressure, bradycardia) indicates drastically increased intracranial pressure and impending brainstem herniation. Immediate surgical intervention (e.g., craniotomy) or osmotherapy is required.
rationales.incorrect: Lowering the BP rapidly drops cerebral perfusion. Noxious stimuli (sternal rub) increase ICP further. Waiting 15 minutes will likely result in death.

scenario: Which task can the Registered Nurse (RN) safely delegate to an Unlicensed Assistive Personnel (UAP)?

options.A: Take the first set of vital signs on a newly admitted patient
options.B: Assess a patient's pain level after giving Tylenol
options.C: Perform routine point-of-care blood glucose testing
options.D: Provide feeding assistance to a patient with new-onset dysphagia

hint: UAPs handle standard, non-invasive procedures that require no nursing judgment.

rationales.correct: Routine fingerstick glucose testing is a standard, procedural skill that requires no independent nursing judgment and is safe to delegate to a UAP.
rationales.incorrect: The FIRST set of vitals must be done by the RN to establish the baseline assessment. Pain assessment and feeding a NEW dysphagia patient (choking risk) are exclusively RN scopes of practice.

scenario: Which task is most appropriate for the RN to delegate to a Licensed Practical Nurse (LPN/LVN)?

options.A: Administer an oral scheduled antihypertensive medication
options.B: Administer an IV push dose of morphine
options.C: Create the initial nursing care plan for a diabetic patient
options.D: Perform the admission assessment on a new patient

hint: LPNs handle medication administration (excluding IV push/high-risk drips) and stable patients.

rationales.correct: Administering routine oral medications to stable patients is a standard responsibility well within the LPN scope of practice.
rationales.incorrect: IV push narcotics, initial care planning, and admission assessments are strictly restricted to the RN scope of practice.

scenario: A registered nurse cannot delegate tasks that involve the 'EAT' acronym. What does 'EAT' stand for in nursing delegation?

options.B: Emergencies, Administration of drugs, Transfers

hint: These require the critical thinking of the nursing process.

rationales.correct: EAT stands for Evaluate, Assess, and Teach. These are the core components of the nursing process requiring independent clinical judgment and can NEVER be delegated to an LPN or UAP.
rationales.incorrect: UAPs handle Eating, Ambulation, and Toileting. Administration of certain drugs is allowed for LPNs.

scenario: A float nurse from the pediatric unit is assigned to the adult Med-Surg unit. Which patient is the safest assignment?

options.A: A pt with a continuous IV heparin drip for a pulmonary embolism
options.B: A pt admitted 1 hour ago with diabetic ketoacidosis
options.C: A pt 2 days post-op from a cholecystectomy waiting to be discharged
options.D: A pt with a tracheostomy requiring frequent deep suctioning

hint: Give the float nurse the most stable, predictable patient.

rationales.correct: A stable post-op patient awaiting discharge requires standard basic nursing care (vitals, ambulation, basic teaching) and is the safest, most predictable assignment for a nurse unfamiliar with adult critical conditions.
rationales.incorrect: Heparin drips, DKA, and complex artificial airways require specialized adult critical care/med-surg assessment skills.

scenario: Two nurses have a conflict over holiday scheduling. They sit down and create a schedule where both nurses compromise to find a creative solution that fully satisfies both of their needs. What conflict resolution style is this?

options.B: Competing

hint: A true win-win solution.

rationales.correct: Collaborating is a 'win-win' conflict resolution style where parties work together to find a creative solution that fully satisfies the concerns of both sides.
rationales.incorrect: Avoiding is ignoring it. Competing is a win-lose (one forces their way). Accommodating is lose-win (one entirely gives up their needs for the other).

scenario: A patient falls out of bed but sustains no injuries. The nurse completes an incident report. How should this be documented in the medical record?

options.A: Document 'Patient fell out of bed. Incident report filed.'
options.B: Attach a copy of the incident report to the chart.
options.C: Document the objective clinical facts of the fall and the patient assessment.
options.D: Omit documentation of the fall since there was no injury.

hint: Incident reports are for internal risk management, not clinical records.

rationales.correct: The chart must reflect purely objective clinical facts (found on floor, vitals stable, MD notified).
rationales.incorrect: NEVER document that an incident report was filed, as this makes the internal document subpoenaable in a lawsuit. Failing to document the fall is falsification.

scenario: A nurse smells alcohol on a coworker's breath, and the coworker's speech is slurred. What is the priority action?

options.A: Tell the coworker to go sleep in the breakroom
options.B: Take over the coworker's patient assignment
options.C: Report the observation to the charge nurse or supervisor immediately
options.D: Wait for them to make a clinical error before reporting

hint: Patient safety is paramount and requires immediate removal via authority.

rationales.correct: An impaired nurse is a direct threat to patient safety. The observing nurse must report this immediately to management so the impaired nurse is removed from patient care duties safely.
rationales.incorrect: You cannot authorize them to sleep it off. Just taking their patients leaves them on the floor. Waiting for an error causes patient harm.

scenario: A patient signed the informed consent for an elective surgery yesterday. Today, as the transporter arrives, the patient says, 'I changed my mind. I don't want the surgery.' Action?

options.A: Tell the patient they already signed the legally binding contract
options.B: Send the patient to the OR and let the surgeon convince them
options.C: Cancel the transport and notify the surgeon immediately
options.D: Administer a sedative to calm the patient's nerves

hint: Consent is a continuous process, not just a piece of paper.

rationales.correct: A competent patient has the right to withdraw consent at ANY time, even right outside the operating room. The nurse must halt the process and notify the surgeon.
rationales.incorrect: Consent is not a binding contract. Forcing them to the OR is battery. Sedating a refusing patient is chemical restraint and battery.

scenario: A patient refuses to take their morning oral medications. What is the nurse's very first action?

options.A: Crush the medications and hide them in applesauce
options.B: Document the refusal and discard the medications
options.C: Assess the patient's reason for refusing the medications

hint: Always investigate the 'why' before taking administrative action.

rationales.correct: Patients have the right to refuse. The nurse must first explore the reason (e.g., side effects, swallowing difficulty, lack of understanding) to provide education or find alternatives.
rationales.incorrect: Hiding meds is battery/unethical. Documenting is required later, but assessment is first. The physician is notified after the nurse gathers assessment data regarding the refusal.

scenario: A competent, alert patient demands to leave the hospital Against Medical Advice (AMA) while an IV is still in place. What is the nurse's legal requirement?

options.A: Call security to restrain the patient
options.B: Remove the IV catheter immediately before they can walk out
options.C: Explain the medical risks of leaving without completing treatment
options.D: Refuse to provide the AMA form until the doctor arrives

hint: Ensure the patient is making an informed decision before they walk out.

rationales.correct: Before a patient leaves AMA, the nurse/provider must ensure they fully comprehend the medical risks of leaving. Only after this education (and asking them to sign the form) is the IV removed.
rationales.incorrect: Restraining them is false imprisonment. Removing the IV is done eventually, but education is first. You cannot hold them hostage waiting for a doctor.

scenario: A patient refuses a life-saving blood transfusion due to religious beliefs. The provider tells the nurse to administer it while the patient is asleep. Best action?

options.A: Administer the blood as ordered to save the patient's life
options.B: Refuse the order and document the patient's refusal of treatment
options.C: Wait until the patient's family arrives to get their consent
options.D: Administer normal saline and document it as blood

hint: Competent adults have an absolute right to bodily autonomy.

rationales.correct: A competent adult has the legal and ethical right to refuse any medical treatment. Administering it against their will is battery. The nurse must advocate for the patient, refuse the unsafe order, and document.
rationales.incorrect: Giving it is battery. Family cannot override a competent adult's refusal. Falsifying documents is illegal and unethical.

scenario: A patient asks the nurse, 'Am I dying? The doctor won't tell me.' The nurse knows the prognosis is terminal. The nurse replies, 'Yes, the treatments are no longer working.' Which ethical principle is the nurse upholding?

options.B: Veracity

hint: The obligation to tell the truth.

rationales.correct: Veracity is the ethical principle of truth-telling. The nurse is obligated to provide honest, accurate information to the patient.
rationales.incorrect: Beneficence is doing good. Justice is fairness in care delivery. Nonmaleficence is doing no harm.

scenario: Which situation best demonstrates the ethical principle of 'Nonmaleficence'?

options.A: Explaining the risks and benefits of surgery to a patient
options.B: Administering pain medication promptly when a patient requests it
options.C: Stopping a medication infusion that is causing a severe allergic reaction
options.D: Allowing a patient to refuse chemotherapy

hint: Do no harm.

rationales.correct: Nonmaleficence is the duty to do no harm. Stopping an active harm (allergic reaction) perfectly demonstrates this.
rationales.incorrect: Informed consent relates to Veracity/Autonomy. Pain meds relate to Beneficence (doing good). Refusal relates to Autonomy.

scenario: A patient with a history of cirrhosis is admitted with hepatic encephalopathy. The provider orders lactulose and rifaximin. What is the primary purpose of adding rifaximin to the regimen?

options.B: To decrease portal hypertension and prevent variceal bleeding
options.C: To destroy ammonia-producing bacteria in the gastrointestinal tract
options.D: To increase the osmotic effect of lactulose in the colon

hint: This is a localized antibiotic that targets the source of the neurotoxin.

rationales.correct: Rifaximin is a non-absorbable antibiotic that stays in the gut. It kills the normal flora bacteria that break down protein into ammonia, effectively lowering serum ammonia levels at the source.
rationales.incorrect: It does not treat systemic peritonitis (it stays in the gut). Propranolol prevents variceal bleeding. It works alongside lactulose but does not increase its osmotic mechanism.

scenario: A patient with acute pancreatitis is prescribed NPO status and an NG tube to low wall suction. The patient complains of severe, continuous abdominal pain. What is the best position to place the patient in?

options.B: Prone position with a pillow under the chest
options.C: Side-lying in a fetal position with knees drawn to the chest

hint: The pancreas is a retroperitoneal organ.

rationales.correct: The pancreas is retroperitoneal. The fetal position (or sitting up leaning forward) pulls the inflamed organ away from the posterior abdominal wall and reduces tension on the abdominal muscles, relieving pain.
rationales.incorrect: Lying supine or leaning backward stretches the abdominal muscles tightly over the inflamed pancreas, exacerbating the pain. Prone puts direct pressure on the abdomen.

scenario: A patient presents with sudden, severe tearing pain in the upper back and a blood pressure of 85/50. A pulsatile mass is palpated in the abdomen. What is the nurse's priority action?

options.B: Notify the surgical team and establish two large-bore IVs
options.C: Place the patient in a High-Fowler's position
options.D: Perform a deep abdominal palpation to measure the mass

hint: The aorta is rupturing.

rationales.correct: These are classic signs of a ruptured abdominal aortic aneurysm (AAA). This is a catastrophic hemorrhagic emergency requiring immediate vascular volume replacement and emergency surgery.
rationales.incorrect: Morphine delays life-saving action. High-Fowler's drops cerebral perfusion during severe hypotension. Deep palpation will completely rupture the aneurysm and cause instant exsanguination.

scenario: A patient with heart failure is receiving an IV infusion of milrinone. Which finding indicates the medication is achieving its intended therapeutic effect?

options.B: Increased heart rate with premature ventricular contractions
options.C: Increased cardiac output and decreased systemic vascular resistance

hint: Milrinone is a phosphodiesterase inhibitor (inodilator).

rationales.correct: Milrinone increases myocardial contractility (positive inotrope) and causes vasodilation (decreases afterload/SVR), which significantly increases cardiac output in severe heart failure.
rationales.incorrect: Decreased urine output indicates worsening HF. PVCs and extreme tachycardia are adverse effects. Milrinone lowers BP due to vasodilation; it does not raise it.

scenario: A patient is admitted with a suspected pulmonary embolism (PE). The provider orders a D-dimer test. What is the clinical significance of a negative D-dimer result in this patient?

options.A: It confirms the presence of a pulmonary embolism.
options.B: It highly rules out the presence of a pulmonary embolism.
options.C: It indicates the patient requires a stat CT angiogram.
options.D: It means the patient has a deep vein thrombosis (DVT) instead of a PE.

hint: This test measures fibrin degradation products.

rationales.correct: The D-dimer test is highly sensitive but not specific. A negative D-dimer reliably rules out the presence of a significant thrombus (like a PE or DVT).
rationales.incorrect: A positive D-dimer requires further testing (CT angiogram) to confirm a PE, but a negative D-dimer essentially excludes it without needing the CT. It does not differentiate between DVT and PE.

scenario: A patient with chronic kidney disease (CKD) complains of severe itching (pruritus). Laboratory results show an elevated serum phosphorus level. Which medication should the nurse anticipate administering?

options.B: Sevelamer hydrochloride

hint: Bind the mineral in the gut to prevent absorption.

rationales.correct: Pruritus in CKD is often caused by calcium-phosphate crystals depositing in the skin due to hyperphosphatemia. Sevelamer binds to dietary phosphorus in the GI tract, lowering serum levels and relieving the itching.
rationales.incorrect: Antihistamines are generally ineffective for uremic pruritus. Epoetin alfa treats anemia. Diuretics are ineffective in ESRD.

scenario: A patient post-thyroidectomy reports numbness and tingling around the mouth. The nurse taps the patient's cheek, resulting in facial muscle spasms. What is the priority intervention?

options.B: Assess the airway for stridor
options.D: Provide a paper bag for the patient to breathe into

hint: This is a positive Chvostek's sign.

rationales.correct: A positive Chvostek's sign and circumoral paresthesia indicate acute hypocalcemia from parathyroid damage during surgery. IV calcium gluconate is the direct antidote to prevent lethal tetany and seizures.
rationales.incorrect: While airway assessment is crucial, the specific symptom combination demands immediate calcium replacement. Potassium does not treat this. A paper bag is for hyperventilation (respiratory alkalosis).

scenario: A patient with a spinal cord injury at T4 experiences a sudden, pounding headache and profuse sweating above the nipple line. Blood pressure is 210/110. What is the immediate first action?

options.B: Check the Foley catheter for kinks
options.C: Elevate the head of the bed to 90 degrees

hint: Use gravity to immediately lower intracranial pressure and systemic blood pressure.

rationales.correct: This is autonomic dysreflexia. The absolute first action is to elevate the head of the bed to a high-Fowler's position to induce orthostatic hypotension, rapidly lowering the BP to prevent a stroke.
rationales.incorrect: Checking the catheter and bowel are the critical second steps. Antihypertensives are given only if mechanical relief fails.

scenario: A patient is admitted with Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA). The blood glucose is 450 mg/dL, and pH is 7.20. Which intravenous fluid should the nurse initiate first?

options.B: 5% Dextrose in Water (D5W)

hint: DKA causes profound osmotic diuresis leading to massive hypovolemia.

rationales.correct: Patients with DKA suffer from profound dehydration. The absolute first priority is rapid volume expansion with an isotonic fluid (0.9% NS) to restore perfusion and blood pressure before starting an insulin drip.
rationales.incorrect: 0.45% NS is hypotonic and used later. D5W worsens hyperglycemia initially. LR is not the standard initial resuscitation fluid for DKA.

scenario: A patient with a traumatic brain injury has an intracranial pressure (ICP) of 22 mmHg. Which nursing intervention is contraindicated?

options.A: Elevating the head of the bed to 30 degrees
options.B: Maintaining the head in a neutral, midline position
options.C: Performing routine endotracheal suctioning every hour

hint: Suctioning causes coughing and gagging, which drastically spikes pressure in the head.

rationales.correct: Routine suctioning is strictly contraindicated because it stimulates coughing and gagging, which causes severe spikes in ICP. Suctioning should only be performed PRN when strictly necessary.
rationales.incorrect: Elevating the HOB, maintaining midline head position (to promote venous drainage), and providing a quiet environment are all core interventions to decrease ICP.

scenario: A patient presents with an acute exacerbation of COPD. The ABG reveals: pH 7.28, PaCO2 65, HCO3 25, PaO2 55. What is the nurse's interpretation?

options.B: Partially compensated respiratory acidosis

hint: Look at the pH direction, the matching PaCO2, and whether the HCO3 has shifted.

rationales.correct: The pH is low (acidosis). The PaCO2 is high (respiratory cause). The HCO3 is normal (22-26), meaning the kidneys have not yet started retaining base to compensate. This is uncompensated respiratory acidosis.
rationales.incorrect: Partial compensation would show an elevated HCO3. Metabolic acidosis requires a low HCO3. Full compensation requires a normal pH.

scenario: A patient receiving a blood transfusion complains of severe lower back pain, chills, and hematuria 10 minutes into the infusion. What is the most likely reaction occurring?

options.B: Febrile non-hemolytic reaction

hint: Lower back pain points to kidney damage from red blood cell destruction.

rationales.correct: Flank/lower back pain and hematuria are the classic hallmarks of an acute hemolytic reaction caused by ABO incompatibility. The destroyed RBCs clog the renal tubules. The transfusion must be stopped immediately.
rationales.incorrect: Anaphylaxis causes wheezing/hives. Febrile causes fever/chills but no back pain/hematuria. TRALI causes severe respiratory distress.

scenario: A patient with a fractured femur develops sudden dyspnea, confusion, and a petechial rash on the upper chest. What does the nurse suspect?

options.B: Fat embolism syndrome

hint: The classic triad: hypoxemia, neurological decline, and petechiae.

rationales.correct: Fat embolism syndrome occurs 24-72 hours after long bone fractures. The classic triad is hypoxemia, neurological changes, and a petechial rash on the chest/axilla.
rationales.incorrect: DVT causes leg swelling. A standard PE causes dyspnea but no petechial rash. Compartment syndrome causes severe limb pain.

scenario: A patient with Addison's disease presents with vomiting, profound weakness, and a blood pressure of 80/40. Which medication must the nurse anticipate administering immediately?

options.B: IV hydrocortisone

hint: The patient is missing stress hormones.

rationales.correct: This is an Addisonian crisis. High-dose IV hydrocortisone is required immediately to replace missing glucocorticoids and restore vascular tone.
rationales.incorrect: Insulin worsens hypoglycemia. Epinephrine is for anaphylaxis. Oral meds are too slow for shock.

scenario: A patient with a continuous heparin infusion for a DVT has an aPTT of 120 seconds. The nurse notes blood oozing from the IV site. After stopping the infusion, what medication should the nurse anticipate giving?

options.B: Protamine sulfate

hint: This is the specific antagonist for unfractionated heparin.

rationales.correct: Protamine sulfate binds to and reverses the anticoagulant effects of heparin. An aPTT of 120 seconds with active bleeding requires immediate reversal.
rationales.incorrect: Vitamin K reverses warfarin. FFP contains clotting factors but is not the specific antidote for heparin. Tranexamic acid is an antifibrinolytic.

scenario: A patient has a chest tube connected to a water-seal drainage system. The nurse observes continuous bubbling in the water-seal chamber. What is the correct interpretation?

options.B: The patient's lung has fully re-expanded
options.C: There is an air leak in the system or pleural space
options.D: The suction pressure is set too high

hint: Intermittent bubbling is normal, but constant bubbling is not.

rationales.correct: Continuous bubbling in the water-seal chamber indicates an air leak, either from the patient's lung or a loose connection in the tubing. It requires troubleshooting to find the source.
rationales.incorrect: Normal function shows tidaling, not continuous bubbling. Full re-expansion causes tidaling/bubbling to stop entirely. High suction causes bubbling in the suction-control chamber.

scenario: A patient with severe acute pancreatitis exhibits Grey Turner's sign. Where on the patient's body does the nurse observe this finding?

options.A: A bluish discoloration around the umbilicus
options.B: A bluish discoloration on the bilateral flanks
options.D: A petechial rash across the chest

hint: You must 'turn' the patient to see this sign.

rationales.correct: Grey Turner's sign is a bluish ecchymosis (bruising) on the flanks. It indicates massive retroperitoneal hemorrhage associated with severe, necrotizing acute pancreatitis.
rationales.incorrect: Discoloration around the umbilicus is Cullen's sign. Jaundice is a liver sign. A petechial rash on the chest is a sign of Fat Embolism Syndrome.

scenario: A patient with suspected meningitis presents with a stiff neck. The nurse gently flexes the patient's neck to the chest, and the patient involuntarily flexes their hips and knees. What is this sign?

options.B: Positive Brudzinski's sign

hint: B = Brain/Neck.

rationales.correct: Brudzinski's sign is positive when severe neck stiffness (meningeal irritation) causes the patient's hips and knees to flex when the neck is flexed.
rationales.incorrect: Kernig's is inability to straighten the leg when the hip is flexed 90 degrees. Babinski is a foot reflex. Romberg tests balance.

scenario: A patient is admitted with Syndrome of Inappropriate Antidiuretic Hormone (SIADH). Which laboratory finding is expected?

options.B: Serum sodium of 120 mEq/L

hint: The body retains pure water, diluting the blood.

rationales.correct: SIADH causes excess water retention without sodium retention, leading to dilutional hyponatremia (sodium < 135 mEq/L).
rationales.incorrect: High sodium, dilute urine (1.002), and high serum osmolality indicate Diabetes Insipidus (water loss), the exact opposite of SIADH.

scenario: A patient with a cast on the right leg reports severe pain unrelieved by narcotics. The toes are pale, cool, and capillary refill is 4 seconds. First action?

options.A: Elevate the leg above the heart
options.B: Apply an ice pack to the cast

hint: Tissue ischemia is occurring due to trapped pressure.

rationales.correct: Unrelieved pain and poor perfusion indicate compartment syndrome, a surgical emergency requiring immediate fasciotomy or cast bivalving to save the limb.
rationales.incorrect: Elevation and ice cause vasoconstriction and decrease arterial flow, worsening ischemia. Narcotics mask symptoms.

scenario: A nurse is preparing to administer an IM injection into the ventrogluteal site. Which bony landmarks must the nurse palpate to locate this site safely?

options.B: Greater trochanter, anterior superior iliac spine, and iliac crest
options.C: Posterior superior iliac spine and ischial tuberosity
options.D: Lateral femoral condyle and greater trochanter

hint: Make a 'V' with your fingers on the side of the patient's hip.

rationales.correct: The ventrogluteal site is located by placing the palm over the greater trochanter, pointing the index finger toward the anterior superior iliac spine, and the middle finger toward the iliac crest.
rationales.incorrect: Acromion process = Deltoid. Posterior iliac spine = Dorsogluteal (unsafe). Femoral condyle = Vastus lateralis.

scenario: A patient is isolated for a Clostridium difficile infection. Which personal protective equipment (PPE) must the nurse don before entering the room?

options.B: Surgical mask, gown, and gloves
options.D: Shoe covers, hair net, and sterile gloves

hint: C. diff is spread by direct physical contact with spores.

rationales.correct: C. difficile requires Contact Precautions. The mandatory PPE for entering the room is a gown and gloves to prevent the transfer of spores to the nurse's clothing and hands.
rationales.incorrect: An N95 is for airborne precautions (TB). A surgical mask is for droplet precautions (Flu). Shoe covers and hair nets are for surgical/sterile environments.

scenario: A confused patient requires soft wrist restraints. How must the nurse secure the restraint ties?

options.A: Use a square knot attached to the side rails
options.B: Use a quick-release knot attached to the immovable bed frame
options.C: Use a slip knot attached to the mattress springs
options.D: Use a double knot attached to the headboard

hint: The knot must come undone instantly, and the attachment point must not move independently of the patient.

rationales.correct: Restraints must always be secured using a quick-release knot (slip knot) so they can be instantly removed in an emergency. They must be tied to the immovable portion of the bed frame.
rationales.incorrect: Tying to a side rail will violently yank the patient's arm if the rail is lowered. Square or double knots cannot be untied quickly in a fire. Mattress springs are not secure.

scenario: A patient with an indwelling Foley catheter is being transported to the radiology department. What must the nurse do with the drainage bag during transport?

options.A: Place it on the patient's lap
options.B: Hang it on the IV pole above the patient's chest
options.C: Empty the bag and hang it on the wheelchair frame below the bladder
options.D: Clamp the catheter tubing to prevent leakage

hint: Prevent stagnant urine from flowing backward into the bladder.
