scenario: A nurse is preparing to administer a large-volume cleansing enema. In which position should the patient be placed?

options.B: Left lateral Sims

hint: Follow the natural downward path of the descending colon.

rationales.correct: The left lateral Sims position aligns with the anatomy of the sigmoid and descending colon, allowing gravity to facilitate the flow of the enema solution deep into the bowel.
rationales.incorrect: Right lateral forces fluid upward against gravity into the descending colon. Supine and prone are less effective and uncomfortable.

scenario: A patient with left-sided weakness is learning to walk with a cane. What is the correct sequence for advancing the cane and legs?

options.A: Hold the cane in the left hand; advance the cane, then the left leg, then the right leg
options.B: Hold the cane in the right hand; advance the cane and the left leg simultaneously, then the right leg
options.C: Hold the cane in the right hand; advance the right leg first, then the cane
options.D: Hold the cane in the left hand; advance the right leg and cane simultaneously

hint: Cane Opposite Affected Leg (COAL).

rationales.correct: The cane must be held on the strong side (right hand). To walk, the patient advances the cane and the weak leg (left leg) forward at the same time to provide a wide base of support, then follows with the strong leg.
rationales.incorrect: Holding the cane on the weak side provides no support. Advancing the strong leg first leaves the patient balancing entirely on the weak leg.

scenario: A patient with a fractured right ankle is learning to walk up stairs with crutches. What is the correct sequence for going UP the stairs?

options.A: Move the right leg and crutches up first, then the left leg
options.B: Move the left leg up first, then bring the crutches and right leg up
options.C: Move both crutches up first, then hop up with the left leg
options.D: Move the right leg up first, then the left leg, then the crutches

hint: Up with the good, down with the bad.

rationales.correct: When going UP stairs, the patient must place the strong/unaffected leg (left leg) on the step first to bear the body's weight, followed by the crutches and the weak leg (right leg).
rationales.incorrect: Stepping with the weak leg or crutches first leaves the patient unable to lift their body weight against gravity, causing a fall backward.

scenario: A nurse accidentally administers 100 mg of metoprolol instead of the ordered 50 mg. What is the very first action the nurse must take?

options.B: Complete an incident report
options.C: Assess the patient's vital signs and clinical status
options.D: Inform the charge nurse of the error

hint: Patient safety comes before reporting.

rationales.correct: The absolute first action after any medication error is to assess the patient for any adverse effects (in this case, bradycardia and hypotension) to ensure their immediate safety.
rationales.incorrect: Notifying the provider is the second step, as the provider will need the patient's current vital signs to give further orders. Incident reports and notifying management come after clinical stabilization.

scenario: A 4-year-old child presents to the ED with a sudden high fever, drooling, severe sore throat, and leaning forward in a tripod position. Which nursing action is strictly contraindicated?

options.B: Examining the throat with a tongue depressor
options.C: Keeping the child calm on the parent's lap

hint: The airway is severely inflamed and highly irritable.

rationales.correct: The child has acute epiglottitis. Inserting anything into the mouth (tongue depressor, swab) can trigger reflex laryngospasm, causing instant, total airway obstruction and death.
rationales.incorrect: Intubation prep, keeping the child calm, and giving oxygen are all necessary and correct, life-saving interventions.

scenario: A 2-month-old infant with Tetralogy of Fallot becomes severely cyanotic and irritable while feeding (a 'Tet spell'). What is the nurse's first action?

options.B: Place the infant in a knee-chest position

hint: Alter the body's hemodynamics to force blood into the lungs.

rationales.correct: The knee-chest position kinks the femoral arteries, drastically increasing systemic vascular resistance. This physically forces blood to flow back through the pulmonary artery to become oxygenated, reversing the right-to-left shunt.
rationales.incorrect: Oxygen and morphine are the second and third steps after mechanical positioning. CPR is only for pulseless cardiac arrest, not cyanotic spells with a pulse.

scenario: A 4-year-old child is diagnosed with Kawasaki disease. Which treatment regimen is mandatory to prevent life-threatening complications?

options.B: Intravenous Immunoglobulin (IVIG) and high-dose aspirin

hint: The disease causes severe systemic vasculitis targeting the coronary arteries.

rationales.correct: Kawasaki disease is treated with a single large dose of IVIG and high-dose aspirin. This combination shuts down the immune-mediated vasculitis and prevents the formation of lethal coronary artery aneurysms.
rationales.incorrect: Antibiotics are useless against this autoimmune condition. Acetaminophen lacks the necessary antiplatelet effect. Acyclovir is for herpes viruses.

scenario: A toddler is diagnosed with Cystic Fibrosis. When should the nurse instruct the parents to administer pancreatic enzymes (pancrelipase)?

options.B: Crushed and dissolved in a warm drink
options.C: Immediately before or during every meal and snack
options.D: Only when the child has fatty, foul-smelling stools

hint: The enzymes must mix with the food in the gut.

rationales.correct: CF patients require pancreatic enzymes to digest food. The enzymes must be taken immediately before or during every meal and snack to be effective.
rationales.incorrect: After meals renders them useless. Heat and crushing destroy the enteric-coated enzymes. They are required daily, not PRN.

scenario: A child with sickle cell anemia is admitted with a vaso-occlusive crisis. The patient is crying with 9/10 joint pain. Which nursing intervention is the highest physiological priority?

options.B: Applying cold compresses to the affected joints
options.D: Teaching the patient distraction and relaxation techniques

hint: Treat the root cause of the ischemia (thick, sludgy blood).

rationales.correct: While pain management (PCA) is critical, aggressive IV hydration is the highest physiological priority. Hemodilution thins the viscous blood, allowing sickled cells to flow past the blockages, reversing the ischemia that is causing the pain.
rationales.incorrect: Cold compresses cause vasoconstriction, making the crisis worse (use heat). Distraction is insufficient for 9/10 ischemic pain. Pain meds are given simultaneously with fluids, but fluids fix the pathology.

scenario: A 9-year-old with Hemophilia A falls and severely bumps their knee. The joint rapidly swells. What is the absolute first medical intervention the nurse must anticipate?

options.B: Performing joint aspiration to relieve pressure
options.C: Administering PO ibuprofen for pain and swelling
options.D: Applying a warm heating pad to the knee

hint: Replace the missing protein in the clotting cascade.

rationales.correct: Hemophilia A is a deficiency of Factor VIII. The absolute first step for active bleeding (hemarthrosis) is replacing the missing clotting factor via IV to stop the internal hemorrhage.
rationales.incorrect: Aspiration before factor replacement will cause fatal bleeding. Ibuprofen blocks platelets and is strictly contraindicated. Heat worsens bleeding (RICE protocol: Rest, Ice, Compression, Elevation should be used instead).

scenario: A 9-month-old is admitted with severe colicky abdominal pain, drawing knees to the chest, and vomiting. The nurse notes 'currant jelly' stool. Diagnosis?

options.B: Hirschsprung's disease

hint: Telescoping bowel shears the mucosa.

rationales.correct: Intussusception (telescoping of the bowel) causes colicky pain and mucosal sloughing, resulting in stools mixed with blood and mucus (currant jelly).
rationales.incorrect: Pyloric stenosis features projectile vomiting. Hirschsprung's features chronic constipation. Appendicitis features constant RLQ pain.

scenario: A 4-week-old infant is constantly hungry and experiences forceful, non-bilious projectile vomiting after every feeding. Assessment reveals an olive-shaped mass in the RUQ. Which acid-base imbalance is expected?

options.B: Metabolic alkalosis

hint: The infant is continuously losing gastric acid.

rationales.correct: The infant has pyloric stenosis. The continuous loss of gastric hydrochloric acid through projectile vomiting leads to metabolic alkalosis and severe dehydration.
rationales.incorrect: Metabolic acidosis results from losing base (e.g., lower GI diarrhea). Respiratory imbalances result from lung/ventilation issues.

scenario: A newborn has not passed meconium in 48 hours and has abdominal distention. A rectal biopsy shows an absence of ganglion cells in the colon. Diagnosis?

options.B: Celiac disease

hint: Aganglionic megacolon.

rationales.correct: Hirschsprung's disease involves a lack of nerve cells (ganglion cells) in the lower colon. This halts peristalsis, causing stool to back up and a classic failure to pass meconium.
rationales.incorrect: Meconium ileus is related to CF. Celiac is gluten intolerance. NEC is bowel necrosis in premature infants.

scenario: A child is diagnosed with Celiac disease. The nurse teaches the parents to adhere strictly to the BROW acronym. What does BROW stand for?

options.B: Barley, Rye, Oats, Wheat

hint: These grains contain gluten or are highly cross-contaminated.

rationales.correct: Celiac disease requires a strict lifelong gluten-free diet. The BROW acronym specifies the grains to avoid: Barley, Rye, Oats (due to heavy cross-contamination), and Wheat.
rationales.incorrect: Rice, beans, corn, and fruits are naturally gluten-free and safe to consume.

scenario: A 4-year-old is admitted with nephrotic syndrome. Which laboratory finding is the defining characteristic of this disease?

options.B: Massive proteinuria

hint: The glomeruli become highly permeable to large molecules.

rationales.correct: Nephrotic syndrome is characterized by massive proteinuria (loss of protein in the urine). This leads to severe hypoalbuminemia (low protein in the blood), which causes fluid to shift into tissues, creating severe edema.
rationales.incorrect: Gross hematuria is a sign of acute glomerulonephritis. WBCs indicate infection (pyelonephritis). The serum albumin is extremely LOW, not high.

scenario: A child is admitted with acute post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis. What is the most critical assessment for the nurse to perform?

options.A: Monitoring blood pressure for severe hypertension
options.B: Assessing the throat for a return of the strep infection
options.D: Checking the urine for massive glycosuria

hint: The inflamed kidneys cannot filter fluid, leading to massive intravascular volume overload.

rationales.correct: Acute glomerulonephritis causes severe fluid retention. This hypervolemia can cause life-threatening hypertension and hypertensive encephalopathy. Frequent BP monitoring is critical.
rationales.incorrect: They experience weight GAIN (edema), not loss. The strep infection is past. Glycosuria indicates diabetes, not glomerulonephritis.

scenario: A 3-year-old child is scheduled for a lumbar puncture to test for meningitis. How should the nurse position the child?

options.A: Prone with a pillow under the abdomen
options.B: Supine with legs extended
options.C: Side-lying fetal position with knees pulled to the chest

hint: Open the spaces between the vertebrae.

rationales.correct: The side-lying fetal position curves the spine outward, maximizing the space between the lumbar vertebrae for safe needle insertion into the subarachnoid space.
rationales.incorrect: Prone, supine, and dangling do not adequately open the intervertebral spaces.

scenario: A 4-year-old boy attempts to stand by pushing his hands against his knees and 'walking' his hands up his thighs. The nurse documents Gowers' sign. What disease is this a hallmark of?

options.B: Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy (DMD)

hint: Progressive, ascending muscle weakness.

rationales.correct: Gowers' sign indicates severe proximal muscle weakness of the pelvic girdle, a classic hallmark of Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy.
rationales.incorrect: Cerebral palsy features spasticity. Spina bifida features lower limb paralysis. Arthritis features joint stiffness.

scenario: A neonate is born with a meningomyelocele (spina bifida). Because of their specific medical history, these children are at exceptionally high risk for developing a severe allergy to what substance?

options.B: Peanuts

hint: Frequent surgeries and urinary catheterizations expose them to this material constantly.

rationales.correct: Children with spina bifida require multiple surgeries and frequent urinary catheterizations from birth. This massive, repeated exposure to latex medical products creates a nearly universal risk for severe latex allergies.
rationales.incorrect: While they can develop other allergies, latex is the uniquely high-risk allergy inherently associated with the medical management of spina bifida.

scenario: To drastically reduce the risk of Sudden Infant Death Syndrome (SIDS), what is the most important instruction the nurse must give new parents?

options.A: Always place the infant on their back (supine) to sleep
options.B: Keep the room very warm and use heavy blankets
options.C: Place the infant on their stomach (prone) to prevent choking on spit-up
options.D: Use bumper pads in the crib to prevent head injury

hint: Back to sleep.

rationales.correct: The 'Back to Sleep' campaign established that the supine position on a firm mattress is the single most effective intervention to prevent SIDS.
rationales.incorrect: Overheating is a major SIDS risk factor. Prone positioning vastly increases SIDS risk. Bumper pads, pillows, and soft bedding are suffocation hazards and are strictly prohibited.

scenario: A neonate is being assessed in the nursery. The nurse performs the Ortolani maneuver and hears a palpable 'clunk'. What does this finding indicate?

options.B: Developmental Dysplasia of the Hip (DDH)

hint: The femur head is popping back into the acetabulum.

rationales.correct: A positive Ortolani maneuver (a palpable 'clunk' as the dislocated femoral head is reduced back into the hip socket during abduction) is the diagnostic hallmark of DDH in neonates. Treatment involves a Pavlik harness.
rationales.incorrect: It is not a normal finding. Spina bifida involves the spine. A femur fracture would cause crepitus and screaming, not a smooth clunk.

scenario: A child presents to the clinic with a high fever, cough, and conjunctivitis. The nurse examines the mouth and notes tiny white spots with a red halo on the buccal mucosa. What is the diagnosis?

options.B: Oral Candidiasis (Thrush)

hint: These are Koplik spots.

rationales.correct: Koplik spots (tiny white/blue spots on the inside of the cheeks) appear a few days before the full-body maculopapular rash breaks out and are the pathognomonic (definitive) sign of Measles.
rationales.incorrect: Scarlet fever has a strawberry tongue. Thrush features large white curd-like patches that bleed when scraped. HFMD features frank blisters.

scenario: A 6-month-old infant is admitted with Respiratory Syncytial Virus (RSV) bronchiolitis. What isolation precautions must the nurse implement?

options.A: Airborne precautions (N95 mask and negative pressure room)
options.B: Droplet and Contact precautions

hint: The virus is spread by large respiratory secretions and survives for hours on surfaces.

rationales.correct: RSV is highly contagious and spreads via large respiratory droplets and direct contact with contaminated secretions/surfaces. Droplet (mask) and Contact (gown/gloves) precautions are mandatory.
rationales.incorrect: It is not airborne (doesn't require negative pressure). Standard precautions are insufficient. Neutropenic is for protecting the patient from the nurse, not vice versa.

scenario: A child is diagnosed with Varicella (Chickenpox). The parents ask the nurse when the child can return to school. What is the correct guidance?

options.A: When the child has been entirely fever-free for 24 hours.
options.B: When all of the vesicular lesions have dried and crusted over.
options.C: Exactly 14 days after the first rash appeared.
options.D: After taking oral antibiotics for 48 hours.

hint: The fluid inside the blisters is highly contagious.

rationales.correct: Children with chickenpox are contagious until every single blister has ruptured, dried, and crusted over (usually about 6 days after the rash starts). Only then can they safely return to school.
rationales.incorrect: Fever-free alone does not mean they aren't contagious. A strict 14-day rule is inaccurate (it depends on the lesions). Antibiotics do not treat viruses.

scenario: A pregnant patient reports her last menstrual period (LMP) began on September 14. Using Naegele's rule, what is her estimated date of delivery (EDD)?

options.B: July 21 of the next year

hint: Subtract 3 months, add 7 days.

rationales.correct: Naegele's rule: Subtract 3 months from September (June), add 7 days to the 14th (21st). The EDD is June 21.
rationales.incorrect: The other dates are math errors based on incorrect application of the formula.

scenario: A pregnant patient has a history of one miscarriage at 10 weeks, one full-term infant (living), and preterm twins born at 34 weeks (both living). What is her GTPAL?
hint: Twins count as one pregnancy and one delivery event.

rationales.correct: Gravida = 4 (current + term + preterm + abortion). Term = 1. Preterm = 1 (twins are ONE delivery). Abortion = 1. Living = 3. GTPAL: 4-1-1-1-3.
rationales.incorrect: Counting twins as two parities (P) is a common error. They are one birth event.

scenario: A patient at 32 weeks gestation presents with painless, bright red vaginal bleeding. Which procedure is strictly contraindicated?

options.B: Digital vaginal examination

hint: The placenta may be covering the cervical opening.

rationales.correct: Painless, bright red bleeding indicates placenta previa. A digital vaginal exam can puncture the placenta, causing massive, lethal hemorrhage for both mother and fetus. Exams are strictly contraindicated.
rationales.incorrect: Ultrasound, fetal monitoring, and IV access are critical, safe interventions.

scenario: A patient at 38 weeks presents with severe, constant abdominal pain, a rigid, board-like abdomen, and dark red vaginal bleeding. Diagnosis?

options.B: Uterine rupture

hint: Pain + Rigid abdomen + Dark bleeding = Separation.

rationales.correct: Placental abruption occurs when the placenta detaches prematurely. It causes severe pain, a rigid abdomen (due to blood trapped in the uterine muscle), and dark red bleeding.
rationales.incorrect: Placenta previa is painless with bright red blood. Uterine rupture presents with loss of fetal station and cessation of contractions. Vasa previa is painless fetal bleeding.

scenario: A laboring patient's water breaks. The fetal heart rate suddenly drops to 70 bpm, and a pulsating cord is felt in the vagina. What is the immediate first action?

options.B: Use two sterile fingers to lift the fetal head off the cord
options.C: Push the cord gently back into the uterus
options.D: Place the patient in a supine position

hint: Relieve the mechanical compression on the lifeline.

rationales.correct: This is a prolapsed umbilical cord. The absolute first action is to manually lift the baby's head off the cord to restore blood flow while maintaining this position until an emergency C-section is performed.
rationales.incorrect: Oxygen is secondary. NEVER push the cord in (causes vasospasm). Supine position worsens compression (use knee-chest).

scenario: A patient with severe preeclampsia is receiving a magnesium sulfate infusion. Assessment reveals a respiratory rate of 10 breaths/min, oxygen saturation 90%, and absent deep tendon reflexes. What is the antidote?

options.B: Flumazenil

hint: This electrolyte directly antagonizes magnesium.

rationales.correct: The patient is in magnesium toxicity. The infusion must be stopped, and the specific antidote, calcium gluconate, administered to reverse respiratory and CNS depression.
rationales.incorrect: Naloxone reverses opioids. Flumazenil reverses benzos. Protamine reverses heparin.

scenario: The fetal heart monitor displays late decelerations. What is the physiological cause?

options.B: Umbilical cord compression

hint: VEAL CHOP.

rationales.correct: Late decelerations indicate uteroplacental insufficiency (the fetus is not getting enough oxygen from the placenta). This requires immediate intrauterine resuscitation (LIONS).
rationales.incorrect: Head compression = Early decels. Cord compression = Variable decels. Sleep cycles decrease variability, not decelerations.

scenario: The fetal monitor shows variable decelerations. What is the most appropriate first-line nursing intervention?

options.A: Turn the patient to her left or right side
options.B: Stop the oxytocin infusion

hint: Variable decelerations mean cord compression. Shifting the baby's weight relieves it.

rationales.correct: Variable decelerations are caused by umbilical cord compression. Changing the maternal position often shifts the fetus off the cord, instantly resolving the deceleration.
rationales.incorrect: Stopping oxytocin, giving fluids, and oxygen are secondary steps if positioning fails.

scenario: A mother who is Rh-negative delivers an Rh-positive infant. To prevent isoimmunization, the nurse must administer Rho(D) immune globulin (RhoGAM) within what time frame?

options.B: Within 72 hours postpartum
options.D: During the first trimester of her next pregnancy

hint: Stop the mother's immune system before it can finalize antibody production.

rationales.correct: RhoGAM must be administered to an Rh-negative mother within 72 hours of delivering an Rh-positive infant to prevent the formation of antibodies that would destroy future Rh-positive fetuses.
rationales.incorrect: While 24 hours is acceptable, the protocol limit is 72 hours. 6 weeks or the next pregnancy is far too late; irreversible sensitization will have already occurred.

scenario: A patient with an unruptured ectopic pregnancy is prescribed methotrexate. What instruction must the nurse provide?

options.A: Avoid taking folic acid supplements or prenatal vitamins
options.B: Drink at least 4 cups of milk daily
options.C: You will need to have your fallopian tubes tied
options.D: Expect severe shoulder pain over the next few days

hint: Methotrexate works by antagonizing a specific vitamin.

rationales.correct: Methotrexate is a folic acid antagonist used to stop the rapidly dividing cells of the ectopic pregnancy. Prenatal vitamins contain folic acid, which reverses the drug's effectiveness.
rationales.incorrect: Milk is irrelevant. Methotrexate preserves fertility. Severe shoulder pain indicates rupture (a surgical emergency), not an expected outcome.

scenario: A patient undergoes a D&C for a hydatidiform mole (molar pregnancy). What is the most critical post-operative teaching point?

options.A: You must use strict contraception for at least 1 year
options.B: Try to get pregnant again quickly to heal the uterus
options.C: You will need a hysterectomy to prevent cancer
options.D: You must undergo radiation therapy for 6 months

hint: Pregnancy hormones mask the growth of a specific cancer.

rationales.correct: Molar pregnancies carry a high risk of developing into choriocarcinoma. hCG levels must be monitored for 1 year. Pregnancy naturally raises hCG, masking cancer, making strict contraception mandatory.
rationales.incorrect: Getting pregnant ruins the hCG monitoring. Hysterectomy/radiation are only for confirmed cancer.

scenario: A patient is admitted in preterm labor at 28 weeks gestation. The provider orders betamethasone IM. What is the primary purpose of this medication?

options.B: To prevent maternal and fetal infection
options.C: To stimulate fetal lung maturation and surfactant production
options.D: To prevent neonatal neural tube defects

hint: Premature babies struggle to keep their alveoli open.

rationales.correct: Betamethasone is a corticosteroid given to the mother to stimulate the production of surfactant in the fetal lungs, drastically reducing the risk of respiratory distress syndrome in the premature infant.
rationales.incorrect: Tocolytics (like nifedipine or terbutaline) stop contractions. Antibiotics prevent infection. Folic acid prevents neural tube defects.

scenario: Two hours postpartum, a patient is bleeding heavily. The fundus is boggy, deviated to the right, and 2 cm above the umbilicus. Most appropriate first action?

options.B: Administer IM methylergonovine
options.C: Assist the patient to empty her bladder

hint: A mechanical obstruction is pushing the uterus out of place.

rationales.correct: A full bladder physically displaces the uterus (usually to the right), preventing it from clamping down. Emptying the bladder fixes the root mechanical cause.
rationales.incorrect: Massage will not keep the uterus firm if the bladder is full. Meds and calling the MD are secondary to emptying the bladder.

scenario: Thirty minutes after a vaginal delivery, a patient is bleeding heavily. The nurse palpates a soft, boggy fundus at the umbilicus. What is the immediate first action?

options.B: Empty the patient's bladder

hint: The uterus is midline but lacks tone.

rationales.correct: The uterus is boggy but midline. The immediate first action for uterine atony is vigorous fundal massage to physically stimulate the muscle to contract and clamp down on the bleeding vessels.
rationales.incorrect: Meds are given if massage fails. The bladder isn't the primary issue if the fundus is perfectly midline at the umbilicus. Calling the provider delays immediate physical intervention.

scenario: A patient with postpartum hemorrhage is prescribed methylergonovine (Methergine). Which condition is an absolute contraindication?

options.B: Hypertension

hint: This medication causes intense systemic vasoconstriction.

rationales.correct: Methylergonovine is a potent vasoconstrictor. It is strictly contraindicated in patients with hypertension or preeclampsia because it can precipitate a lethal hypertensive crisis or stroke.
rationales.incorrect: Asthma is a contraindication for Carboprost (Hemabate). Diabetes and C-sections are not contraindications.

scenario: A breastfeeding mother develops mastitis. Her breast is red, swollen, and painful, and she has a fever. What should the nurse teach her about feeding?

options.A: Stop breastfeeding immediately to prevent infecting the infant
options.B: Pump the affected breast and discard the milk
options.C: Continue breastfeeding frequently to completely empty the breast
options.D: Apply cold packs to the breast prior to feeding

hint: Milk stasis is the enemy of healing.

rationales.correct: Mastitis requires antibiotics and frequent, complete emptying of the breast. The milk is safe for the infant. Frequent feeding prevents abscess formation.
rationales.incorrect: Stopping feeding causes severe engorgement. Discarding milk is unnecessary. Warm packs (not cold) are used prior to feeding to dilate ducts and promote let-down.

scenario: A pregnant patient in her third trimester feels dizzy, clammy, and nauseated when lying flat on her back. What is the physiological cause?

options.A: Compression of the inferior vena cava by the uterus
options.B: Sudden drop in blood sugar

hint: Supine hypotensive syndrome.

rationales.correct: When a pregnant woman lies supine, the heavy gravid uterus compresses the inferior vena cava, drastically reducing venous return to the heart and causing maternal hypotension. Turning to the left side resolves it.
rationales.incorrect: It is a mechanical positional issue, not related to blood sugar, breathing, or an embolus.

scenario: A pregnant patient at 36 weeks tests positive for Group B Streptococcus (GBS). What is the mandatory intervention?

options.B: Administer IV antibiotics to the mother during labor
options.D: Administer IV antibiotics to the infant after birth

hint: Protect the baby as they pass through the birth canal.

rationales.correct: GBS is harmless to the mother but lethal to the infant. IV antibiotics (penicillin) are given during labor to ensure the baby is exposed to the drug while passing through the birth canal.
rationales.incorrect: Oral antibiotics prior to labor fail because the bacteria quickly repopulate. C-sections are not required. Treating the baby after birth is too late to prevent exposure.

scenario: At 1 minute of life, a newborn has a heart rate of 120, a lusty cry, active flexion, pulls away from suction, and has acrocyanosis (blue hands/feet). APGAR score?
hint: Acrocyanosis loses one point.

rationales.correct: HR > 100 (2). Cry strong (2). Active flexion (2). Reflex irritability (2). Color acrocyanosis (1). Total = 9.
rationales.incorrect: A perfect 10 requires a completely pink body. The baby is not compromised enough for an 8 or 7.

scenario: A patient with gestational diabetes delivers a 10 lb macrosomic infant. At 1 hour of life, the infant is jittery, twitching, and lethargic. Priority action?

options.A: Assess the infant's blood glucose level
options.B: Wrap the infant in warm blankets
options.D: Assess the infant for opioid withdrawal

hint: The maternal glucose supply is gone, but the baby's insulin is still high.

rationales.correct: Infants of diabetic mothers are at high risk for profound rebound hypoglycemia. Jitteriness and tremors are classic signs requiring an immediate heel stick to check blood sugar.
rationales.incorrect: Warming addresses cold stress but ignores the lethal glucose drop. Calcium treats hypocalcemia. Withdrawal is unlikely given the specific GDM history and macrosomia.

scenario: A mass casualty incident has occurred. Using the START triage method, a victim has a respiratory rate of 36 breaths per minute. Which tag should the nurse assign?

options.B: Yellow (Delayed)

hint: RPM: Respirations > 30 = Red.

rationales.correct: In START triage, any patient with a respiratory rate greater than 30 breaths per minute is immediately tagged Red (Immediate) because they are in respiratory distress and shock.
rationales.incorrect: Yellow is < 30 but cannot walk. Green is walking. Black is apneic even after airway repositioning.

scenario: A triage nurse assesses a disaster victim who is not breathing. The nurse repositions the airway, but the patient remains apneic. Which tag is assigned?

options.B: Yellow

hint: Resources cannot be spent on CPR during a mass casualty.

rationales.correct: If a patient remains apneic after a simple airway repositioning in a mass casualty event, they are tagged Black (Expectant/Deceased). Resources must be saved for survivable victims.
rationales.incorrect: If they started breathing after repositioning, they would be tagged Red. Yellow and Green patients are breathing spontaneously.

scenario: Which task can the Registered Nurse (RN) safely delegate to an Unlicensed Assistive Personnel (UAP)?

options.A: Provide feeding assistance to a patient with new-onset dysphagia
options.B: Perform routine point-of-care blood glucose testing
options.C: Educate a patient on how to use an incentive spirometer
options.D: Assess a patient's pain level after giving Tylenol

hint: UAPs handle standard, non-invasive procedures that require no nursing judgment.

rationales.correct: Routine fingerstick glucose testing is a standard, procedural skill that requires no independent nursing judgment and is safe to delegate to a UAP.
rationales.incorrect: New dysphagia requires an RN assessment during feeding. Education and Assessment are exclusively RN scopes of practice.

scenario: Which task is most appropriate for the RN to delegate to a Licensed Practical Nurse (LPN/LVN)?

options.A: Perform the admission assessment on a new patient
options.B: Administer an IV push dose of morphine
options.C: Perform a sterile dressing change on a central venous catheter
options.D: Create the initial nursing care plan for a diabetic patient

hint: LPNs handle complex, sterile, but predictable procedures on stable patients.

rationales.correct: Sterile dressing changes (including central lines on stable patients) are procedural tasks well within the LPN scope of practice.
rationales.incorrect: Admission assessments, IV push narcotics, and initial care planning are strictly restricted to the RN scope of practice.

scenario: A registered nurse cannot delegate tasks that involve the 'EAT' acronym. What does 'EAT' stand for in nursing delegation?

options.B: Evaluate, Assess, Teach

hint: These require the critical thinking of the nursing process.

rationales.correct: EAT stands for Evaluate, Assess, and Teach. These are the core components of the nursing process requiring independent clinical judgment and can NEVER be delegated to an LPN or UAP.
rationales.incorrect: UAPs handle Eating, Ambulation, and Toileting. Administration of certain drugs is allowed for LPNs.

scenario: Which patient requires the most immediate assessment by the charge nurse?

options.A: A patient with heart failure with 2+ pitting edema in both legs
options.B: A patient 6 hours post-thyroidectomy who says, 'My voice sounds squeaky and my neck feels tight.'
options.C: A patient with asthma requesting their PRN albuterol inhaler
options.D: A patient with diabetes who is sweating and has a blood sugar of 68 mg/dL

hint: The airway is physically closing.

rationales.correct: A squeaky voice (stridor) and neck tightness post-thyroidectomy indicate a rapidly expanding hematoma crushing the trachea. This airway emergency precedes all others.
rationales.incorrect: Edema is chronic HF. Asthma needing PRN meds is urgent but not currently in failure. Blood sugar of 68 is low, but easily fixed with juice by a UAP or LPN while the RN saves the airway.

scenario: A nurse receives report on 4 patients. Who should be seen FIRST?

options.A: A pt with pneumonia who has a temperature of 38.5 C (101.3 F)
options.B: A pt with heart failure who has new-onset frothy pink sputum
options.C: A pt with a kidney stone complaining of 9/10 flank pain
options.D: A pt with diabetes whose morning blood sugar is 160 mg/dL

hint: Frothy pink sputum means the lungs are actively filling with fluid.

rationales.correct: New-onset frothy pink sputum indicates acute pulmonary edema, a life-threatening airway/breathing emergency requiring immediate intervention (diuretics/oxygen).
rationales.incorrect: Fever in pneumonia is expected. 9/10 pain is important but not life-threatening (ABCs first). BS of 160 is slightly elevated but not an emergency.

scenario: A patient is 1 day post-op from an open cholecystectomy reporting sudden, severe shortness of breath. SpO2 is 86%. What is the most likely cause?

options.B: Pulmonary embolism

hint: Sudden dyspnea after surgery is a classic sign of a lethal clot.

rationales.correct: Sudden shortness of breath and hypoxemia post-op is the hallmark of a Pulmonary Embolism. Immobility and surgery are major risk factors.
rationales.incorrect: Asthma would have wheezing. Pneumonia develops over days with fever. Anxiety does not drop SpO2 to 86%.

scenario: A physician asks the nurse to obtain informed consent for a surgical procedure. What is the nurse's legal role in this process?

options.A: Explaining the risks and benefits of the surgery to the patient
options.B: Ensuring the patient understands the surgical alternatives
options.C: Witnessing the patient's signature voluntarily on the consent form
options.D: Determining if the patient truly needs the surgery

hint: The nurse acts as an advocate and a legal observer.

rationales.correct: The nurse's legal role in informed consent is purely to act as a witness that the patient signed voluntarily, was competent to sign, and that the signature is authentic.
rationales.incorrect: Explaining risks, benefits, and alternatives is the legal responsibility of the provider performing the procedure.

scenario: A patient wants to leave the hospital Against Medical Advice (AMA) while an IV is still in place. What is the nurse's priority action?

options.A: Call security to restrain the patient
options.B: Remove the IV catheter immediately before they can leave
options.C: Explain the medical risks of leaving without completing treatment
options.D: Refuse to provide the AMA form

hint: Ensure the patient is making an informed decision before they walk out.

rationales.correct: Before a patient leaves AMA, the nurse/provider must ensure they fully comprehend the medical risks of leaving. Only after this education (and asking them to sign the form) is the IV removed.
rationales.incorrect: Restraining them is false imprisonment. Removing the IV is done eventually, but education is first. You cannot refuse to let a competent adult leave.

scenario: A nurse smells alcohol on a coworker's breath, and the coworker's speech is slurred. What is the priority action?

options.A: Tell the coworker to go sleep in the breakroom
options.B: Take over the coworker's patient assignment
options.C: Report the observation to the charge nurse or supervisor immediately
options.D: Wait for them to make a clinical error before reporting

hint: Patient safety is paramount and requires immediate removal via authority.

rationales.correct: An impaired nurse is a direct threat to patient safety. The observing nurse must report this immediately to management so the impaired nurse is removed from patient care duties safely.
rationales.incorrect: You cannot authorize them to sleep it off. Just taking their patients leaves them on the floor. Waiting for an error causes patient harm.

scenario: Two nurses are riding in a crowded hospital elevator. One nurse says to the other, 'Did you hear that Mr. Smith in Room 402 tested positive for Syphilis?' What is the primary issue with this statement?

options.B: A severe HIPAA violation (Breach of Confidentiality)

hint: Discussing protected health information in a public space.

rationales.correct: Discussing a patient's protected health information (name, room number, diagnosis) in a public area where unauthorized individuals can hear is a direct and severe breach of HIPAA confidentiality laws.
rationales.incorrect: Slander requires the statement to be false (this is true, just confidential). It is not insubordination against management. Malpractice involves clinical care causing injury.

scenario: A nurse logs into the electronic health record (EHR) to check the lab results of their ex-spouse, who is admitted on a different floor. The nurse is not assigned to this patient. Consequence?

options.A: No consequence, as long as the nurse doesn't tell anyone
options.B: A minor reprimand for curiosity
options.C: A severe HIPAA violation resulting in potential termination and fines
options.D: It is permitted because healthcare workers have universal access

hint: You may only access records required for your direct job duties.

rationales.correct: Accessing the medical record of ANY patient you are not directly caring for is a major HIPAA violation. EHRs track every click, and this breach is grounds for immediate termination.
rationales.incorrect: There is no 'universal access' or 'curiosity' exemption. Silence does not negate the illegal unauthorized access.

scenario: A patient asks the nurse, 'Am I dying? The doctor won't tell me.' The nurse knows the prognosis is terminal. The nurse replies, 'Yes, the treatments are no longer working.' Which ethical principle is the nurse upholding?

options.B: Veracity

hint: The obligation to tell the truth.

rationales.correct: Veracity is the ethical principle of truth-telling. The nurse is obligated to provide honest, accurate information to the patient.
rationales.incorrect: Beneficence is doing good. Justice is fairness in care delivery. Nonmaleficence is doing no harm.

scenario: A patient with terminal cancer signs a DNR order. The patient stops breathing. The daughter screams at the nurse to 'Do everything and save him!' What is the nurse's ethical duty?

options.A: Begin CPR to prevent a lawsuit from the daughter
options.B: Honor the DNR order and provide comfort care only
options.C: Call the hospital ethics committee immediately while doing compressions
options.D: Ask the attending physician to overturn the DNR verbally

hint: A competent adult's legal directive supersedes family emotion.

rationales.correct: A DNR signed by a competent patient is a legally binding directive of their autonomy. Family members cannot override the patient's documented wishes. The nurse must honor the DNR.
rationales.incorrect: Initiating CPR is battery. The ethics committee isn't needed for a clear DNR. The MD cannot arbitrarily overturn it for family appeasement.

scenario: Which situation demonstrates the ethical principle of 'Nonmaleficence'?

options.A: Explaining the risks and benefits of surgery to a patient
options.B: Administering pain medication promptly when a patient requests it
options.C: Stopping a medication infusion that is causing a severe allergic reaction
options.D: Allowing a patient to refuse chemotherapy

hint: Do no harm.

rationales.correct: Nonmaleficence is the duty to do no harm. Stopping an active harm (allergic reaction) perfectly demonstrates this.
rationales.incorrect: Informed consent relates to Veracity/Autonomy. Pain meds relate to Beneficence (doing good). Refusal relates to Autonomy.

scenario: A hospital unit has seen a 25% increase in medication errors. The manager initiates a process to identify the systemic reasons for the failures, rather than blaming individuals. What is this process called?

options.B: Peer review

hint: Finding the fundamental breakdown in the system.

rationales.correct: Root Cause Analysis (RCA) is a retrospective quality improvement process used to identify systemic, underlying system failures that lead to adverse events, rather than assigning individual blame.
rationales.incorrect: Peer review evaluates individual performance. Risk avoidance is not a QI process. Disciplinary panels focus on punitive action.

scenario: A patient is declared brain dead. The family is arriving soon. Who should request organ donation from the family?

options.B: The attending physician
options.C: A trained representative from the Organ Procurement Organization (OPO)

hint: Requests require specialized training to avoid coercion and conflict of interest.

rationales.correct: Federal guidelines require that requests for organ donation be made exclusively by trained OPO professionals to ensure ethical, sensitive communication.
rationales.incorrect: Nurses and doctors should not initiate the request to avoid the perception of a conflict of interest (e.g., 'they stopped treating him to harvest organs').

scenario: During shift handover, the nurse uses the SBAR format. Under which section would the nurse state, 'I suggest we order a stat chest X-ray and a fluid bolus'?

options.B: Background

hint: What do you want the provider to do?

rationales.correct: In SBAR, 'Recommendation' is where the nurse explicitly states what they need from the provider (e.g., orders for tests, medications, or for the provider to come evaluate).
rationales.incorrect: Situation is the immediate problem. Background is history. Assessment is vital signs/clinical findings.

scenario: A patient falls out of bed but sustains no injuries. The nurse completes an incident report. How should this be documented in the medical record?

options.A: Document 'Patient fell out of bed. Incident report filed.'
options.B: Attach a copy of the incident report to the chart.
options.C: Document the objective clinical facts of the fall and the patient assessment.
options.D: Omit documentation of the fall since there was no injury.

hint: Incident reports are for internal risk management, not clinical records.

rationales.correct: The chart must reflect purely objective clinical facts (found on floor, vitals stable, MD notified).
rationales.incorrect: NEVER document that an incident report was filed, as this makes the internal document subpoenaable in a lawsuit. Failing to document the fall is falsification.

scenario: A patient with severe thermal burns over 40% of their body surface area is receiving fluid resuscitation. Which finding is the most reliable indicator that fluid replacement is adequate?

options.B: Blood pressure of 100/60 mmHg

hint: The kidneys provide the most accurate real-time assessment of core organ perfusion.

rationales.correct: In burn resuscitation, urine output is the gold standard for evaluating fluid status and organ perfusion. A urine output of 30-50 mL/hr (or 0.5 mL/kg/hr) indicates adequate vascular volume.
rationales.incorrect: Heart rate and blood pressure can be deceptively stable due to compensatory mechanisms. Edema is expected due to third-spacing but does not reflect intravascular volume.

scenario: A patient with Myasthenia Gravis is prescribed pyridostigmine. To prevent the most dangerous complication of this disease during meals, when should the nurse administer the medication?

options.B: 30 to 45 minutes before meals
options.D: First thing in the morning on an empty stomach

hint: The medication must peak when the patient's swallowing muscles are challenged.

rationales.correct: Pyridostigmine improves muscle strength by increasing acetylcholine. Giving it 30-45 minutes before meals ensures the medication peaks during eating, maximizing swallowing strength and preventing lethal aspiration.
rationales.incorrect: Bedtime dosing doesn't help with meals. Giving it after eating defeats the purpose. Empty stomach dosing ignores the timing related to the aspiration risk of meals.

scenario: A patient with End-Stage Renal Disease (ESRD) is prescribed sevelamer. The patient asks how this medication works. What is the nurse's best response?

options.A: It helps your kidneys excrete excess potassium.
options.B: It stimulates your bone marrow to produce red blood cells.
options.C: It binds to phosphorus in your food so it can be passed in your stool.
options.D: It replaces the calcium your failing kidneys cannot absorb.

hint: Renal failure causes a buildup of a mineral that reciprocally lowers calcium.

rationales.correct: Sevelamer is a phosphate binder. Because failing kidneys cannot excrete phosphorus, this medication binds to dietary phosphorus in the GI tract, preventing absorption and treating hyperphosphatemia.
rationales.incorrect: Polystyrene sulfonate removes potassium. Epoetin alfa stimulates RBCs. Calcium acetate provides calcium; sevelamer is non-calcium-based.

scenario: A patient with liver cirrhosis is receiving lactulose. Which clinical finding best indicates that the medication is achieving its therapeutic goal?

options.A: The patient has three soft bowel movements a day.
options.B: The patient's abdominal girth decreases by 2 inches.
options.C: The patient's level of consciousness improves and they are oriented to time and place.

hint: What is the ultimate complication caused by the toxin this drug removes?

rationales.correct: Lactulose traps ammonia in the gut for excretion. High ammonia causes hepatic encephalopathy (confusion, coma). The ultimate therapeutic indicator is an improved level of consciousness.
rationales.incorrect: While bowel movements prove the drug is working mechanically, the therapeutic goal is reversing encephalopathy. It does not treat ascites (girth) or directly increase urine output.

scenario: A patient is admitted with an acute myocardial infarction (MI). The provider orders IV morphine. Beyond pain relief, what is the primary cardiac benefit of morphine in an MI?

options.A: It dissolves the clot in the coronary artery.
options.B: It prevents the aggregation of platelets.
options.C: It acts as a venodilator, decreasing preload and cardiac workload.
options.D: It increases the heart rate to improve cardiac output.

hint: Morphine affects the blood vessels returning to the heart.

rationales.correct: Morphine relieves pain, which decreases sympathetic stress, but its primary hemodynamic benefit is venodilation. This reduces venous return (preload), decreasing myocardial oxygen demand and cardiac workload.
rationales.incorrect: Thrombolytics (tPA) dissolve clots. Aspirin/Clopidogrel prevent platelet aggregation. Morphine typically lowers heart rate by reducing anxiety, not increasing it.

scenario: A patient arrives in the ED with symptoms of an ischemic stroke that began 90 minutes ago. The patient's blood pressure is 200/110 mmHg. What is the priority nursing action before administering IV alteplase (tPA)?

options.A: Administer the tPA immediately, as the blood pressure is a compensatory mechanism.
options.B: Administer an IV antihypertensive to lower the blood pressure below 185/110 mmHg.
options.C: Wait until the 3-hour window has passed to see if the BP drops naturally.
options.D: Prepare the patient for an immediate craniotomy.

hint: High pressure increases the risk of bleeding into the brain once the clot-buster is given.

rationales.correct: For safe tPA administration, blood pressure must be strictly maintained below 185/110 mmHg to prevent hemorrhagic conversion of the ischemic stroke. An IV antihypertensive (like labetalol) is required first.
rationales.incorrect: Giving tPA at 200/110 risks fatal brain bleeding. Waiting wastes the 'golden window' for tissue salvage. Craniotomies are for bleeds/tumors, not initial ischemic strokes.

scenario: A patient with a pulmonary embolism is on a continuous heparin infusion. The aPTT comes back at 130 seconds, and the patient has epistaxis. What is the nurse's first action?

options.B: Administer protamine sulfate
options.D: Apply direct pressure to the nose

hint: Remove the source of the toxicity before giving the antidote.

rationales.correct: An aPTT of 130 is critically high, and epistaxis indicates active bleeding. The absolute first action is to stop the heparin infusion to halt further anticoagulation.
rationales.incorrect: Vitamin K is for warfarin. Protamine sulfate is the heparin antidote, but stopping the drip comes first. Direct pressure is a basic first aid measure but does not fix the systemic overdose.

scenario: A patient with ARDS is mechanically ventilated with a Positive End-Expiratory Pressure (PEEP) of 15 cm H2O. The patient suddenly becomes hypotensive. What is the physiological cause?

options.A: The PEEP is forcing fluid into the alveoli.
options.B: High intrathoracic pressure is compressing the vena cava, reducing venous return.
options.C: The patient is experiencing an allergic reaction to the ventilator tubing.
options.D: The PEEP is causing a massive vagal response.

hint: Pressure in the chest fights against blood trying to return to the heart.

rationales.correct: High PEEP (>10 cm H2O) drastically increases intrathoracic pressure. This pressure crushes the superior and inferior vena cava, severely decreasing venous return to the heart, which instantly drops cardiac output and blood pressure.
rationales.incorrect: PEEP pushes fluid OUT of the alveoli. Allergies and vagal responses are not the primary, direct mechanical consequence of high PEEP.

scenario: A patient is admitted in myxedema coma. The patient is profoundly lethargic with a respiratory rate of 6 breaths/min. What is the absolute priority intervention?

options.B: Apply a forced-air warming blanket

hint: Apply the ABCs.

rationales.correct: Myxedema coma causes severe hypoventilation and loss of airway protection (lethargy). According to ABCs, securing the airway and supporting ventilation (intubation) is the absolute first life-saving priority.
rationales.incorrect: IV levothyroxine is the specific treatment but requires a patent airway first. Active warming (blankets) is contraindicated as it causes fatal vasodilation. Fluids are secondary to Airway.

scenario: A patient with hyperthyroidism undergoes surgery. Post-operatively, the patient develops a temperature of 105 F (40.5 C), a heart rate of 150 bpm, and severe agitation. What condition is occurring?

options.B: Thyroid storm

hint: Manipulation of the gland caused a massive hormone surge.

rationales.correct: Thyroid storm (thyrotoxic crisis) is a hypermetabolic emergency caused by excessive thyroid hormone release, characterized by hyperpyrexia, extreme tachycardia, and agitation.
rationales.incorrect: Malignant hyperthermia happens during anesthesia induction. Infections take days to peak. Hypocalcemia causes spasms/tetany, not extreme fever.

scenario: A patient with a fractured tibia has a newly applied fiberglass cast. The patient complains of excruciating pain unrelieved by IV narcotics, and the toes are pale and pulseless. What is this complication?

options.B: Fat embolism syndrome

hint: The '6 Ps': Pain, Pallor, Pulselessness, Paresthesia, Paralysis, Poikilothermia.

rationales.correct: Compartment syndrome occurs when pressure within the muscle fascia (or under a tight cast) exceeds capillary perfusion pressure, causing ischemia, severe pain, pallor, and pulselessness. It requires immediate cast bivalving or fasciotomy.
rationales.incorrect: DVT causes swelling and calf pain. Fat embolism causes dyspnea and petechiae. Osteomyelitis is a chronic bone infection.

scenario: A patient recovering from a femur fracture suddenly becomes severely short of breath, confused, and develops a petechial rash across the chest and axillae. What is the likely diagnosis?

options.B: Acute respiratory distress syndrome

hint: The classic triad: hypoxemia, neuro decline, and petechiae.

rationales.correct: Fat Embolism Syndrome (FES) typically occurs 24-72 hours after long bone fractures. Marrow fat enters the bloodstream, causing the classic triad of respiratory distress, altered mental status, and a petechial rash.
rationales.incorrect: A standard DVT-induced PE causes dyspnea but no petechiae. ARDS and Anaphylaxis lack the petechial rash associated with long bone trauma.

scenario: A patient with a history of peptic ulcer disease suddenly develops a rigid, board-like abdomen and severe pain radiating to the right shoulder. What is the priority action?

options.B: Insert a nasogastric tube to decompress the stomach
options.C: Notify the provider and prepare for emergency surgery
options.D: Place the patient in a supine position

hint: Gastric contents have spilled into the peritoneal cavity.

rationales.correct: A rigid, board-like abdomen and referred shoulder pain (phrenic nerve irritation) indicate a perforated ulcer causing chemical peritonitis. This is a surgical emergency.
rationales.incorrect: Antacids are useless for a hole in the stomach. NG tube insertion can worsen the tear. The patient should sit up slightly to pool contents downward, not lie supine.

scenario: A patient is admitted with a small bowel obstruction. They are experiencing severe nausea and vomiting of feculent matter. What is the priority nursing intervention?

options.B: Insert a nasogastric tube for decompression
options.C: Prepare the patient for a colonoscopy

hint: Relieve the pressure building up above the blockage.

rationales.correct: In a bowel obstruction, gastric contents, gas, and fluids back up, causing severe distention and vomiting. Inserting an NG tube to suction decompresses the stomach, relieves nausea, and prevents fatal aspiration.
rationales.incorrect: Enemas and oral laxatives are strictly contraindicated in mechanical obstructions (they can cause rupture). Colonoscopy is for lower bowel issues, not immediate stabilization.

scenario: A patient with Addison's disease is brought in unresponsive with a blood pressure of 70/40 mmHg. Which combination of IV fluid and medication is the absolute priority?

options.B: 0.9% NS and high-dose hydrocortisone

hint: The patient lacks aldosterone (salt/water loss) and cortisol.

rationales.correct: Addisonian crisis features profound hypovolemia, hyponatremia, and lack of glucocorticoids. Rapid fluid resuscitation with isotonic 0.9% Normal Saline and high-dose IV hydrocortisone is life-saving.
rationales.incorrect: Insulin worsens hypoglycemia. LR contains potassium, which is contraindicated (Addison's causes hyperkalemia). Epinephrine and levothyroxine treat other endocrine/shock issues.

scenario: A patient with Cushing's syndrome is admitted to the unit. Based on the disease pathology, which nursing diagnosis is the highest priority?

options.B: Risk for Hypoglycemia

hint: Excessive cortisol profoundly suppresses the immune system.

rationales.correct: Hypercortisolism severely suppresses the immune response. Patients are at critical risk for overwhelming infections, which may be 'silent' (lacking normal fever/inflammation signs).
rationales.incorrect: Body image (moon face) is psychosocial. Cushing's causes HYPERglycemia. Mobility is a lesser priority than the immediate life-threat of infection.

scenario: A patient with a spinal cord injury at T4 reports a sudden, pounding headache. The nurse notes profound diaphoresis above the lesion level and a BP of 210/110. What is the immediate FIRST action?

options.B: Elevate the head of the bed to 90 degrees
options.D: Check the rectum for an impaction

hint: Use gravity to instantly drop the blood pressure.

rationales.correct: The patient is experiencing autonomic dysreflexia. The absolute first action is to elevate the head of the bed to a high-Fowler's position to induce orthostatic hypotension, rapidly lowering the BP to prevent a stroke.
rationales.incorrect: Checking the bladder and bowel are the critical second steps to remove the noxious stimulus. Antihypertensives are given only if mechanical relief fails.

scenario: A patient with DKA is receiving an IV insulin infusion. The blood glucose drops to 240 mg/dL, but the anion gap remains high. What should the nurse do?

options.B: Add 5% dextrose to the IV fluids while continuing the insulin
options.C: Administer 1 ampule of IV sodium bicarbonate
options.D: Switch the insulin drip to subcutaneous glargine

hint: The body still needs insulin to clear the ketones, but you must prevent hypoglycemia.

rationales.correct: When blood glucose falls below 250 mg/dL but DKA is not resolved, dextrose must be added to the IV fluids. This prevents fatal hypoglycemia while allowing the insulin drip to continue until ketones are fully cleared.
rationales.incorrect: Stopping insulin causes an instant rebound into severe DKA. Bicarbonate is for severe acidosis. Subcutaneous insulin is for transition later.

scenario: A patient with Hyperosmolar Hyperglycemic State (HHS) has a blood glucose of 900 mg/dL and is severely lethargic. What is the absolute first step in management?

options.A: Administer an IV bolus of Regular Insulin
options.B: Initiate rapid IV fluid resuscitation with 0.9% Normal Saline

hint: HHS causes extreme osmotic diuresis leading to profound, life-threatening dehydration.

rationales.correct: Patients with HHS suffer from massive fluid deficits (often 8-10 Liters). The absolute first priority is aggressive fluid resuscitation to restore intravascular volume and cerebral perfusion before giving insulin.
rationales.incorrect: Giving insulin before fluids drives water into the cells, worsening vascular collapse. Bicarbonate is for severe acidosis (DKA). SQ insulin is too slow.

scenario: A patient with heart failure is taking digoxin. Which symptom is a classic, specific indicator of digoxin toxicity?

options.B: Yellow-green halos around lights

hint: Visual disturbances are a hallmark.

rationales.correct: Visual changes, specifically seeing yellow-green halos around objects, along with nausea, vomiting, and bradycardia, are classic signs of digoxin toxicity.
rationales.incorrect: Dry cough is from ACE inhibitors. Pitting edema is a sign of heart failure itself. Toxicity causes bradycardia (<60), not 90 bpm.

scenario: A nurse accidentally sustains a needle stick from a hollow-bore needle used on a patient. What is the absolute first action the nurse must take?

options.A: Report the incident to the charge nurse
options.B: Wash the puncture site thoroughly with soap and water
options.C: Go to occupational health for an HIV test
options.D: Squeeze the finger vigorously to bleed out the virus

hint: Basic first aid takes precedence over administration.

rationales.correct: The absolute first action is to decontaminate the wound by washing it with soap and running water to physically flush away potential pathogens.
rationales.incorrect: Reporting and occupational health follow first aid. Squeezing/milking the wound damages tissues and can actually increase pathogen absorption.

scenario: A fire breaks out in a patient's room. According to the RACE protocol, what is the very first action the nurse must take?

options.A: Pull the fire alarm in the hallway
options.B: Close the door to the patient's room to confine the smoke
options.C: Rescue the patient by moving them out of the room
options.D: Extinguish the fire using the nearest extinguisher

hint: Life safety always takes priority over property or system activation.

rationales.correct: RACE stands for Rescue, Alarm, Confine, Extinguish. The absolute first action is to Rescue anyone in immediate danger by pulling the patient out of the room.
rationales.incorrect: Activating the alarm, confining the smoke, and extinguishing the fire are vital but must wait until the patient is safely removed from the hazard.

scenario: When operating a fire extinguisher using the PASS acronym, what does the 'P' stand for?

options.B: Pull the pin

hint: You cannot operate the extinguisher until the safety mechanism is removed.

rationales.correct: PASS stands for Pull the pin, Aim at the base of the fire, Squeeze the handle, Sweep from side to side.
rationales.incorrect: Pressing, pointing, and pushing are incorrect terminology for the standard acronym.

scenario: The nurse is inserting a Foley catheter into a female patient. The catheter accidentally slips into the vagina. What is the correct next step?

options.A: Remove the catheter, wipe it with an alcohol swab, and insert it into the urethra
options.B: Leave the catheter in the vagina as a landmark and insert a new sterile catheter into the urethra
options.C: Remove the catheter and blindly angle the next attempt higher up
options.D: Call the physician to perform the catheterization

hint: The vagina is not sterile. You need to know where NOT to go on the second attempt.

rationales.correct: If a catheter enters the vagina, it is contaminated. Leaving it in place serves as a visual landmark so the nurse does not accidentally enter the vagina again. The nurse must use a brand-new sterile catheter to enter the urethra.
rationales.incorrect: Reusing a contaminated catheter introduces vaginal flora into the sterile bladder, causing CAUTIs. Starting completely over removes the helpful landmark.

scenario: A nurse needs to measure a patient for a nasogastric (NG) tube. What is the correct technique for determining the length of insertion?

options.A: Measure from the tip of the nose, to the top of the ear, to the umbilicus
options.B: Measure from the tip of the nose, to the earlobe, to the xiphoid process
options.C: Measure from the chin, to the earlobe, to the bottom of the sternum
options.D: Measure a standard 50 cm for all adult patients

hint: NEX method.

rationales.correct: The standard NEX method (Nose-Ear-Xiphoid) is used. Measure from the tip of the nose, to the earlobe, and down to the xiphoid process. This approximates the distance to the stomach.
rationales.incorrect: Measuring to the umbilicus goes too deep (into the intestines). A standard 50 cm does not account for anatomical differences between patients.

scenario: A patient is receiving a continuous enteral feeding via a nasogastric tube. What is the most critical nursing intervention to prevent aspiration pneumonia?

options.A: Keep the head of the bed elevated at 30-45 degrees at all times
options.B: Flush the tube with 30 mL of cranberry juice every 4 hours
options.C: Change the feeding bag and tubing every 48 hours
options.D: Check the pH of the gastric aspirate once a week

hint: Gravity prevents fluid from traveling up the esophagus into the trachea.

rationales.correct: Keeping the head of the bed elevated between 30 and 45 degrees (semi-Fowler's) is the primary mechanical defense against gastric reflux and fatal pulmonary aspiration during continuous tube feedings.
rationales.incorrect: Juice curdles formula. Tubing is changed every 24 hours. pH must be checked prior to intermittent feeding, not weekly.

scenario: A patient is receiving a continuous enteral feeding via an NG tube. The nurse checks the gastric residual volume (GRV) and aspirates 450 mL. The patient complains of nausea. Next action?

options.A: Discard the residual and stop the feeding completely
options.B: Return the residual, hold the feeding, and notify the provider
options.C: Return the residual and increase the feeding rate to flush the stomach
options.D: Discard the residual and administer IV antiemetics

hint: High volume indicates delayed emptying, but the fluid contains essential electrolytes.

rationales.correct: A GRV >250-500 mL with symptoms (nausea) indicates delayed gastric emptying and high aspiration risk. The feeding must be stopped and the MD notified. The residual MUST be returned to prevent severe metabolic alkalosis and hypokalemia.
rationales.incorrect: Discarding residuals causes severe electrolyte derangements. Increasing the rate risks fatal pulmonary aspiration.

scenario: A patient's IV site is cool to the touch, swollen, and pale. The IV infusion rate has slowed significantly. What complication does the nurse suspect?

options.B: Infiltration

hint: Non-vesicant fluid is leaking into the subcutaneous tissue.

rationales.correct: Infiltration occurs when an IV catheter slips out of the vein, allowing fluid to leak into surrounding tissue. This causes the site to become cool (due to room temperature fluid), pale, and swollen.
rationales.incorrect: Phlebitis causes the site to be red, warm, and painful. Extravasation involves tissue-destroying drugs. Infection causes purulent drainage and warmth.

scenario: A patient's peripheral IV site is red, warm to the touch, and painfully tender along the vein pathway. The nurse suspects which complication?

options.B: Phlebitis

hint: Inflammation of the blood vessel.

rationales.correct: Phlebitis is the inflammation of a vein, characterized by redness, warmth, tenderness, and a palpable cord along the vein. The IV must be removed immediately.
rationales.incorrect: Infiltration presents as cool, pale, and swollen. Extravasation is tissue necrosis from a vesicant drug. Air embolism causes systemic respiratory distress.

scenario: The nurse is administering an IV vesicant chemotherapy drug. The patient complains of burning, and the nurse notes swelling and pallor at the site. What is the FIRST action the nurse must take?

options.B: Stop the infusion pump.
options.C: Aspirate the drug from the catheter.
options.D: Inject the specific antidote into the site.

hint: Halt the delivery of the damaging agent before taking any other steps.

rationales.correct: The patient is experiencing extravasation. The absolute first action is to stop the infusion pump immediately to prevent any more of the tissue-destroying vesicant from entering the subcutaneous tissue.
rationales.incorrect: Removing the catheter immediately eliminates the best route to deliver the antidote into the exact tissue space. Aspiration and antidote administration are steps 2 and 3.

scenario: A patient receiving a blood transfusion complains of severe itching, hives, and wheezing 10 minutes into the infusion. What type of reaction is occurring?

options.B: Febrile non-hemolytic reaction

hint: These symptoms mirror a severe peanut or bee-sting allergy.

rationales.correct: Hives (urticaria), itching (pruritus), and wheezing (bronchospasm) are classic signs of an allergic/anaphylactic reaction to plasma proteins in the donor blood. Stop the blood and prepare epinephrine.
rationales.incorrect: Hemolytic causes back pain/shock. Febrile causes fever/chills. TRALI causes dyspnea and hypotension, but not hives.

scenario: A patient receiving a blood transfusion complains of severe lower back pain, chills, and hematuria 15 minutes into the infusion. What type of reaction is occurring?

options.B: Anaphylactic reaction

hint: The donor cells are being destroyed, and the debris is clogging the kidneys.

rationales.correct: Flank/lower back pain and hematuria are the classic hallmarks of an acute hemolytic reaction caused by ABO incompatibility. The destroyed RBCs clog the renal tubules. The transfusion must be stopped immediately.
rationales.incorrect: Febrile causes fever/chills but no back pain/hematuria. Anaphylaxis causes wheezing/hives. TRALI causes severe respiratory distress.

scenario: Within 2 hours of initiating a packed red blood cell (PRBC) transfusion, the patient develops sudden severe dyspnea, frothy sputum, bounding pulses, and a BP of 160/90. Diagnosis?

options.B: Transfusion-associated circulatory overload (TACO)

hint: The heart cannot handle the volume, pushing fluid into the alveoli.

rationales.correct: Dyspnea, frothy sputum (pulmonary edema), bounding pulses, and hypertension indicate TACO (fluid overload). The infusion rate was too fast for the patient's cardiovascular system to handle.
rationales.incorrect: TRALI presents with severe dyspnea but typically HYPOtension and fever. Hemolytic reactions feature back pain/fever. Anaphylaxis features wheezing and hives.

scenario: When setting up a sterile field, the nurse accidentally touches the outer 1/2-inch edge of the sterile drape with an unsterile bare hand. What is the nurse's action?

options.A: Consider the entire sterile field contaminated and start over
options.B: Continue setting up the field, as the outer 1-inch border is already considered unsterile
options.C: Pour sterile saline over the touched area to clean it
options.D: Place a new sterile drape directly over the touched area

hint: The edges of the drape rest on the unsterile table.

rationales.correct: The outer 1-inch (2.5 cm) border of any sterile drape is universally considered contaminated because it touches the unsterile surface. Touching this specific 1-inch border does not contaminate the rest of the inner sterile field.
rationales.incorrect: Starting over is unnecessary if only the 1-inch border was touched. Saline does not sterilize. You cannot simply cover a contaminated area with another drape.

scenario: What is the correct sequence for donning personal protective equipment (PPE)?

options.B: Gown, mask, goggles, gloves

hint: Work from the bottom up, securing the outer layer first, and covering your hands last.

rationales.correct: The standard sequence for donning PPE is Gown first, followed by the Mask/Respirator, then Goggles/Face Shield, and finally Gloves (pulled over the gown cuffs).
rationales.incorrect: Putting gloves on first contaminates them while tying the gown. Masks must go under goggles.

scenario: When removing (doffing) PPE, which item should be removed first?

options.B: Goggles

hint: Remove the most heavily contaminated item first.

rationales.correct: Gloves are considered the most contaminated piece of PPE and should be removed first (or simultaneously with the gown if tearing it away).
rationales.incorrect: Removing the mask first exposes the mucous membranes to contaminated hands/gloves.

scenario: A patient is isolated for a Clostridium difficile infection. Which hand hygiene method is mandatory for the nurse?

options.B: Washing hands with soap and water
options.D: Hand hygiene is not required if gloves were worn

hint: Spores resist chemical destruction.

rationales.correct: C. difficile forms hardy spores that alcohol cannot kill. Handwashing with soap and water is mandatory to physically scrub and flush the spores down the drain.
rationales.incorrect: Alcohol sanitizers and chlorhexidine wipes are completely ineffective against C. diff spores. Hand hygiene is always required after glove removal.

scenario: A nurse is preparing to enter the room of a patient with active pulmonary tuberculosis. Which specific piece of PPE is mandatory?

options.B: A fit-tested N95 respirator

hint: TB requires airborne precautions to filter tiny droplet nuclei.

rationales.correct: Tuberculosis is spread via tiny airborne droplet nuclei. A fit-tested N95 respirator or a PAPR is mandatory to filter these microscopic particles.
rationales.incorrect: Surgical gowns and face shields protect against splashes, not airborne nuclei. A standard surgical mask is used for droplet precautions (like flu), but its pores are too large to stop TB.

scenario: A patient is prescribed an intramuscular (IM) injection of iron dextran. The nurse utilizes the Z-track method. What is the primary purpose of this technique?

options.A: To decrease the pain of the injection
options.B: To prevent the medication from leaking back into the subcutaneous tissue and staining the skin
options.C: To ensure rapid absorption into the bloodstream
options.D: To avoid hitting the sciatic nerve

hint: Pulling the skin sideways seals the puncture path.

rationales.correct: The Z-track method displaces the skin and subcutaneous tissue before injection. When released, the tissue slides back, creating a staggered pathway that traps the medication deep in the muscle, preventing leakage and skin staining (e.g., with Iron).
rationales.incorrect: It does not decrease pain, speed up absorption, or prevent hitting nerves (site selection prevents hitting nerves).

scenario: A patient with a nasogastric tube is prescribed a sustained-release antihypertensive medication. What is the most appropriate action by the nurse?

options.A: Crush the pill thoroughly and mix it with 30 mL of warm water
options.B: Dissolve the intact pill in 50 mL of normal saline over 30 minutes
options.C: Administer the pill whole down the NG tube
options.D: Hold the medication and request a liquid or alternative formulation from the provider

hint: The delivery mechanism of the drug cannot be altered.

rationales.correct: Sustained-release (and enteric-coated) medications cannot be crushed because it destroys the time-release mechanism, leading to a massive overdose upfront. An alternative formulation must be requested.
rationales.incorrect: Crushing it causes overdose. Dissolving it takes too long and may clog the tube. Giving it whole will instantly occlude the NG tube.

scenario: A 4-year-old child presents to the ED with sudden onset high fever, drooling, severe sore throat, and leaning forward in a tripod position. Which nursing action is strictly contraindicated?

options.B: Examining the throat with a tongue depressor
options.C: Keeping the child calm on the parent's lap

hint: The airway is severely inflamed and highly irritable.

rationales.correct: The child has acute epiglottitis. Inserting anything into the mouth (tongue depressor, swab) can trigger reflex laryngospasm, causing instant, total airway obstruction and death.
rationales.incorrect: Intubation prep, keeping the child calm, and giving oxygen are all necessary and correct, life-saving interventions.

scenario: A 2-year-old child presents with a barking, seal-like cough and mild inspiratory stridor at rest. The diagnosis is viral croup. What is the most effective initial non-pharmacologic intervention?

options.B: Perform chest physiotherapy

hint: Reduce the swelling of the subglottic area gently.

rationales.correct: Cool mist humidification constricts the swollen blood vessels in the upper airway, reducing subglottic edema and easing the stridor and barking cough of croup.
rationales.incorrect: Chest PT is for lower airway secretions (like CF). Forceful coughing irritates the airway further. A 2-year-old cannot safely gargle.

scenario: A 6-month-old infant is admitted with Respiratory Syncytial Virus (RSV) bronchiolitis. What isolation precautions must the nurse implement?

options.A: Airborne precautions (N95 mask and negative pressure room)
options.B: Droplet and Contact precautions

hint: The virus is spread by large respiratory secretions and survives for hours on surfaces.

rationales.correct: RSV is highly contagious and spreads via large respiratory droplets and direct contact with contaminated secretions/surfaces. Droplet (mask) and Contact (gown/gloves) precautions are mandatory.
rationales.incorrect: It is not airborne (doesn't require negative pressure). Standard precautions are insufficient. Neutropenic is for protecting the patient from the nurse, not vice versa.

scenario: A toddler is diagnosed with Cystic Fibrosis. When should the nurse instruct the parents to administer pancreatic enzymes (pancrelipase)?

options.B: Crushed and dissolved in a warm drink
options.C: Immediately before or during every meal and snack
options.D: Only when the child has fatty, foul-smelling stools

hint: The enzymes must mix with the food in the gut.

rationales.correct: CF patients require pancreatic enzymes to digest food. The enzymes must be taken immediately before or during every meal and snack to be effective.
rationales.incorrect: After meals renders them useless. Heat and crushing destroy the enteric-coated enzymes. They are required daily, not PRN.

scenario: A child is suspected of having Cystic Fibrosis (CF). Which diagnostic test is the gold standard to confirm this disease?

options.B: Sweat chloride test

hint: CF involves a defect in the transport of salt and water across cell membranes.

rationales.correct: The sweat chloride test is the definitive diagnostic gold standard for CF. Patients with CF excrete significantly higher levels of chloride (>60 mEq/L) in their sweat due to defective cellular transport proteins.
rationales.incorrect: A chest X-ray shows lung damage but isn't diagnostic. Stool fat analysis indicates pancreatic insufficiency. Sputum cultures identify secondary infections (like Pseudomonas).

scenario: An infant with Tetralogy of Fallot becomes intensely cyanotic, tachypneic, and agitated (a 'Tet spell') while crying. What is the nurse's immediate first action?

options.B: Place the infant in the knee-chest position

hint: Increase systemic resistance to push blood back to the lungs.

rationales.correct: Placing the infant in a knee-chest position kinks the femoral arteries, instantly increasing systemic vascular resistance and forcing blood back through the pulmonary artery for oxygenation.
rationales.incorrect: Oxygen and morphine are given AFTER the mechanical positioning. CPR is only for pulseless cardiac arrest.

scenario: During a routine assessment of an infant, the nurse notes bounding pulses in the arms and weak, thready pulses in the legs. Blood pressure is significantly higher in the arms than the legs. Diagnosis?

options.B: Coarctation of the Aorta

hint: A stricture in the descending aorta acts like a kinked hose.

rationales.correct: Coarctation of the aorta is a narrowing of the descending aorta. This causes high pressure and bounding pulses before the stricture (upper extremities) and low pressure/weak pulses after the stricture (lower extremities).
rationales.incorrect: ASD, TGA, and VSD do not typically present with this classic upper-to-lower extremity pressure gradient.
